File.Name: f828f_f828-024.jpg
Notes: 20775194978 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae perched in a new flower head of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. The spiders seem quite variable as to the color of their abdomens. Some are all green, others are all brown. This one is a bit of both. I expect to find these spiders in the fall, and this is the first one I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: f829a_f829-025.jpg
Notes:
20803606310 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), but I can't rule out some kind of Polistes. This one is perched between a floating Coffeeberry leaf and a stem of cattail in our old-bathtub water garden. It taking water from the tub, just like the deer do almost every might. If you have an old bathtub, you can do some good by making it available to the critters - especially in California during this four year drought. We have to refill our tub every morning. (We figure that the deer have already eaten everything in the garden that appeals to them!) (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2015)

The NWS predicted that today would be cooler than yesterday - it was, but not by much! It was still in the mid-90s on our upstairs porch at home. Hopefully it will really cool down tomorrow or the next day. I have to do my twice-a-year service for the non-profit Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, but I'm waiting for cooler days to do it. That's when I'll get back to my Oregon photos.

File.Name: f829b_f828-019.jpg
Notes: 20981668462 This is a beautiful Vespid Wasp (Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I believe this one is a Paper Wasp (genus Polistes) - maybe Polistes dorsalis californicus, but there are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" like this - all handsome. I usually only see these wasps near the end of summer. I wonder what they do for the rest of the year? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)
CommonNames: Vespid Wasp; Scale Broom; Paper Wasp
ScientificName: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Polistes; Polistes dorsalis californicus
associatedTaxa: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Polistes; Polistes dorsalis californicus

File.Name: f829c_f829-012.jpg
Notes: 20370029673 This is a little Wasp (Hymenoptera) on a flower head in the mint patch of the garden, with a Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly in the background. I believe the wasp is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) which have a distinctive "double abdomen". I believe this is the wasp that makes neat little mud nests, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2015)
CommonNames: Wasp; Cabbage White; Potter Wasp; Mason Wasp

File.Name: f829d_f829-014.jpg
Notes: 20802877580 This is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working a new flower head in the mint patch of the garden. This is the butterfly in the background of my [Next] photo. It's not an exciting butterfly, but it does have its charms and it's not so easy to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2015)

File.Name: f829e_f829-001.jpg
Notes: 20998055701 This is a little nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I found it in the the mint patch of the garden on a plant that happened to be flowering. You can see its spike-like proboscis. It stuck to the shadows, waiting for prey. I found a few more of them - all nymphs - on the blackberry leaves along our driveway, but I like this photo best. I figure late summer and autumn is the time for the predators to pick off the summer pollinator insects. And so it goes... (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2015)

File.Name: f829f_f829-017.jpg
Notes: 20990273365 This is an interesting little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a flower head in the mint patch of the garden. I believe this little fly with the Popeye-like enlarged femurs is genus Syritta or Tropidia in the subfamily Eristalinae. See this photo for a another shot. I like to get the eyes in focus, and this photo fails at that. But at least it gets the diagnostic enlarged femur in focus! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2015)

File.Name: f830a_f830-008.jpg
Notes:
20399258703 Here is another more orthodox photo of a nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - see my [Previous] photo! This one is walking s_l_o_w_l_y across a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway. This is a late-instar nymph, with little training wings. Soon it will be an adult. There seem to be lots of these right now. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2015)

It was cloudy and foggy this morning, though the temperature did get up to the pleasantly warm mid-80s in the afternoon. That's better than 95∞ yesterday and 106∞ degrees the day before! The downside in this change in the weather has been the wind - both last night and all day today. We're under NWS wind advisories, but hopefully tonight will be the end of it.

I did start work on the newsletter for the non-profit Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I'll be more preoccupied than usual for a couple of days.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: f830b_f830-003.jpg
Notes: 21027394951 This is a belly-shot of a little nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. The nymph was walking upside down hanging from a spider web in a plant of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). I got this photo shooting almost straight down. There wasn't much light and it was breezy, but I did the best I could. It's a unique view of a interesting insect! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2015)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: f830c_f806-008.jpg
Notes:
20396707974 You can't go to Oregon without finding at least one banana slug!

This is a large spotted green Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods on a fallen trunk of dead conifer wood. My grandkids actually found it, tucked away in a hollow of rotten wood, with a partner who had no spots. I didn't have my camera for once, but I went back the next day and found just this one. You can see its eye-stalks and feelers on the right and a bit of its slime trail on the left. These were the only two slugs of any kind we found this year. I guess these large slugs are having a tough time in this drought year, but our banana slugs at home are having an even harder time! I don't know how they manage to survive. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2015)

File.Name: f830d_f816-001.jpg
Notes: 21019072145 This is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails in the lowlands back home. It's definitely a native. This one is quite small, the shell is less than 1/2 inch across. I found it on the outside of a window in our kitchen one morning, and moved it to a mossy place that had some sun. It's surprisingly fast! At least the head is in reasonable focus. I hadn't made my coffee yet! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2015)

File.Name: f830e_f810-012.jpg
Notes:
20831414509 "The Underwater Home of the North Umpqua Fish"

Here are my twin grandkids watching a large fish swim by in the observation room of the Winchester Dam fish ladder on the North Umpqua River in Oregon. This is a real working fish ladder, not an aquarium, so you never know what will come by. We were lucky this visit!

I don't really know my fish, but I like to think this is a summer-run Steelhead Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss). These anadromous fish are born in fresh water, spend most of their life in the sea, and return to the river to spawn. In this drought year - in Oregon as well as California - the river is low and warm, which is a difficult combination for these native fish. Steelhead spawn in the tributaries of the river. See this photo. The North Umpqua Foundation has more info. (Winchester Dam, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2015)

(Julie and I had charge of the grandkids for a week while their mom and dad went to the Shakespeare Festival in Ashland. We were looking for places to visit, and this was one of them. It's a keeper!)
Locality: Julie and I had charge of the grandkids for a week while their mom and dad went to the Shakespeare Festival in Ashland.  We were looking for places to visit

File.Name: f830f_f810-016.jpg
Notes: 20829621508 This is the fish ladder at Winchester Dam on the North Umpqua River in Oregon, just north of Roseburg. It's a low dam, and the fish ladder provides a way for anadromous fish like salmon and steelhead trout to get past it. Fish enter at the bottom-right (in my photo) and wind their way up into the river above. There's a little observation room with glass windows near the top. See my [Next] photo! (Winchester Dam, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2015)

File.Name: f831a_f831-001.jpg
Notes:
21023267176 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I found this adult male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on the inside of the window screen in the bathroom this morning. So that's what the racket was last night! I captured it and put it in the refrigerator to "chill out" for a while. Even so, it got away from me in less than a minute when I tried to pose it on our porch railing!

This cricket is rather small and has beautiful cyan-turquoise colors. Maybe it's Riley's Tree Cricket (Oecanthus rileyi). This is a one of our "thermometer crickets" that works the night shift and sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights. You can hear a sample of its song here. Sometimes they create beautiful jams, but mostly they stay in synch with each other. Now how do they do that? (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2015)

I've been pretty distracted today, but not with the newsletter I should be working on! Julie and I went down to the Humane Society this morning and adopted a new dog. Brody is a 1 year old terrier mix. He's been a handful so far, and we're still trying to work out an arrangement with our cats. If 1 human year comes to 7 dog years, then he's about 7 years old - just like my grandkids, yipes!

File.Name: f831b_f831-018.jpg
Notes:
21056698791 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly leaving a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. These pretty ladies are distinctive for having just two large eyespots below on the hindwings, and they almost always have a white spot in the orange rectangle near the margin in front of the forewing. This is the first one I've seen since we got back from Oregon a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2015)

File.Name: f831c_f806-027.jpg
Notes: 21022339766 Here are two shots of what I believe is a flower spike of the Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - aka Habenaria unalascensis ssp. elata. It's an orchid, but it really looks more like an asparagus stalk when it flowers. The plants have large basal ovate leaves that wither away completely before the flowers appear. It's curious that we have this same orchid species at home - known as the Chaparral Orchid, see this photo. You can see its long nectar spurs and (if you look close) the twisted ovaries that turn the flowers upside down, like all orchid flowers. This was the only plant I found this year. When I went back to this place two weeks later, it was gone. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2015)
ScientificName: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Habenaria unalascensis; elata
associatedTaxa: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Habenaria unalascensis; elata

File.Name: f831d_f807-006.jpg
Notes: 21055953971 Here are a close pair of flower stalks of Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia, Orchidaceae) in the Oregon woods. There were quite a few of these flowers when we first got to our cabin in early August. By the time we left a few weeks later, they were all done. I didn't use flash or do any tricks to get that black background. I just found the flowers in a beam of sunlight in the shade-dark woods. It's times like that you realize just how fast the Earth turns and the Sun moves. Shoot fast in the woods when the light is right! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2015)

File.Name: f831e_f812-040.jpg
Notes: 20859590330 Here is a trio of native orchids in the Orchidaceae plant family, all from the Oregon woods. On the left is the White Bog Orchid (Platanthera dilatata var. leucostachys), the only one I found that still had flowers. In the center is (I think) Western Coralroot (Corallorhiza mertensiana), but the flowers were all done. Both of these are from the trail to Twin Lakes at about 5,000 feet elevation. On the right is Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia) by our cabin near Susan Creek at about 1,000 feet elevation. See my [Next] photo. The flowers are more interesting than beautiful, but they are always nice to find. (Twin Lakes and Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
CommonNames: White Bog Orchid; Western Coralroot; Rattlesnake Orchid
ScientificName: Orchidaceae; Platanthera dilatata; leucostachys; Corallorhiza mertensiana; Goodyera oblongifolia
associatedTaxa: Orchidaceae; Platanthera dilatata; leucostachys; Corallorhiza mertensiana; Goodyera oblongifolia

File.Name: f831f_f804-007.jpg
Notes: 21037412302 There's a whole flora under the waterfall at Fall Creek along the North Umpqua River in Oregon. I can see Five-Finger Ferns and Saxifrages like Boykinia and no doubt more. It's an interesting ecosystem - and a summer treat after being so dry back home in California! (Fall Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2015)

File.Name: f901a_f901-003.jpg
Notes:
21075132765 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a late-instar nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide in a a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway. This one looks quite different from the cricket I showed yesterday in this photo, colored brown instead of green, though I believe they are both in the same genus. This one is still a nymph, but it has "training wings" and it won't be long before it matures. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2015)

Our new puppy is doing better with the cats, though it's more of a stand-off than a truce. My distraction today was a shout down from East Camino Cielo road this morning. An old friend and another botanist were looking for the uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata, Berberidaceae plant family based on data from a 1950's collection. See this photo. I went up the hill to help them find it. As I understand it, they are collecting samples to gene-sequence in order to determine clonal relationships both on the coast and the islands. I'm just a naturalist not a botanist, but it was my pleasure to help!
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Wild Blackberry; Shiny-Leaved Barberry
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Berberis pinnata; Berberidaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Berberis pinnata; Berberidaceae

File.Name: f901b_f817-007.jpg
Notes: 20453894253 These are large Pacific Yew (Taxus brevifolia) trees in the Taxaceae plant family, with branches loaded with a thick growth of Mosses (Bryophyta). The three larger trunks left of center may be Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae), I'm not sure now. Deciduous trees like maples and ash attract mosses because they let the light in during winter. I'm not sure why the evergreen yew trees also attract mosses, but they sure are photogenic. I got this photo in a place that's now easily accessible across the Tioga footbridge over the North Umpqua River. This is now considered the Tioga Segment of the North Umpqua Trail, an 80 mile trail along the other side of the river. (Tioga Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2015)
ScientificName: Taxus brevifolia; Taxaceae; Bryophyta; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae
associatedTaxa: Taxus brevifolia; Taxaceae; Bryophyta; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

File.Name: f901c_f817-014.jpg
Notes: 20887743969 This is the start of the Tioga Segment of the North Umpqua Trail through an old-growth stand of mostly Western Red Cedar (Thuja plicata, Cupressaceae). It's a beautiful forest that's now easily accessible across the Tioga footbridge over the North Umpqua River. This is now considered the Tioga Segment of the North Umpqua Trail, an 80 mile trail along the other side of the river. (Tioga Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2015)

File.Name: f901d_f817-008.jpg
Notes: 20451556434 Here are leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae) in front of a large trunk of Western Red Cedar (Thuja plicata, Cupressaceae). The cedar trees are one of my favorites, and there are large old-growth trees at this place across the Tioga footbridge over the North Umpqua River. This is now considered the Tioga Segment of the North Umpqua Trail. (Tioga Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2015)
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Thuja plicata; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Thuja plicata; Cupressaceae

File.Name: f901e_f817-030.jpg
Notes: 21073699505 Here are lichen-covered conifers with Big Twin Lake in the background, at about 5,000 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. I believe the trees are Shasta Red Fir (Abies magnifica shastensis), though you can't tell from this photo. That's the problem identifying all the different conifers in the Oregon woods - what you need to see to confirm an ID is 100 feet off the ground! One of these years, I want to really work at photographing Oregon trees to show their diagnostic features. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)

File.Name: f901f_f815-030.jpg
Notes: 21080787351 I believe these are the cones of Western White Pine (Pinus monticola, Pinaceae). I gathered these under a single large tree, and the cones seem to expand as they age on the ground. The largest gray cone is about a foot long. I found these under a large tree at about 1500 feet elevation in a small grove, but they are mostly scattered in the forest. The wood is highly regarded. It's a five-needled pine. The cones are similar to Sugar Pine (Pinus lambertiana - especially when expanded - but not quite as large. I think we also have a few near our cabin, and I've confused them before based on the cones. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes

File.Name: f902a_f902-004.jpg
Notes:
20480129013 This is another handsome Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) digging into a new flower in the mint patch of the garden. This one is fairly large, almost It's almost honeybee-size. These furry flies are quite attractive. I've been staying close to the garden because of our new puppy dog, but I've still been finding new insects. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2015)

It was cool today, and we even had some drizzle in the morning that was almost rain. The sun finally brole through after noon, but it was still partially cloudy. This was the first day in months that I wore sweat pants all day! A week ago it was in the 100s! That's the way it is as we approach autumn, meteorological autumn at least - it rocks... back and forth!

File.Name: f902b_f902-011.jpg
Notes: 20913155828 This is a handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) digging into a new flower in the mint patch of the garden. I've been staying close to the garden because of our new puppy dog, but I've still been finding new insects. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2015)

File.Name: f902c_f828-022.jpg
Notes: 20479676513 Here is a mating pair of Long-horned Beetles in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera on new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. They're in mating position, but I don't see any actual connection. These beetles are quite large, almost an inch long with antennae that go on for another inch. See this photo for another shot of the same beetle. There are many different kinds of these beetles, so I'll leave it at that. Happy Hump Day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: f902d_f815-036.jpg
Notes: 20478869303 This is a handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) in the Oregon woods at about 1,500 feet elevation. It has short wings, but I reckon that's just how it is. If I was home in the Santa Barbara mountains, I would guess the short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the subfamily of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). But that one has blue tibia and this one is red. (See Alice Abelia's fine photo here.) I'm not sure what this is, but it's probably the same subfamily. I think this one is perched on a frond of native Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum, Polypodiaceae). (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
CommonNames: Short-horned Grasshopper; Spur-throated Grasshoppers; Western Bracken Fern
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Pteridium aquilinum; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Pteridium aquilinum; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: f902e_f812-051.jpg
Notes: 20911240090 This is a fairly large Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) in the Oregon woods at about 5,000 feet elevation. It's either a nymph or wingless, but it does have generous genitalia. I *should* know what this grasshopper is. I've see it in years past, but I can't seem to find it in my notes or at BugGuide. Maybe it's a Spur-throated Grasshopper (subfamily Melanoplinae)? I found this one perched on a leaf of (I guess) Western Cone-Flower (Rudbeckia occidentalis, Asteraceae), a daisy without ray flowers. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)

File.Name: f902f_f812-006.jpg
Notes: 20477832413 This is an interesting Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) in the Oregon woods at about 5,000 feet elevation. I guess from its general appearance that it is a Band-winged Grasshopper (subfamily Oedipodinae), maybe genus Trimerotropis. It resembles photos at BugGuide of the Crackling Forest Grasshopper (Trimerotropis verruculata suffusa). (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)

File.Name: f903a_f903-012.jpg
Notes:
20505836223 This is a small Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working brand new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. I'm pretty sure the butterfly is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). These are the first new flowers I've seen this year, and the flowers are interesting. If you look close at the bottom flower head by the butterfly's rear leg, you can see that the individual flowers are "perfect" - with both stamens and pistil. These shrubs are normally "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, but these flowers are both. I'm not sure what this means. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2015)

I took our new dog Brodie on a walk in the canyon today - our first walk together. It went well, but he kept slinking back to the point that I finally had to put him on a leash. That was pretty distracting from getting new photos. *Sigh* Our new dog is also distracting me from finishing the newsletter I should be working on. It's a holiday weekend coming up, so I figure I'll get it done by next Tuesday, no problem.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides; dioecious

File.Name: f903b_f903-025.jpg
Notes: 21133892971 I'm sure this is a different Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) than I've ever noticed before. All of our usual suspects are the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica), see this photo. I'm pretty sure this one is another Jagged Ambush Bug (genus Phymata), but maybe Phymata americana? I'm guessing, but this one is larger and does look quite different from the usual. I found it on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is getting ready to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2015)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Jagged Ambush Bug; Coyote Bush

File.Name: f903c_f903-001.jpg
Notes: 21115639622 This is another nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. I'm not sure if this is the same or different from the nymph in my [Previous] photo. I've been finding lots of these predators lately. Julie says it looks like a delicate ocean shrimp. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2015)

File.Name: f903d_f902-007.jpg
Notes: 20937420870 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera in a threatening defensive pose on a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway. Sure it's not the best photo, taken on a gloomy morning in low light - but it captures the moment. It looks downright malevolent! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2015)

File.Name: f903e_f902-003.jpg
Notes: 20938369489 This is a remarkable butt shot of a handsome Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on new flowers in the mint patch of the garden. It's the same fly I show in this photo, two photos back. It's a large fly, almost honeybee-size. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2015)

File.Name: f903f_f830-011.jpg
Notes: 20936770770 This is a common Blow Fly (Calliphoridae, Diptera) - a trash fly - perched on a cactus pad of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. Is it a green or blue blue bottle fly? With those iridescent colors, it all depends on how you look at it! This is also a good butt shot. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2015)

File.Name: f904a_f904-023.jpg
Notes:
20528927734 I found this tiny - but perfect - Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) in the woods today. It's 1220 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.8 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I didn't know they came this small. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)

It's been hard to concentrate on my comments tonight because TCM is showing W.C. Fields movies tonight! I can listen at my computer, but I have to get up to see. W.C. Fields is such a master of physical comedy, that I've been getting up a lot!

File.Name: f904b_f904-019.jpg
Notes: 21159011091 This is an interesting little Fly (order Diptera) tucked into a leaf cluster of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). There was another leaf blocking the view, but it's a nice shot of the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes. Looking at the antennae, this might be a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae). I tried to move the leaf, and the fly took off - of course. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)
ScientificName: Diptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; ommatidia; Bombyliidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; ommatidia; Bombyliidae

File.Name: f904c_f904-036.jpg
Notes: 20963109148 I was trying to get a photo of this late-summer flower of (I think) native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family, when a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flew into my photo. Maybe it's a Mining Bee (genus Andrena, family Andrenidae) because it does have pollen collecting hairs on its rear legs. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)
CommonNames: Small Wirelettuce; Wild Bee; Mining Bee

File.Name: f904d_f904-001.jpg
Notes:
20529318893 This is a neat "butt shot" of a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a cactus pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I'm pretty sure it's genus Phidippus, maybe a female Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). To the right of the spider are tiny "glochids" - those irritating small tufted cactus spines that are impossible to remove. I'm still feeling a few after taking this photo this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)

D'oh - Thursday is the day for Beautiful Bug Butts, but I always seem to get good photos on Friday. HBBBT anyway!
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Prickly Pear; Johnson Jumper

File.Name: f904e_f904-007.jpg
Notes:
20528871193 I believe these are galls of the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa) in the Cynipidae family of the Hymenoptera. They are growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae. Unlike most galls, they seem to shrivel up the leaves that feed them. See this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)

This has not been a good year for oak galls, no doubt because it hasn't been a good year for the wasps that make them. Four years of drought has made a difference.

File.Name: f904f_f904-012.jpg
Notes: 20526972604 This is a ripening fruit of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, with next years flower buds below it. The trees are related to avocados, and it shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". The fruit taste very pungent unless they are dead ripe, but the nuts inside are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right. See this YouTube video. The trees are not producing many fruit in this drought year. We haven't prepared the nuts for some time, but every few years we get a bumper crop. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)

File.Name: f905a_f904-020.jpg
Notes:
20989653849 Here is one more photo of the tiny - but perfect - Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) in the woods I found yesterday. See this photo and read Stoil Ivanov's comment. He suggests that it is genus Holcocephala, which sure looks right because of those widely spaced eyes. Looking at the info and photos at BugGuide, that seems like a good match - especially for species Holcocephala fusca. But there are no records of these flies west of Oklahoma. On the other hand, it is a tiny fly, easy to overlook. I'm still unsure. Names aside, this is a nice shot of the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2015)

Just these four photos today - and even that's more than I'd planned,except that I already had three of these photos ready to go in my "Ready Folder". I spent the first day of Labor Day weekend laboring - finally getting back to that newsletter I was supposed to be working on last week. Our new puppy dog Brodie made it hard to concentrate, and I realized I wouldn't get my work to the printer before the weekend, so I put it off. No problem. The newsletter is basically done except to check alignments and add a few photo captions. I'll send it out for review tomorrow, and maybe even get out for some new photos!

File.Name: f905b_f901-006.jpg
Notes: 21149881826 This is a cute little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera walking across a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway. You can see its proboscis folded under it's head. They look like skinny Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae), but I believe they are plant feeders. This one has its wings ruffed up to take off. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2015)
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Wild Blackberry; Assassin Bugs
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Reduviidae

File.Name: f905c_f831-016.jpg
Notes: 21183817101 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly leaving a Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. See this photo for another shot that shows its upper wings - though I really think the undersides are more beautiful! It's the two large eyespots below on the hindwings (rather than four) that distinguish this pretty lady. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2015)

File.Name: f905d_f828-002.jpg
Notes: 21175794165 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp path in the garden after watering. It looks fresh - you can still see some blue on its forewings, which wears away before too long. I never find these butterflies visiting flowers, but they seem to fly all summer long. By book says they only have two broods - but is that really true? (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2015)

File.Name: f906a_f906-003.jpg
Notes:
21016661479 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly perched for a moment on the leaf of a succulent plant in the garden. The real beauty of these butterflies is their upper wings, which they never show when perched. I'm still trying to get the perfect shot, but see this photo.

I finished my newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society today, and sent it out to the board for review. I'll deliver it to the printers on Tuesday, after the holiday. I did get out for new photos today, but late, and there was some breeze. Just four photos again today, but I'll do better tomorrow!
Locality: Colias eurydice) butterfly perched for a moment on the leaf of a succulent plant in the garden. The real beauty of these butterflies is their upper wings

File.Name: f906b_f906-010.jpg
Notes: 21016291329 This is a mass of Parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) pupal cases on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I believe this is the work of Braconid Wasps in the family Braconidae. Maybe there's the husk of an unfortunate caterpillar under there? I found this mass on a hanging dead leaf and moved it to a still-green part of the same plant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2015)

File.Name: f906c_f906-015.jpg
Notes: 20580141814 I believe this is a Gall-like Scale Insect (Kermesidae, Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that has come to a bad end. Compare this photo of the same insect, taken last spring when all was well. It looks like some kind of parasite has busted out. I noticed this on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2015)

File.Name: f906d_f906-005.jpg
Notes:
21014439200 Here are two shots showing male staminate flowers (left) and female pistillate flowers (right) on different shrubs of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. These "dioecious" plants look quite different in flower, but they are the same. The male plants have a yellowish cast from the pollen and the female plants have a whitish cast from the stigmas. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2015)

I'm confused though. The male plants on the left sure seem to have little capitate stigmas rising from between the anthers. But if they are bisexual flowers, why do the stigmas look so different from the feathery stigmas on the right? Questions...

File.Name: f907a_f907-017.jpg
Notes:
20611212213 This is looking close at a cute little Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) clutching a spent flower of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). It's puppy dog cute! I'm pretty sure this is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the subfamily of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). I'll post another photo tomorrow which shows the short wings, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

Our weather has swung back to the warm side - 90∞ on the upstairs deck, and no doubt warmer below. Temperatures are expected to rise into triple-digits as the week moves on. That's how it is in our autumn - it swings between warm and cool as different ridges and troughs influence our weather. Despite the heat, it was a pleasure to get out for photos today! I'll take my newsletter to the printers tomorrow and be done with it!
CommonNames: Short-horned Grasshopper; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Spur-throated Grasshoppers
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae

File.Name: f907b_f907-019.jpg
Notes: 21044023890 This is a tattered Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly working a new late-summer flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family, see this photo. Butterflies are getting scarce in this drought year, so I figure every one I find is worth documenting. I've been seeing very few of the small butterflies - the blues and hairstreaks. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: f907c_f907-004.jpg
Notes: 21043641730 This is looking straight-down on another late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I don't think I've ever seen one with such straight antennae. I've been finding a lot of these lately. It might well be a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

File.Name: f907d_f907-025.jpg
Notes:
21239393191 These are tough new late-summer flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's downright amazing that any plant can produce flowers at the end of our dry summer in a drought year, but here it is. You can see a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on the lower-left flower. These late-summer flowers are important. They keep the insects alive during the hardest part of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

If you look closer than I did when I got this photo, you can see the abdomen of (I think) a tiny Assassin Bug disappearing over the edge of the lower-right flower!
CommonNames: California Aster; Wild Bee; Assassin Bug

File.Name: f907e_f907-039.jpg
Notes:
20608411384 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say)

Here is the first new flower I've seen this year of next year's Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are the first new flowers of the next rain year. Yep, they are quite glandular. I usually find the first flowers close to the Autumnal Equinox (Sep. 21 or so), see this photo. They seem to be even earlier in this drought year. Is it a sign? For what?? (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

These are our first native shrubs to flower, but they don't all flower at the same time like some other natives. New plants will typically keep coming into flower from autumn until spring, September until March. They might be our longest flowering native.

File.Name: f907f_f907-041.jpg
Notes:
21204463906 Well, I'll be... Here are the first new buds I've seen this year of next year's Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. If you look close, you can even see a few new flowers - see my [Next] photo! I usually find the first flowers close to the Autumnal Equinox (Sep. 21 or so), see this photo. They seem to be even earlier in this drought year. Is it a sign? For what?? (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

These are our first native shrubs to flower, but they don't all flower at the same time like some other natives. New plants will typically keep coming into flower from autumn until spring, September until March. They might be our longest flowering native.

File.Name: f908a_f908-002.jpg
Notes:
21248456962 Another day, another Assassin Bug!

They seem very common in the woods right now. This is another late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning, maybe the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). This one is perched on a flower head in the mint patch. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2015)

My work on the newsletter is all done, and I delivered it to the printer in Solvang today. Of course I got a few photos along the way, but only a few. It was hot and breezy, 102∞ in Solvang where I was. That's too hot. I'm ready for another swing to the cool side.

File.Name: f908b_f908-007.jpg
Notes:
21070532470 This is a little Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera in the mint patch of our garden. This one is flexing its wings while grooming. I don't think I've ever seen the wings before, but they are quite pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

These bugs are native to northern Africa, but they are a new introduction, maybe as recent as 2008. They are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc as a major agricultural pest. They've been here for a while. If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's any chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry and even our garden.

File.Name: f908c_f908-016.jpg
Notes:
21258278625 Here are Milkweed Aphids on new seedpods of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. I guess the aphids are Aphis nerii in the order Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The aphids do some damage, but they are pretty and attract interesting predators. This milkweed is a weedy native that I usually find growing on bare soil along the highway. I overexposed this photo almost two stops to bring out some detail in the shade. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 September 2015)

If you ever want to lower your esteem for your fellow humans, just hunker down next to a plant along an all-too-busy highway and try to get a photo. It's really annoying. ;=)

associatedTaxa: Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Aphis nerii; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

I'm pretty sure this is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the subfamily of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

File.Name: f908e_f908-022.jpg
Notes: 20634839784 Here are two photos showing flowers of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for the whole plant. It's growing along Highway 246 in a place that was mowed last spring, so all the growth must have been since then. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get down on your hands and knees to really see the flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring! The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid. The flowers are related to our shrubby Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), see this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 September 2015)

File.Name: f908f_f908-019.jpg
Notes: 21230900606 This is a whole plant of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo for a closer look at the flowers. This one is growing along Highway 246 in a place that was mowed last spring, so all the growth must have been since then. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get down on your hands and knees to really see the flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring! The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid. The flowers are related to our shrubby Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), see this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 8 September 2015)

File.Name: f909a_f909-036.jpg
Notes:
21097222108 This is a pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. It's perched on late summer flowers native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. If you look close, you can see some sparkles in its wing spots. These butterflies have been scarce lately, so I'm happy to find this one. I haven't seen one since late June. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

Today was another too-hot day - up to 97∞ on the upstairs deck and 103∞ at Paradise along the river. We had some humidity from Hurricane (now Tropical Storm) Linda down in Baja, which makes it feel worse. I'm ready for another swing to the cool side, maybe next week.
Locality: now Tropical Storm) Linda down in Baja

File.Name: f909b_f909-032.jpg
Notes: 20663668023 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's perched on late summer flowers native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. I'm glad their still out there - both the flowers and the butterfly! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

File.Name: f909c_f909-007.jpg
Notes: 21284460185 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae perched on a stem of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see one of the lavender flowers in the background. If you look at the bottom of the stem, you can also see part of an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) that has become the spider's prey. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

File.Name: f909d_f909-013.jpg
Notes: 20661563034 This is looking close at a late-summer flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. Look at that little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey inside the flower! This photo also shows the wonderful large pollen grains that seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn. They that get everywhere, including the spider. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the flowers - different flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: f909e_f909-012.jpg
Notes: 21292033131 Here are pretty new late-summer flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. It does the soul some good to find these flowers at the very end of our season. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year. See my [Next] photo. The plants have been flowering for a while, but this is their main time - right now. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

File.Name: f909f_f909-043.jpg
Notes:
21095722288 This is a native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family, with the Santa Barbara (and Ventura) backcountry in the background. It's nearly done flowering, but I reckon it still has a few live ones. I got this photo near the top of La Cumbre Peak, nearly 4,000 feet high. This shrub only grows in the higher mountains. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

For years I've been getting photos of one convenient shrub outside the fence of an "antenna farm" near here, see this photo. That was before we went to Oregon. Now I'm shocked to discover that the people in charge have cut it down. "You bastards!" - as Kyle always says on South Park. *Sigh*

File.Name: f910a_f910-021.jpg
Notes:
21298859922 This is a stunning Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing all of its eyespots. I found this one on a flowering shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. They open and close their wings while feeding, and I captured this one just right. I believe those blue stripes on the leading edge of its forewings marks it as newly emerged - but already scarred. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2015)

It was another hot day, so I wasn't inspired to go anywhere. It was a few degrees cooler today, only in the 90s on the deck and 99∞ by the river at Paradise. It was still 102∞ in Solvang today, and the local paper reported 107∞ yesterday. That's too damn hot! We're under an Excessive Heat Warning from the NWS. It is expected to slowly cool down over the next few days. I'm ready for it.

File.Name: f910b_f910-006.jpg
Notes: 20686494584 This is a pretty little Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect on a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). They have amazing eyes! I've been seeing a few of them on the window screens at night. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2015)

File.Name: f910c_f910-001.jpg
Notes: 21317144131 This is another cute little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). You can clearly see its proboscis, but it's for sucking leaves not prey. The rain drops are from our fire hose. We had to shut off the water today to do some plumbing work. There was still water in the line, so Julie watered the woods - including this odd insect. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2015)

File.Name: f910d_f910-010.jpg
Notes: 21120745270 This is a little Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide in the mint patch of the garden. I wouldn't have noticed it except for the antennae that go all the way from here to there! These crickets sleep all day and work the night shift. They are the songsters of our woods in the fall. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2015)

File.Name: f910e_f907-006.jpg
Notes: 21121570799 This is another "beautiful bug butt shot" of a nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched in the mint patch of the garden. Who would have ever guessed that it has a happy little piggy face on its butt! See my [Previous] photo, and this photo showing more of the same insect. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

File.Name: f910f_f907-009.jpg
Notes: 21120156410 This is a "beautiful bug butt shot" of the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched in the mint patch of the garden. Sure it's trying to get away from me, but not before I got a decent photo from the side - see this photo of the same insect. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

File.Name: f911a_f911-011.jpg
Notes:
21148458440 This is another large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg case on a dead grass stem. I realize it's not the best photo. It has hot and breezy today, which made it hard to concentrate, but I like the circular aspect of this photo. This was my only critter photo today, and I always try to post today's photos first. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2015)

I had errands in town this morning, and it was hot and breezy by the time I got home. So it goes. It was still hot again today - 104∞ by the river at Paradise a few miles away, which is just as hot as its been all week. But darned if the NWS isn't giving us a chance of real rain on Monday to Wednesday as the remnants of Hurricane Linda come in. I'm not holding my breath, but it would be a treat!

File.Name: f911b_f911-015.jpg
Notes:
21325478002 Here are handsome tri-colored berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, but they usually wait until the time comes. I've been seeing seen Bushtits, Wrentits, and newly arriving Warblers take the berries at home. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2015)

I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries. This plant is related to native Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though!
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana

File.Name: f911c_f911-008.jpg
Notes: 21344255171 These are still-new flowers of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. (No relation to gillyweed in Harry Potter 4!) It's a summer plant that's been flowering since early April, see this photo. I love these late-summer flowers that choose this time to flower, even in a drought year. They keep the insects alive! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2015)

File.Name: f911d_f911-007.jpg
Notes:
21309150846 This is a tiny but pretty flower of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The flower stalks are really the inferior ovaries that will finally split open to release seeds. It's a tough one to photograph. The tall stems sway in the slightest breeze. I had to wait for that moment when the wind dies to get this photo. It's related to common wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry summer soil in our mountains to flower. You have to admire these late-season natives that flower at the end of summer, even in this drought year. They keep the insects going! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2015)

This plant is most remarkable for when it flowers. I expect our late-summer flowers to be composites, but these are different. We seem to have two different populations in our mountains. These small ones like this that are only a foot or two tall, and some larger plants that are 5 or 6 feet tall. I'll try to get a photo of the larger plants tomorrow.

File.Name: f911e_f910-016.jpg
Notes: 21335247815 This is a handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. What pretty patterned wings it has. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2015)

File.Name: f911f_f907-001.jpg
Notes: 21343224561 This is a small Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working flowers in the mint patch of the garden. It's probably the same kind of fly as this photo - but with these little flies, who knows? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2015)

File.Name: f912a_f912-017.jpg
Notes:
20747037314 I believe this is a cute little Nut and Acorn Weevil (genus Curculio, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) beetle on the acorn cup of an interesting Oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains. See my [Previous] photo for some discussion of the oak. (If you look close at the acorn on the right of that photo, you can see this weevil.) What a long proboscis it has! It appears to be sucking the acorn cup, but as a beetle it must be chewing with tiny nibbles. The hairs of this weevil look soft, like feathers on a fledgling birds. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

It was mostly cloudy and cooler today, but it got up to 96∞ down by the river a few miles from home. The forecast still calls for a bit of rain on Monday night or Tuesday, but right now it's only a 40% chance - which means there's a 60% chance that it won't rain. We'll see what develops. Our luck on these odd rains has been poor this year. We need it.

File.Name: f912b_f912-018.jpg
Notes:
21180886750 This is an interesting Oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains. If you look close, you can see a small Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on the acorn cup to the right, see my [Next] photo. If you look at that leaf standing up in the center showing its backside, you can see that the leaves are flat and shiny underneath, very different from Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

This oak mostly grows as a "scrub oak", but occasionally it becomes a copse of small trees. It's been called many names - Quercus pacifica or Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus parvula or Quercus parvula var. shrevei or Quercus shrevei. See the discussion with this photo. I think I prefer Quercus pacifica as a name, though that one usually grows on the Channel Islands as the Island Scrub Oak. Why not?

This oak looks like a Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath; without any sign of "hairy armpits"; the catkins are longer, more airy, and pinker; and the plants appear to be semi-deciduous - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once.
CommonNames: Oak; Weevil; Coast Live Oak; Island Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Q. agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Q. agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: f912c_f912-025.jpg
Notes: 21377114471 This is another pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a new flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant is kind of weedy, but it is a California native. The butterflies have been scarce since June, so I'm happy to find them again. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

File.Name: f912d_f912-002.jpg
Notes:
21376852321 Another day, another Assassin Bug!

This is another late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning, maybe the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). This is another photo from the mint patch of our garden. Look close, it has a glob something orange on its beak. These little nymphs are so pretty, I don't mind posting them again and again! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

File.Name: f912e_f912-019.jpg
Notes:
21376452621 These are tiny but pretty flowers and seed heads of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. This is one of the larger plants that I mentioned yesterday in this photo. This plant is 6 or 7 feet tall, growing in a ditch near Lizard's Mouth on West Camino Cielo. Other plants in the mountains are much smaller, almost like two different taxa. It's a tough one to photograph. The tall stems sway in the slightest breeze. I had to wait for that moment when the wind dies to get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

I love these late-summer flowers that choose this time to flower, even in a drought year. They keep the insects alive! Did you notice the aphids?

File.Name: f912f_f912-009.jpg
Notes: 21376188991 These are leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae plant family starting to show some fall color. If you look close behind the leaves, you can see that this tree is loaded with seeds. It will be fun to come back in autumn and hit the branches with a stick - and watch them fly! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

File.Name: f913a_f913-014.jpg
Notes:
21211933999 This different adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera has captured a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) for prey. I found it on a flowering staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. I reckon late summer and autumn is the time for predators like this one to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. Yep, it's "Nature red of tooth and claw" as Tennyson wrote. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2015)

This may well be the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), but compare the critter in my [Previous] photo. Are they the same or different? Sure, the colors of that one may be off if it is really freshly emerged. They look structurally the same to me, but there are color differences.

It was cooler today, finally! The day started warm, in the upper 80s, but it cooled down in the afternoon into the 70s - as cool as it's been all week, day or night. We still have a chance of rain mostly on Tuesday, but the NWS has been backing off on the forecast. Now there's a 30% chance of 1/10 inch or less. *Sigh* Of course I'll take it!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Bee; Coyote Bush; Leafhopper Assassin Bug

File.Name: f913b_f913-008.jpg
Notes:
21210212710 This is a fully adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. Maybe it's brand new. It's perched on the same mint leaf as the late-instar nymph I showed yesterday in this photo, and I suspect they are one and the same. Can they transform in a day? (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2015)

This may be the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), but compare the critter in my [Next] photo. Are they the same or different? Sure, the colors of this one may be off if it is really freshly emerged.

File.Name: f913c_f913-033.jpg
Notes:
20775262094 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg case in a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Her eyes and chelicera are remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2015)

The front end of this spider looks fine, but her abdomen is uncommonly skinny. I'm not sure what this means, but starvation comes to mind. Despite my photostream, insects have been scarce in this drought year. I'll post a "butt shot" for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday in a few days.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: f913d_f913-028.jpg
Notes: 21209397638 This is the "pinched form" of galls of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae in the Hymenoptera family) growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family. They look like little tricorn hats! I had to bend the leaf back and snag it on a neighbor leaf to bring the galls into the light. All galls have been relatively scarce this year, but their peak season is just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2015)
CommonNames: Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: f913e_f912-030.jpg
Notes:
20775670053 Here is another photo of the first new flowers I've seen this year of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are quite glandular, but these also seem unusually pale. The other flowers I've been showing lately are the last flowers of the old rain year, but these are the first new flowers of the new rain year! I first noticed them on Sep. 7 with this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

Sure, I'm hoping for decent rain this year with a strong El NiÒo in the works. I guess it's Godzilla vs. the Pacific Blob as some pundits have said. I'll wait and see...

File.Name: f913f_f912-013.jpg
Notes: 21209712179 Here is another photo of the cute little Nut and Acorn Weevil (genus Curculio, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) beetle on the acorn cup of an interesting Oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the weevil and the photo before that for some discussion about the oak. What a long proboscis it has! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2015)

File.Name: f914a_f914-005.jpg
Notes:
21427992825 I found this extraordinary little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. The spider itself is tiny, but its arms are large! I believe this is a male, maybe of the Whitebanded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes)? It does seem to have a "distinctive white ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes" which R.J. Adams says is the distinguishing characteristic in his Spiders of California book. This is a pretty extraordinary example of sexual dimorphism. Compare this photo of maybe the female of the species! (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2015)

Compare this and my [Previous] photos - one up and one down! I couldn't decide which I liked best.

It was cool and cloudy today, and we had a bit of real rain! Enough to get the bottom of the rain gauge wet, but hardly enough to measure. They're calling it 1/100 inch on the SB rainfall map, but I think that's optimistic. Today we only had a 20% chance of rain, but tonight that's going up to 60%, and 40% tomorrow! This is a trough from the north that is pulling up residual moisture from once-hurricane Linda in the south. It's a weather pattern that I hope continues this winter!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Prickly Pear; Whitebanded Crab Spider

File.Name: f914c_f914-010.jpg
Notes: 21238437290 I believe this is a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata) spider in the family Araneidae, with distinctive "horns" on its abdomen - though this one doesn't look very silvery. It's hanging from its web, wrapping its prey - an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera)? It's got a safety rope! I found this one right above a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) which are very attractive to many kinds of bees and wasps. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2015)

I believe this one is a Paper Wasp (genus Polistes) - maybe Polistes dorsalis californicus, but there are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" like this - all handsome. I usually only see these wasps near the end of summer, and I've never seen their nests. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2015)

File.Name: f914e_f914-023.jpg
Notes: 21238126658 I believe this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera perched on a flower head of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). They are soft-bodied insects, with abdomens that extend beyond their wings. They seem quite common on these late-season shrubs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2015)

File.Name: f914f_f914-006.jpg
Notes: 21237761778 This is a whole flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. It was pretty gloomy today, but the gloom brings out the color of the flowers. This is a stump-sprout on the burn of the White Fire a couple of years ago. It's also a place along Paradise Road that was bulldozed last year. Oh well, these shrubs typically grow on the river bed, a place that often get disturbed by floods. They are probably my favorite late-summer flowering plant, very attractive for late-season insects. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 September 2015)

File.Name: f915a_f915-017.jpg
Notes:
21265186108 Here are new berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, with raindrops. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, but they usually wait until the time comes. I've been seeing seen Bushtits, Wrentits, and newly arriving Warblers take the berries at home. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

We had real rain last night, nearly 1/2 inch, more than was expected! We've been in a cloud today, but it's been dead calm. It was a real treat walking through the garden and the woods today looking for rain photos. It's been months. I actually went out looking for mushrooms and banana slugs, but not yet. Now we're expected to slowly swing back to the warm side, maybe hot by next weekend. That's how our autumn weather is - it rocks... back and forth!

File.Name: f915b_f915-036.jpg
Notes: 21426378386 This is looking at Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today, from our upstairs deck at home. We've been in a cloud all day after nearly 1/2 inch of real rain last night. It was a gloomy day, but just right to show the new lichens that have already revived on the trunks of these trees. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

File.Name: f915c_f915-028.jpg
Notes:
21264200640 These are open pistillate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. I've tried to photograph these before in the sun, but I didn't like the results. We've been in a cloud all day after our nearly 1/2 inch of rain last night, and that turned out to be perfect light. The flowers in the upper-right are starting to go to seed as the pappus grows longer. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

It's interesting to compare these feathery pistils with what seem to be capitate pistils of the mostly staminate plants. There's a good example in the upper-right of this photo - if you ignore the Assassin Bug. I'm confused by these most common of native shrubs.

File.Name: f915d_f915-012.jpg
Notes:
21451789055 These tiny cactus spines have skewered raindrops from our storm last night! Sure, it's a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. These are little spines coming from a clump of "glochids" of the cactus - those irritating small tufted spines that are impossible to remove. This is a tight crop. It's impossible to get closer without paying the cost! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

We had real rain last night, nearly 1/2 inch! It was a real treat walking through the garden and the woods looking for rain photos. It's been months.

Notes: 21264737629 Here are raindrops on a pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning, looking at it edge-wise. We had real rain last night, nearly 1/2 inch! It was a real treat walking through the garden and the woods looking for rain photos. It's been months. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

File.Name: f915f_f915-014.jpg
Notes: 21440394392 Here are raindrops on spent flowers of a succulent in our garden, maybe genus Kalanchoe, Crassulaceae. We had real rain last night, nearly 1/2 inch! It was a real treat walking through the garden and the woods looking for rain photos. It's been months. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2015)

File.Name: f916a_f916-020.jpg
Notes:
21489192881 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae is having a blessed event! Brand new spiderlings are just starting to emerge from her egg case. Are those white things the "egg shells"? (I often find them on old egg cases.) The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg cases until the hatch is over. I have another photo of the same spider here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

We had more drizzle and light rain last night, bringing our total for the storm to just over 6/10 inch. We started today still in a cloud with some drizzle. I took my morning walk in the lovely cloud with dead calm wind. It was a pleasure! It finally cleared up around 1:30 and some breeze kicked up, spoiling the mood.

File.Name: f916b_f916-023.jpg
Notes: 20859370393 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg case in a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Her egg case looks a bit soggy from the recent rain. I have another photo of the same spider here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

File.Name: f916c_f916-030.jpg
Notes: 21479974375 This is a closer shot of the (I believe) Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in my [Previous] photo. It was hiding in its "spider cave" - the tube-like retreat on the edge of a flat sheet of webbing on the ground. This gloomy morning was just right for photographing this spider. I probably couldn't have got this shot in contrasty sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

File.Name: f916d_f916-028.jpg
Notes: 21291649710 I believe that this is the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, with raindrops. If you look close in the funnel opening, you can see the spider! See my [Next] photo for a closer shot. I found this on the ground in a place where our misguided neighbor recently cut everything to the ground, including this shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, Ericaceae). (%@$#!) It's a good "spider cave" for the spider though. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

File.Name: f916e_f916-025.jpg
Notes:
21452767806 The first real rain of the year always does wonders in the woods. We had 6/10 inch of rain over the last two days, and the woods are already coming alive! I have no idea what kind of moss this is growing on a sandstone rock outcrop. I suspect the flat green liverworts are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). If you look close, you can see new "leaves" of a Cladonia lichen at the center-bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

Unfortunately, the forecast is for us to swing back to the warm side this weekend - up to 97∞ on Sunday?? All this beautiful new life will disappear until our next swing to the cool side. So it goes all autumn.

File.Name: f916f_f916-016.jpg
Notes: 21291856639 These are newly revived Lichens growing on a shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the canyon. Our 1/2 inch+ of rain yesterday did the woods some good! This shrub attracts lichens like no other, and I have no idea why. I have no idea how many different kinds of lichen there are in this photo - one or many? I need to get some books this year and really learn this interesting group. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2015)

File.Name: f917a_f917-045.jpg
Notes:
21491288662 This is another Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). This one is working a late-summer flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. I love these late-season flowers that keep the insects alive during the hardest part of the year in our California mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)

The rain is gone, but it was still quite chilly this morning, only about 50∞ when I got up this morning. We actually turned on the heater for the first time all summer. It did get up to about 80∞ today after the morning chill, and it's expected to get downright hot this weekend - before it cools down again next week. I've been looking for the first banana slugs and mushrooms, but nothing yet. We did have an early hatch of winged termites yesterday afternoon - from the woods, not our house! The birds loved it - especially that sweet little Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) who landed on our porch railing about two feet from my head! I was hoping for more action today, but it didn't happen.
Locality: Wilsonia pusilla) who landed on our porch railing about two feet from my head! I was hoping for more action today

Notes: 20878809324 These are pretty new late-summer flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family, growing in the higher mountains. They're not having a good time in the drought year, and many plants have no flowers or very small flowers. They are favorites of our late-season butterflies. I usually find them along our mountain roads. Now what is it about roads that attracts native flowers? (Weeds too, of course.) (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)
