File.Name: f917d_f917-004.jpg
Notes:
21490197192 Here is a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a cactus pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I'm pretty sure it's genus Phidippus, maybe a female Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). See this photo. The spider is nicely posed next to a clump of spines on the cactus pad. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2015)

This is another tight crop. I had to lean into the cactus patch to get even this shot. The big spines merely scratch, but the tiny "glochid" spines at the base get into your clothes and skin and are really irritating! I'm still trying to pull a few out, hours later.
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Prickly Pear; Johnson Jumper
ScientificName: Salticidae; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni

File.Name: f917e_f917-012.jpg
Notes:
21474460616 This is a "butt shot" of a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae. It's perched in a native shrub of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see some feathery seeds above the spider. HBBBT! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)

This spider sure looks different from the spider in my [Previous] photo - fat butt vs. skinny butt. We actually have two different species in California, P. viridans and P. longipalpis. Maybe they are different species? Or maybe this one just hasn't dropped her eggs yet, which must make a difference in girth!

Like many large spiders who sit exposed on their webs, this one seems to have a face on its butt. Now why would a spider evolve a face on its body that is recognizable by us humans?
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; P. viridans; P. longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; P. viridans; P. longipalpis

File.Name: f917f_f913-031.jpg
Notes: 21474273776 This is a "happy butt" shot of the same large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg case in a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family that I show two photos back. You can see her spinnerets. This is a tight crop - maybe too tight, but what the heck. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2015)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: f918a_f918-013.jpg
Notes:
21500697026 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - probably the species Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It has amazing patterned eyes. This one is doing *something* on a moist garden path. They have a distinctive high-pitched buzz when they fly, so I usually hear them before I see them. They flick their wings and waggle their butts when at rest, so I can notice them even without my glasses. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2015)

I set out today to photograph a fly for the Fly Day Friday group, and I found lots of flies, both at home and the river. But I still couldn't get a photo of the large bee flies that I've been seeing. They take one look at me, and disappear. Well, I'll keep trying.

I think I did see an uncommon Queen Butterfly (Danaus gilippus) today at the river! It was working the same Scale Broom flowers as the rest of my photos today. This one also took one look at me and disappeared. See this old photo at Fotolog.

The weather did swing back to the warm side today, up to 96∞ by the river, where I was. The temps are expected to reach a peak on Sunday - then there's a chance of rain on Monday! That's the way it is in our autumn. It swings... back and forth!
Locality:
Danaus gilippus) today at the river! It was working the same Scale Broom flowers as the rest of my photos today. This one also took one look at me and disappeared. See this old photo at Fotolog.

The weather did swing back to the warm side today
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Queen Butterfly; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Danaus gilippus
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Danaus gilippus

File.Name: f918b_f918-051.jpg
Notes: 20903967204 This is looking close at the face of the interesting Fly (order Diptera) in my [Previous] photo. Flies has such remarkable faces! I found this one working new late-summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family on a bluff down by the (dry) river. It does have a bristly butt in my [Previous] photo, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but maybe not. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)

File.Name: f918c_f918-049.jpg
Notes: 21535083041 This is a larger interesting Fly (order Diptera) than the one in my [Previous] photo. This one is larger than a house fly. I found it working new late-summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family on a bluff down by the (dry) river. It does have a bristly butt, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but maybe not. This fly has a remarkable face, see my [Next] photo. It also has nice black and white stripes on its thorax. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)

File.Name: f918d_f918-035.jpg
Notes: 21499972736 This is an interesting little Fly (order Diptera) working the new late-summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family on a bluff down by the (dry) river. It's smaller than a house fly. It does have a bristly butt, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but maybe not. I saw quite a few of these small flies on the flowers today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)

File.Name: f918e_f918-032.jpg
Notes: 21338936439 This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a late-summer flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's no doubt a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). You can see its proboscis folded under its head. It's interesting that these little predators are color-shifters, often (but not always!) matching the color of the flowers they are on, like this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Scale Broom; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: f918f_f918-054.jpg
Notes: 20904508763 Here is another uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working a new flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name, but there's nothing toxic about it. This is a tighter crop than I prefer, so I stuck it here is the back of my photos for the day. But it's an interesting butterfly, and I want to post a shot of every one I manage to capture. For some reason. these butterflies are having a good year in this time of drought. Checking my records, this is the fifth one I've managed to photograph this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f919a_f919-010.jpg
Notes:
21563836841 This is a pretty little female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family that's just starting to flower up here in the mountains. It's interesting that I usually find these butterflies down by the river - working the same composite plants. Their host plant is said to be mallows, but I don't know of any that are still green in the mountains. It's a small mystery. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)

It was warm today, in the low 90s at home and up to 101∞ down by the river where I was yesterday. Tomorrow is expected to be a bit warmer. Then we actually have a chance of tropical rain on Monday to Wednesday. Screwy weather!
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: f919b_f919-014.jpg
Notes: 20931977214 Here are new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family that's just starting to flower up here in the mountains. These are growing along West Camino Cielo road close to the highway. If you look close, you can see a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans, Oxyopidae) on the right (with prey!) , and a couple of "loogies" of Spittle Bug slime. Those strange insects seem to prefer composites in the late summer. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)
CommonNames: Coastal Goldenbush; Green Lynx Spider; Spittle Bug
ScientificName: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae
associatedTaxa: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae

File.Name: f919c_f919-028.jpg
Notes:
20931350544 This large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) flew into my photo when I was taking the [Previous] shot! It's a beautiful late-summer flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The bee did trample the pistils a bit, like a deer in the garden. We should always be so lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)

I wonder where these large bees live when they're not burrowing into the fascia boards of our house? I don't think I've ever seen a nest in the wild.
CommonNames: Carpenter Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: f919d_f919-027.jpg
Notes: 21527567496 This is a beautiful late-summer flower of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. These flowers have a family resemblance with their cousin Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in my [Previous] photo. Both have long pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar! I was focused close on this flower when a large black Carpenter Bee flew into my shot! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Rod Wirelettuce; Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa

File.Name: f919e_f917-053.jpg
Notes: 20932420033 This is a new flower of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a weedy looking plant, but it is native, and the small flowers are remarkable up close. The remind me a lot of the Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in my [Next] photo. Both have long pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar! The Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in our mountains is very similar, see this photo. That plant is much smaller, only a foot or so high, while S. virgata is as tall as I am or more. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)
CommonNames: Rod Wirelettuce; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Small Wirelettuce
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Stephanomeria exigua; S. virgata
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Stephanomeria exigua; S. virgata

File.Name: f919f_f919-032.jpg
Notes: 21366402909 Here are two different galls on native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. On the right are a group of galled flowers (not berries). On the far left is a galled leaf - curled, with red veins. I'm not sure what critter is in either of these galls, as neither is mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suppose the next step is to bag these galls and look to see what emerges, but I'm happy just to notice them! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)

File.Name: f920a_f920-024.jpg
Notes:
21397233759 Here is a different clutch of tiny spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans, Oxyopidae) from my [Previous] photo. I can't see mama spider, but I'm sure she's nearby keeping an eye on the kids. This is the other egg case, the one in a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae), see this photo. It's interesting that both egg cases picked the same week to hatch. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)

It was wicked hot today - up to 102∞ at the river and into the high 90s at home. It's still 80∞ as I write this at 7 pm. It's hard to get motivated to go out for photos when it's this hot, but I got a few. The NWS is backing of their rain prediction, but it sounds like it will cool down a bit tomorrow.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: f920b_f920-027.jpg
Notes: 21395821710 Here's a clutch of tiny spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans, Oxyopidae). You can see the egg case on the right, and the mama spider on the left, out of focus. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg cases until the hatch is over and have no inclination to eat their young. I tried to get a closer shot, but I like this one best. See this photo of the spiderlings just beginning to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)

File.Name: f920c_f920-001.jpg
Notes: 21395636210 This is another nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning, probably the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). This is probably the last thing many small insects ever see! I've just about closed the book on taking photos of these tiny predators. They seem to be common right now, but sometimes I can't resist! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)

File.Name: f920d_f920-005.jpg
Notes:
21395173038 I believe this is a colony of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This isn't the best photo, but you can see a number of the actual aphids to the right of the woolly mass, both above and below. The aphids live on the underside of the leaf, but I hooked this leaf onto a neighbor leaf to get this photo. Maybe the aphids are bailing out because they don't like the sunlight? (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)

There was some breeze today, which made getting this photo difficult. I did the best I could.
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Stegophylla; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Stegophylla; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f920e_f920-014.jpg
Notes:
21571273652 Well I'll be darned... I found this new flower of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family today in the canyon! You can see a couple of recent buds to the left of the flower, so I guess it wasn't alone. The last flowers of this plant I saw were way back in May. I don't know what it's doing open right now, but I welcome it! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)

This is the benefit of walking the same trails again and again. You see things you otherwise wouldn't notice!

File.Name: f920f_f920-013.jpg
Notes: 21570991972 Here are a pair of different galls on one shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. On the left is the integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera). On the right is a tumor-like gall of the Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera). Galls are plentiful in our mountains - mostly on oaks, but other plants get them too. Autumn seems to be their main time. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2015)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: f921a_f921-005.jpg
Notes:
21425237948 I found this brown male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide behind a new green fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden today. That wouldn't do for a photo, so I nudged it over a bit for a better pose. (It immediately went back to its favorite place!) These male crickets are the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights. See this photo from last year for one caught in the evening cricket jam! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2015)

I took Julie to the airport this morning as she headed off on an adventure with a couple of lady friends. She'll be back in a few weeks. This leaves me with new chores including the garden and our new dog Brodie. No problem, but I have to break him of the habit of snuffling into every new photo-op I find!

Interesting weather today! It started very warm, in the mid-90s when I got home from the airport at noon, and 104∞ at the river. Then it dropped into the 80s, the 70s, and now it's in the low 60s and we're in a cloud. What a remarkable change. Rain is no longer in the forecast, but it sure was an interesting day.

File.Name: f921b_f921-009.jpg
Notes: 21621508911 This is the caterpillar of some kind of Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera), but I have no idea what kind. This is a group that includes Blues, Coppers, and Hairstreaks - a big group! I found this one today feeding on a leaf of some kind of non-native mallow (Malvaceae) in the garden. (It sure looks like a cultivar of native Desert Mallow (Sphaeralcea ambigua) and maybe it is.) It was quite windy today. I took *many* photos to get this one keeper. This caterpillar was not tended by ants. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2015)

File.Name: f921c_f918-037.jpg
Notes: 21621161461 This is a beautiful large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata) spider in the family Araneidae, with distinctive "horns" on its abdomen. This is the same spider I show in this photo a week ago, but it shows the spider better. I found this one right above a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) which is very attractive to many insects. This one looks more white than silvery. Does it have a face on its butt? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f921d_f919-001.jpg
Notes:
21424616959 Here are new berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaves that the berries are coming from. See this photo for the pretty flowers. The berries look tasty, but in my experience they taste bitter, even when dead ripe. The berries are scarce in this drought year, but see this photo of the berries in a better year! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2015)

Most web sites (e.g. Wikipedia) just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else.

File.Name: f921e_f917-021.jpg
Notes: 21423554808 Here are new almost-autumn flowers of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. These are the first fully open flowers I've seen this year. It's another of those late-season composite flowers in our woods. They are important for keeping the insects alive! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)

File.Name: f921f_f917-034.jpg
Notes: 21423997119 These are legume-type seed pods of the locally uncommon native False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. There's just one seed in those glandular pods - if the weevils don't get them first! See this photo of the flowers last June. This shrub is not common in our mountains, and it really looks more like it belongs in the desert. It occurs near the Santa Ynez River and on the mountain passes - Refugio and Arroyo Burro, but for some reason it skips San Marcos Pass. I find this shrub to be very interesting! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2015)

File.Name: f922a_f922-029.jpg
Notes:
21630356912 I guess everyone knows this plant, and it's no one's favorite! But it is a California native and it is remarkably well adapted.

These are the new "flowers" of native Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) in the Asteraceae plant family. The male staminate flowers form on top and the bristly female flowers form below. The pistillate burs are hardly recognizable as flowers, but they are. These plants grow like weeds in disturbed places like the river wash. We've all had to pick the burs from our shoe laces and dogs. It`s been called the original inspiration for velcro - or is that Burdock Thistle (genus Arctium, family Asteraceae)? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)

Marine layer fog - and fog winds - rolled over the mountains this morning, so I headed down to the river to find some sun and calm. But it was windy there too - really too windy for photos, but I got a few. It wasn't super hot today, but it still got up to 90∞ where we were. I had our new dog Brodie with me, and we went for a swim in a spring-fed pool after walking on the exposed dry river bed. Brodie had a good time.

I almost forgot - tonight is the autumnal equinox at 1:22 am PDT! Tomorrow will officially be autumn. It's that time of year when we lose the sun in most of our garden at our north-facing house, but maybe there will soon be mushrooms and banana slugs. Happy equinox!
ScientificName: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae

File.Name: f922b_f922-020.jpg
Notes: 21649975521 Here are new flowers on a shrub of native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis, Asteraceae) on the dry wash of the river. I reckon this shrub isn't widely known, but it has flowers at a time when any flowers are scarce, especially in this drought year. It's another one of the late-summer and autumn flowering yellow-flowered composites that make their home on the dry river bed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)

File.Name: f922c_f922-009.jpg
Notes: 21017895804 Here are new flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. It's a sightly woody perennial with hairy stems, wavy leaves, and ray flowers. We have many composite shrubs and subshrubs along the river right now. See this photo and the two [Previous] to it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)

File.Name: f922d_f922-023.jpg
Notes: 21452227670 These are flowers and seed heads of native Wild Tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus, Asteraceae) growing on the dry river bed. Our native Tarragon has the same species name as the real French Tarragon used as a kitchen herb, but our natives don't seem to have much flavor, even when dried. It's an interesting plant, but not especially pretty. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)

File.Name: f922e_f922-013.jpg
Notes: 21451898690 This is the fabulous foliage of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. They are probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus), but I'm not sure. The plants seem to lose their pretty woolly-white leaves when they flower in early spring, compare this photo of what I'm pretty sure is the same species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)

File.Name: f922f_f922-028.jpg
Notes: 21018361993 I believe these are the leaf galls of the Willow Gall Mite (Aculops tetanothrix, Eriophyidae, Acarina) - kin at least. They are growing on the leaves of some kind of Willow (genus Salix, Salicaceae) plant family. I found these in a damp place down by the river today - one of the last. This section of the river normally holds water, but the old water holes are grown over with a jungle of cattail and other plants. There might still be standing water there, but I didn't care to beat a path through the vegetation to find out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2015)
ScientificName: Aculops tetanothrix; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Salix; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Aculops tetanothrix; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Salix; Salicaceae

File.Name: f923a_f923-009.jpg
Notes:
21654747422 This is a handsome male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on a spent flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo by the river today, but see this photo of a female up in the mountains on the same plant. I also saw these butterflies working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum), but they seem to prefer the Isocoma. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

Today was a very nice day, though it did get up to 94∞ down by the river where I was. I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but I stopped at my favorite place along the river on the way. My son took our new dog Brodie on a walk this morning, so I had some time to concentrate on photos.
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Coastal Goldenbush; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Isocoma
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Isocoma

File.Name: f923b_f923-011.jpg
Notes: 21639682886 This is a handsome Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) on new autumn flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. It is maybe an inch long. I'm not sure what kind it is, but I've seen them before and only on this plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: f923c_f923-024.jpg
Notes: 21042701304 This is another soft-bodied Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera munching a stamen on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). Maybe it's genus Epicauta? They have fat abdomens that extend beyond their wings. They seem quite common on these late-season composite shrubs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

File.Name: f923d_f923-005.jpg
Notes: 21639033036 This is a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a cactus pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I'm pretty sure it's genus Phidippus, maybe a female Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). A moment before, this was was sitting head down just waiting... Then a small Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) landed nearby and the spider came instantly to life! It missed the fly, but it's remarkable how fast it moved to capture it! It's looking where the fly was, but I'm sure it's also looking at me with at least one of those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2015)

This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) tucked into a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's no doubt a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). You can clearly see its crab-like claws. This one looks so earnest! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

Notes: 21041801974 I believe this is a mating pair of Whitecrossed Seed Bugs (Neacoryphus bicrucis) in the family Lygaeidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera. They are having their nuptials on a stem of non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae) that was growing into a shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) , visible in the background. It's interesting that they were rocking back and forth - to scare me away? It didn't work. I reckon that's the larger female to the left. Happy Hump Day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

File.Name: f924a_f924-044.jpg
Notes:
21692830105 My butterfly photo today is this beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on a new autumn flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This photo shows the two (rather than four) eyespots on the underside of its hind wing. It was pretty windy today, and this photo shows it. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2015)

It was another warm day today, and quite breezy. That's a dangerous combo! The winds felt like the "fog winds" that come with the marine layer fog, but there was no fog today. But there was a distinct haze layer that came up to the top of the mountains, and maybe that was the temperature inversion that brings the wind? Whatever, it was breezy today.

This one is perched on a new autumn flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one has its (rather short) proboscis ready for action. It's a handsome fly. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2015)

I've been trying to get a photo of these large flies for some time, and failing. I finally got this shot today, despite the breeze. Sure, I saved a photo for the Fly Day Friday group tomorrow!

I found this one right above a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) which is very attractive to many insects. File.Name: f924e_f921-006.jpg
Notes: 21503701070 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) disappearing over the edge of a green cactus fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) in the garden. See this photo for the whole cricket. I had to nudge it over a bit from the shadows for a better pose, but it immediately went back to its favorite place! These male crickets are the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2015)

See this photo for a shot of the whole fly. It does have a bristly butt, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but looking at this photo it looks more a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae). Whatever it is, it has an interesting behind. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2015)

File.Name: f925a_f925-004.jpg
Notes:
21530780189 Here is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new flowers in the mint patch of the garden this morning. Maybe it's genus Mecaphesa? We're starting to lose the sun in our garden after the equinox a few days ago, but I waited for the light to come through the trees for this photo. It was worth waiting! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2015)

It was warm and breezy again today, in the low 90s on our upstairs porch. Tomorrow might be the hottest day, then it's expected to cool down - just a bit.

File.Name: f925b_f925-012.jpg
Notes: 21726753871 This is an outstanding Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) sitting in an unusual perch on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm sure it's a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the subfamily of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). I wonder what it's doing up in the tree? (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2015)

File.Name: f925c_f925-008.jpg
Notes: 21705575492 This is a small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Maybe it's another Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini) that we also had last spring? At least it's similar to that species. These little flies come in bigger and smaller versions, maybe a gender difference? These are the most common "interesting flies" in the garden right now, but they're not easy to photograph. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2015)

File.Name: f925d_f924-053.jpg
Notes: 21705467152 This is another shot of the large bee-sized Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) - maybe subfamily Anthracinae - that I found yesterday, see this photo. It's working a new autumn flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's proboscis is fully engaged. It's a handsome fly. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2015)

File.Name: f925e_f924-006.jpg
Notes: 21529726309 This is a cute little Fly (order Diptera) that I found posed for takeoff on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. It's tiny. It's 990 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.8 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) At least I got the eyes sharp! It has patterned wings. Maybe it's a Drosophilid (family Drosophilidae) or possibly a Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae)? The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't post this one. But HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2015)

File.Name: f925f_f923-026.jpg
Notes:
21528296658 I wish this were a better photo because it is an interesting fly, but this is the best I got. The fly has the iridescent green/blue colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) in the genus Lucilia - trash flies - and maybe it is. But I found this one in the woods, working flowers of native native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't think it's a trash fly. The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't post this one. But HFDF anyway! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

It looks a bit like the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria) fly to me. See this photo and the photos at BugGuide. These flies are green or blue, and have a white or yellow beard. It's hard to judge colors with these iridescent critters.

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. The fly is native to North America. Its larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue, requiring an exit would to infest. It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.
Locality: Cochliomyia macellaria) fly to me. See this photo and the photos at BugGuide. These flies are green or blue
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Scale Broom; Secondary Screwworm
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Lucilia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Lucilia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae

File.Name: f926a_f926-016.jpg
Notes:
21753240221 At first glance, this is just another Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). But look closer in the lower-left and you will notice the large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is stalking this prey! Maybe the spider is a Whitebanded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the facial band is yellow not white? The autumn flower is native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This location near the Knapp's Castle trail is the lowest place I've found the plant, just within the boundary of what I consider to be San Marcos Pass - from Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle, and from the Trout Club to the base of Cold Springs Bridge. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2015)

It was downright hot today, almost 100∞ in the shade downstairs, and in the 90s on the upstairs deck. It's expected to be a few degrees cooler tomorrow, but not enough to matter. The heat makes it difficult to concentrate on photos. Having our new puppy Brodie along doesn't help!
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Crab Spider; Whitebanded Crab Spider; Douglas' Groundsel
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Thomisidae; Misumenoides formosipes; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: f926b_f926-030.jpg
Notes: 21717208096 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working an autumn flower of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like their cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), but the foliage is quite different. Like its cousin, these shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This plant is male or staminate. These shrubs are pretty much endemic in our mountains, with a few outliers. The butterflies have lovely orange and white bodies. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 September 2015)
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush

File.Name: f926c_f926-015.jpg
Notes: 21752339651 This is a little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a still-open flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family, with the sun behind. I'm getting confused by our late summer skippers, so I'll just "skip her" as they say. It sure is pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2015)

File.Name: f926d_f926-005.jpg
Notes: 21731027602 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of (I think) a native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family still flowering in our garden. The bee is pulling out of the flower, showing its long proboscis. The flowers are interesting, growing in a place that gets regular summer water. The usual rule is to not water natives in the summer, but these native plants do just fine. They just appeared in the garden many years ago, and are still doing OK. They will never be a garden favorite, because the flowers soon turn brown after opening and not very attractive. The bees love these flowers and keep coming back. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2015)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: f926e_f926-001a.jpg
Notes: 21751597851 This is a beautiful little orange Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. See this photo for a better shot of what I think is the same fly. It might just be another Muscidae fly, but it's rather pretty. This one is larger and hairier than the orange fly in this photo yesterday, maybe 1/4 inch long. The contrasty light what a challenge, but this photo is decent. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2015)

File.Name: f926f_f926-010.jpg
Notes: 21741896955 Here are two very different galls on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family. Both galls are the work of different Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). The gall on the left is the "pinched form" of the work of a Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae) growing on the underside of the leaf. The wasp larva is in that dark bump in the center. The one on the right sure looks like a new gall of an "oak apple gall" of California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) growing on a stem. I'm really confused by the timing of these latter galls. This one is about the size of a grape, but it will get much larger if conditions allow. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2015)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasps; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; California Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus

File.Name: f927a_f927-013.jpg
Notes:
21781763201 This handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) is standing at attention on a new flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. I believe it is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes). The bright red and blue legs are quite attractive.

It was 10 degrees cooler today than it was yesterday. *Whew* I'm glad! It was still in the 80s at home and up to 97∞ at the river today, but its turning in the right direction. There were high clouds today that might interfere with eclipse viewing. I'm still waiting for the moon to rise over the ridge to the east.

File.Name: f927b_f927-008.jpg
Notes:
21148925904 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Maybe it's a female Thiodina hespera with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes? It's almost translucent, with beautiful blue markings under it's eyes. Heh, you can see through the lens of its "right anterior median eye" - that big one in front! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2015)

This spider is really tiny. It's 1080 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.)

File.Name: f927c_f927-026.jpg
Notes: 21148749424 Here are more new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are quite glandular, but these also seem unusually pale. You an see a bit of the typical peeling bark on the stem. These are the first new flowers of the new rain year, coming before real rain. I first noticed them on Sep. 7 with this photo. Yep, this is the same shrub I've been showing. It's the only one with flowers so far, but I have been seeing buds on other plants. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2015)

File.Name: f927d_f927-029.jpg
Notes: 21148417484 Here are ripening berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. Not every tree has berries in this drought year, but hopefully there will be enough for the Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata). See my Christmas photo from last year, actually taken a few years ago. I've been hearing the pigeons lately in our oak trees. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2015)

File.Name: f927e_f927-023.jpg
Notes: 21770914285 Here are two shots of insect-galled leaves of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family - head on and from the side. The galled leaves form a little pocket at the base where a critter lives. I'm not sure what critter makes these galls, as it's not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suppose the next step is to bag the galls and see what emerges, but I'm happy just to notice them! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2015)

File.Name: f927f_f924-027.jpg
Notes:
21770563825 Seeds of life...

This is looking into a ripe seedpod of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road in our mountains. There is new life in there! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2015)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f928a_f928-031.jpg
Notes:
21799628785 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). This one is working a new flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family, though the actual yellow flower is hidden behind its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

I did get a view of the eclipse last night through the clouds, though the moon didn't come up over the ridge to the east until 8:30 when the eclipse was past full. I'm sure many other photographers got photos with better equipment than I have for that sort of thing. It was also cloudy this morning, and breezy too. It was almost chilly, though it did warm up to the 80s in between.

File.Name: f928b_f928-001.jpg
Notes: 21611268990 This is a tiny Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) sitting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It was cloudy this morning and almost chilly - high clouds instead of the usual marine-layer fog. The low light turned out to be just right to bring out the iridescent colors of this beautiful little fly. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: f928c_f928-023.jpg
Notes:
21176206634 This is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the mint patch of the garden. Maybe it's genus Mecaphesa? It was trying to hide under a leaf, but I managed to nudge it out into the sun for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

I've had good luck finding spiders lately, so I might try a run in the Arachtober group this year. The challenge is to post a spider pic every day during the month of October. I've started saving alternate takes of my spider photos in a special folder.

File.Name: f928d_f928-008.jpg
Notes: 21177349513 When I first saw this tiny critter, I thought it was an ant. Then I looked closer and saw that it's a spider with eight legs. It's only about 1/8 inch long. Maybe it's an Antlike Jumping Spider (subfamily Synagelinae ) in the family Salticidae? It's about to balloon away from its perch in the mint patch of the garden - if you look close you can see a faint silk thread coming from its abdomen. I know, this isn't the best photo - but I won't apologize for it. It was a cloudy morning with low light. This is a good photo, considering! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: f928f_f928-026.jpg
Notes: 21786097512 I believe these are the galls of a Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa) in the Cynipidae family of the Hymenoptera. These are growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae. This has not been a good year for oak galls, no doubt because it hasn't been a good year for the wasps that make them. Four years of drought has made a difference. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: f929a_f929-027.jpg
Notes:
21638993469 This is a beautiful little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working new pistillate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. These butterflies have been scarce this year, so I'm happy to find one now. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

It was cool and breezy this morning, then warm and downright windy in the afternoon. The wind had all the makings of a sundowner event, but it seems to have backed off. That F-word that rhymes with "ire" has me on edge.

We got a postcard that our power will be off from 8-3 tomorrow for maintenance work. No problem, I also have errands in town tomorrow. Maybe no new photos, but I have lots in the Good Stuff folder.

File.Name: f929b_f929-021.jpg
Notes: 21799407186 This is another pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a new flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family in our canyon. The butterflies have been scarce since June, so I'm happy to find them again. At first, I thought this might be some other kind of Blue (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera), but I'm pretty sure it's an Acmon. You can see some iridescent speckles on the hindwing eyespots. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: f929c_f929-004.jpg
Notes: 21637209118 I found this goofy-looking critter on a leaf in the garden this morning. I'm pretty sure it's a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - maybe genus Orgerius in the family Dictyopharidae? This may well be the adult. I tried to reposition my tripod to get a different angle, and I managed to bump the plant - and the planthopper hopped away. The white spots on the leaf are the result of our hard water. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: f929d_f929-009.jpg
Notes: 21201775784 This is a brown female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on top of the fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden today. The fruit is obscured by the old remains of the flower. If you compare this photo of a male cricket, you will see that the males have much broader wings than the ladies. The males do all the singing at night. The ladies are silent, but receptive. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: f929e_f929-018.jpg
Notes: 21636219718 I believe this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) looking for prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - but I've been wrong about these before! This one is less than an inch long, and it has the same "so blue it's black" colors as our Tarantula Hawks (genus Pepsis). This was a "shoot first, think later" photo as the twitchy wasp was only on the leaf for a moment. As it turns out, it was underexposed by about one full stop. I tried to brighten it up a bit afterwards. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)
CommonNames: Spider Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Tarantula Hawks
ScientificName: Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pepsis
associatedTaxa: Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pepsis

File.Name: f929f_f929-007.jpg
Notes: 21833365321 These are ripening seeds of Curly Dock (Rumex crispus) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Apparently it's not native, but it may as well be. The plant is abundant in disturbed places, especially when there's a bit of extra moisture. The new seeds in their husks are quite pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: f930a_f929-030.jpg
Notes:
21850997495 This is another photo of a pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in the canyon. This one is sitting on a new pistillate flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is probably the same butterfly I show in this photo, back on the flowers where I first noticed it. These butterflies have been scarce since June, so I'm happy to find them again. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

I took our cat to the vet this morning and returned home to find out that we were officially "evacuated" as the power people had to use a helicopter to replace a power pole in our canyon. We had to kill a few hours before I got home, and Ginger the cat was not happy! All I knew about this was a postcard saying that the power would be off from 8-3 today. As Strother Martin famously said in the film Cool Hand Luke, "What we've got here is failure to communicate."

The real news today was the wind. It started windy and cool, then got windy and hot as the day went on. I couldn't have got any photos anyway in this wind. It feels like a real sundowner, *ire weather for sure.

File.Name: f930b_f928-012.jpg
Notes: 21663883509 This is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a damp path in the garden after watering. It looks fresh - you can still see some blue on its forewings, which wears away before too long. I dig those soccer ball eyes! Sure, this is an easy photo, but I never get tired of photographing these fine butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: f930c_f929-015.jpg
Notes: 21229445243 This is another adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a fading staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. Most of the flowers are done, but maybe this predator has his eyes on that one flower in the background that is still going. I believe this is the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coyote Bush; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: f930d_f929-029.jpg
Notes:
21663266549 This is another photo of the tiny but pretty flowers and seed heads of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in our canyon. The flower stalks are really inferior ovaries that finally split open to release dandelion-like seeds. It's related to common wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry summer soil in our mountains to flower. Most of our autumn flowers are composites, so this one that is something else in interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

We seem to have two different populations of these plants in our mountains. Some are just a foot or two tall like this one, while others are head-high or more like the one in this photo.

File.Name: f930e_f928-034.jpg
Notes: 21859449601 These are new and old flowers of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a low woody shrub that gets masses of flowers as summer comes to an end and into autumn. The flower heads are pretty, with involucres like little pine cones. This is one of those plants that keeps the pollinator insects alive during this tough time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: f930f_f929-022.jpg
Notes: 21823474886 This is the inflorescence of a native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family in our canyon. It grows like a weed along roads, but it really is a native plant. It has a tall stem that opens up into flowers on top. I know at least one native plant gardener who was surprised to discover that it is a native. Insects don't seem to like it much, but maybe it counts as survival food? I found an Acmon Blue butterfly on the flowers yesterday - but only after I chased it away from the Coyote Bush that it was really interested in. See my photo two shots [back]. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: fa01a_fa01-003.jpg
Notes:
21849705626 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a micro-moth (Lepidoptera) for prey. Maybe it's genus Mecaphesa? I found this one on a staminate shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. It was windy, so I had to take *many* photos to get this keeper. Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2015)

We actually had just a bit of rain this morning! It wasn't enough to measure, but it did get the cushion of my chair on the porch wet. But it was also windy all day which made getting new photos difficult. I went shopping. Maybe the wind will die down tomorrow.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Lepidoptera; Mecaphesa; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Lepidoptera; Mecaphesa; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa01b_fa01-005.jpg
Notes:
21687546678 Empty nest syndrome?

This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans, Oxyopidae) perched on her empty egg case with the shed skins of her offspring. She will probably perch here until she dies when cold weather finally comes. She is in a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I have a photo of her babies here. This is also a pretty nice "butt shot" - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2015)

It's been a good week for "beautiful bug butts" - mostly critters trying to get away from me!
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: fa01c_f929-011.jpg
Notes: 21863252922 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on top of the fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden today. The fruit is obscured by the old remains of the flower. See this photo for a photo of the whole cricket on the same fruit, but this is the view as it crawled away from me. Those spiky things on its butt are her ovipositors. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: fa01d_f928-005.jpg
Notes: 21875051315 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a tiny Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) sitting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It was a cloudy morning, just right to bring out the iridescent colors of this beautiful little fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2015)

File.Name: fa01e_f926-028.jpg
Notes: 21687857729 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working an autumn flower of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. See this photo for another shot of the same butterfly. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 September 2015)
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis

File.Name: fa01f_f929-016.jpg
Notes:
21686377650 These are no-longer-new flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a tough plant to photograph - too contrasty, too easy to over-expose. I think this photo does it right. These plants have been flowering for a while, but half-heartedly in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

"Mug-wort" is the mug plant, and of course you drink beer from mugs. I've heard that mugwort is used to flavor Porter Beers - but maybe that's a different mugwort? This one has a sharp sage-like smell that is not unpleasant, but not what I want in my beer! Many people believe it is a remedy for poison oak, and I believe it does have astringent properties. And it certainly does no harm!

File.Name: fa02a_fa02-045.jpg
Notes:
21279546364 Here are two shots of a beautiful Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly showing her upper and lower wings. This is the first one I've seen since April. She's working still-new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. If you compare the photo on the right with this photo on an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, you can see the difference between the two - four eyes on her hindwings instead of two. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 October 2015)

I'm a bit distracted this evening. I went to the Santa Ynez River today looking for a particular Silver Argiope spider - and it wasn't in the place where it's been for a few weeks. Oh well, I did get some nice photos along Paradise Road, starting and stopping my truck many times. Then I drove home with no problem, but coming down the road in our canyon, my truck suddenly sputtered and died. It wouldn't start. *Sigh* I'll call triple-A tomorrow. At least it died on our road and not on the highway!
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Scale Broom; American Lady; Silver Argiope
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Vanessa virginensis
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Vanessa virginensis

File.Name: fa02b_fa02-062.jpg
Notes:
21713966318 Here's a two-fer of a Beetle (Coleoptera) and a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) sharing flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I believe the beetle is a soft-bodied Blister Beetle (family Meloidae) and the bug is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae). This image was at the bottom of my photo, with no room to expand the image - but it is a pretty cool shot, getting both insects in focus. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 October 2015)

The beetle is a chewer - chewing the stamens for their pollen. The bug is a sucker - sucking nectar from the flowers. Different strokes for different folks!
CommonNames: Beetle; Scale Broom; Blister Beetle; Bordered Plant Bug
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Meloidae; Largidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Meloidae; Largidae

File.Name: fa02c_fa02-036.jpg
Notes: 21278946554 Here's a two-fer of beautiful Vespid Wasps (Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I believe they are Paper Wasps (genus Polistes) - maybe Polistes dorsalis californicus, but there are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" like this - all handsome. I usually only see these wasps near the end of summer, and I've never seen their nests. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 October 2015)
CommonNames: Vespid Wasps; Scale Broom; Paper Wasps
ScientificName: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Polistes; Polistes dorsalis californicus
associatedTaxa: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Polistes; Polistes dorsalis californicus

File.Name: fa02d_fa02-004.jpg
Notes: 21889190422 I found this interesting Fly (order Diptera) on the upstairs porch railing this morning. I think it might be genus Euthera, a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. It has pretty patterned wings. It also has really interesting wing-like halteres that I've never seen before - those dark things rising above and just behind the wing joints. I see the same thing in this photo at BugGuide, so maybe this fly is Euthera setifacies? My tripod was downstairs and I didn't want to leave the fly, so I improvised. This fly might be locally unusual. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2015)

File.Name: fa02e_f929-006.jpg
Notes: 21713034778 This is a beautiful little orange Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. It looks a bit like a Drosophilid, but it's probably just be another Muscidae fly. It is rather pretty. I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2015)

File.Name: fa02f_f923-017.jpg
Notes:
21713517479 This is a handsome large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider wrapping its prey. These spiders have remarkable "horns" on their abdomen. I found this one right above a flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) which is very attractive to many insects. Arachtober 2. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2015)

This photo looks remarkably similar to this shot, but it is a different photo as well as a different crop. I went back to this place near the Santa Ynez River today fully expecting to get a fresh shot - and the spider was gone. Its web looked abandoned and full of dandelion-like seeds from the scale broom. Darn, I'll have to find another one. I had my mind set on posting a photo of this spider today, so here it is - albeit from the archives.
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa03a_fa03-012.jpg
Notes:
21927142005 Here is yet another spider-like adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see its mighty proboscis. I believe this is the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I know, I've been showing a lot of these critters, but I like this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

It was sunny and warm today without a cloud in the sky, but the NWS forecast still calls for rain tomorrow - rain likely, 60% chance. I'll believe it when I see it! But clouds do seem to be obscuring the stars right now, and there is some breeze from the south...
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: fa03b_fa03-001.jpg
Notes:
21926624465 Just a ball of fluff - with legs!

Here are four shots of (I believe) a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) insect in the insect order Neuroptera. You can best see its pincher-like jaws in the lower-right photo. I found this one on the underside of a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It's tiny, maybe 1/8 inch. I know these aren't the best photos, but what a great critter! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

File.Name: fa03c_fa03-008.jpg
Notes:
21303434894 I'm pretty sure this is the web of a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) - a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae). It makes perfect little orb webs and decorates them with the bodies of its prey - not many in this case, but it's been a tough time for spiders in this drought year. This is the first one I've seen in some time. The webs are about the size of a CD, with CD-like color vanes. If you look close, you can see the tiny spider sitting in her inner circle. Arachtober 3. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

You have to work not to over-expose such shots. The web seems to disappear on the right because of a shadow. They almost disappear in direct sunlight! I got this photo with the sun behind, a few stops underexposed.

File.Name: fa03d_fa03-031.jpg
Notes: 21737919698 This is a mating pair of Ambush Bugs (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. At least I assume they are mating and the same species - they look so different! The white one on the right seems to have longer antennae, and the brown one on the left has a little twist at the base of its antennae. As far as I know, these are both Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica). They are color-shifters. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)
CommonNames: Ambush Bugs; Coyote Bush; Pacific Ambush Bugs
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: fa03e_fa03-011.jpg
Notes:
21935287761 Here is another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) sprawled across leaves in the mint patch of the garden this morning. The males do all the singing at night. The ladies are silent, but a receptive audience. Compare this photo of a male cricket, with much broader wings. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

This photo may look like I used flash, but I rarely use it. This is just our natural light as the sun starts to track below the oak trees at our north-slope house.

File.Name: fa03f_fa03-014.jpg
Notes:
21925206745 This native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family only has one successful berry in this drought year. That's the way it is. Compare this photo of the berries in a better year! You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaves that the berries are coming from, like a little umbrella. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

The berries look tasty, but in my experience they taste bitter, even when dead ripe. Most web sites (e.g. Wikipedia) just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - but isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else. Now why would any plant produce inedible berries?

File.Name: fa04a_fa04-004.jpg
Notes:
21765145428 I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, with raindrops. My ID is based on its web and eye pattern - it looks a lot like this photo at BugGuide of a genus Agelenopsis spider. Look at that neat way it's holding its third legs up in the air - a threatening pose? I got this photo in low light and light rain today, in a shrub of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. See my [Previous] photo (here) for a shot of part of it's web. Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)

We did have rain today, a bit over 1/4 inch! Not the many inches of rain we need to recharge the oaks, but not bad for early October. It's also quite chilly with the high today not much over 50∞. There's a chance for more rain tonight, but there's already been a patch of blue sky "big enough to make a pair of Dutchmanís breeches" (as my Mom used to say) so that might be it. On the other hand, it's clouded up again, so I'm still hoping for more.
Locality: as my Mom used to say) so that might be it.  On the other hand

File.Name: fa04b_fa04-009.jpg
Notes: 21952654495 Here are raindrops on (I believe) the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in a shrub of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. The pattern of raindrops on these webs seems distinctive, different from the similar Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) webs - compare this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)
CommonNames: Funnel Web Weaver; Rosemary; Sheetweb Spider

File.Name: fa04c_fa04-012.jpg
Notes: 21764256058 These are new seedlings coming up from a mulch of mostly oak leaves, with one larger madrone leaf on the right. I got this photo in the rain today, but the plants no doubt came up after the rain on September 15, see this photo. I think the seedlings are all weedy plants of non-native Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae). The weeds always sprout first and sometimes fail if more rain doesn't come, but there are *always* more weed seeds to try again, so they can afford to be profligate. The natives are more conservative, and wait until we've had an inch or two of rain before they sprout. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)

File.Name: fa04d_fa04-022.jpg
Notes: 21764918929 This is the trunk of a young native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family, with raindrops. How beautiful these trees are with their peeling bark and skin-like stems. This photo of the "Naked lady Madrone" is one of my top-viewed photos - though maybe that's just because of the name? (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)

File.Name: fa04e_fa04-021.jpg
Notes: 21330239593 This is a tangle of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) climbing over a young native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The clematis is deciduous which gives the madrone a better chance to get some sun in the winter months. Look at the lichens on the oaks in the background. How quickly they come back with a bit of rain! It was raining pretty good when I got this photo from our covered front porch today, but it's hard to capture falling rain unless it really dumps. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: fa04f_fa04-015.jpg
Notes: 21329842993 You can bet these drought-stressed plants of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family were happy to see a bit of rain today! Compare this photo from last spring. The plants are hanging on - barely. We've had a bit over 1/4 inch of rain so far from today's storm. Not the many inches of rain we need to recharge the oaks, but enough to help these sages. There's a chance for more rain tonight, but there is already a patch of blue sky "big enough to make a pair of Dutchmanís breeches" (as my Mom used to say) so that might be it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2015)

File.Name: fa05a_fa05-022.jpg
Notes:
21970527062 We had a hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) today - from our driveway, not the foundation of our house! I tried to find where they were emerging from, but no luck. Maybe I stepped on their exit hole? This might be a Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae). After our rain yesterday, I've looking for mushrooms and banana slugs, but the termites are also critters that the rain brings out. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)

We ended up with just over 1/4 inch of rain from the storm yesterday, bringing our total for the rain year (since September 1) to 0.86 inches - 155% of normal! Sounds good, but we're still in drought conditions, and it's expected to swing back to the warm side later this week.

I did arrange with triple-A to tow my truck down to town today. It's a bad fuel pump. I'll get it back tomorrow.
Locality: since September 1) to 0.86 inches - 155% of normal!  Sounds good

File.Name: fa05b_fa05-013.jpg
Notes: 21795212629 This is a different Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) that cannot roll up into a ball. I call these "sow bugs" - as distinct from the "pill bug" in my [Previous] photo. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. This one is up in a flower in the mint patch of the garden. I dig those bumpy eyes! These are critters that the rain brings out. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)

File.Name: fa05c_fa05-018.jpg
Notes: 21955570656 I believe this the woodlouse that can roll up in a ball, so I guess it's genus Armadillidium, probably Armadillidium vulgare. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. They are known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names. This one is kind of pretty with those yellow markings! This isn't the best photo, but it goes well with my [Next] photo. These are critters that the rain brings out. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)
ScientificName: woodlouse; Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea
associatedTaxa: woodlouse; Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea

File.Name: fa05d_fa05-021.jpg
Notes:
21990711201 Here are two photos of very different insects eggs taken close to home.

On the left is an "Ootheca" (egg mass) that I suspect are the eggs of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). These are probably the eggs of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) which are so common in the woods right now, including this same shrub. I wouldn't have known this, but compare this fine photo by Mr. Hickatee Ian Morton in Belize. Thanks Ian! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)

On the right are tiny white insect eggs that I can't identify on a spine of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I got this photo a couple of weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Nuttall's Snowberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Prickly Pear
ScientificName: Ootheca; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Zelus renardii; Opuntia; Cactaceae
