File.Name: fa05e_fa05-009.jpg
Notes: 21793614099 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on fading flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family, see my [Previous] photo. I'm not sure what kind it is, but from the hairs/setae on its abdomen, I suspect it's another genus Mecaphesa. This one is small, but I found another larger one nearby - but with a crummy photo. What do these spiders do when their flowers fade away? Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)

File.Name: fa05f_fa05-004.jpg
Notes: 21793203769 These are fading flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. They are turning brown at the end of their flowering time. The local Santa Barbara author Stewart Edward White once wrote (something like) "it's the brown of ripeness, not decay." His book "The Mountains" (1904) remains one of the better books about the Santa Barbara backcountry. It's available at the Internet Archive if you can't find a print copy. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2015)

File.Name: fa06a_fa06-016.jpg
Notes:
22018071691 Finally, a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera)! I believe this one is genus Sinea or maybe Acholla. Its font legs don't seem to be spiny, but its head sure is. What ever it is, it's a gnarly predator! This one is perched on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

It was very nice today, with highs around 70∞. The weather is expected to swing back to the warm side over the next few days. Yep, I got my truck back after it got a new fuel pump. Runs fine, but that was expensive. I'll probably think about this every time I cross the highway - it could *suddenly* die!
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Sinea; Acholla; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Sinea; Acholla; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fa06b_fa06-028.jpg
Notes:
21819626020 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a nearly spent pistillate flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got a better shot of this spider today, but this is such a perfect pose! It was a calm morning, but a pesky breeze kicked up just as a found this tiny spider. Maybe it's another genus Mecaphesa? They seem common in the woods right now when you look close. Arachtober 6. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

It was a good day for crab spider photos! I found two more, white and yellow, which I'll save in case I need them for Arachtober.

File.Name: fa06c_fa06-011.jpg
Notes: 22017173461 This is a tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. My first thought was a Braconid Wasp in the family Braconidae - just because it's tiny. But bleu.geo suggests it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae. The wasp is grooming its wings. It is 900 pixels across in my 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, 1/8 inch. Tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

File.Name: fa06d_fa06-013.jpg
Notes: 21818586630 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after a rain. You can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in the light trails. My exposure time was 1/15 second, and I count mostly 11 beats in that time, so they are beating their tiny wings 165 times a second - really fast! You can see a hint of the actual flies at the top. Maybe they are tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae? This seems early in the year to find them, and maybe that's a good sign for weather to come! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

File.Name: fa06e_fa06-017.jpg
Notes:
21818158020 This is another short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) on a stem of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) this morning. See my [Previous] photo. It's a handsome little beast. I wonder if its been eating those prickly leaves? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

The plants were looking terrible a few weeks ago, but they are greening up after recent small rains. It's interesting to observe how native plants deal with our four-year drought. It's good to know that some native plants have strategies. I guess all our natives have been through extended droughts before, and can adapt.

File.Name: fa06f_fa06-019.jpg
Notes: 21979772316 This is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) munching a leaf in a still-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. See my [Next] photo. Uh - do you notice anything missing? This grasshopper has lost its right hind leg - it's jumping leg. I wonder if it always veers to the right, or can it compensate? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

File.Name: fa07a_fa07-029.jpg
Notes:
21842318019 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) perched on her egg case in a native shrub of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a "green lynx" though this one is more yellow and others are more brown. I think they all have green faces. That's a composite seed of the shrub on the right, next to part of of "loogie" of Spittle Bug slime - see my [Previous] two photos. Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)

It was a lovely day today, a bit warmer than yesterday - 80s rather than 70s. Over the next couple of days we're expected to be in the 90s. So much for our little rain! But there's a chance of more rain next week...

File.Name: fa07b_fa07-006.jpg
Notes: 21406088104 I believe this is an adult Froghopper (Cercopoidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) whose larvae are the Spittlebugs that make frothy "loogies" on our late summer composites. See my [Previous] photo. This one is sitting on a leaf of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a "loogie" just below. I guess the first question is which end is which? Look close! It sure looks like it has black eyes on the left, but I'm sure its real eyes are on the right, with antennae. It looks a lot like this photo at BugGuide of the species Clastoptera lineatocollis from Orange County in Southern California, but who knows. I took many photos in the breeze today, and this was my best shot. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)
ScientificName: Cercopoidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Clastoptera lineatocollis

File.Name: fa07c_fa07-015.jpg
Notes: 21840347260 These are new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains - with fresh "loogies" of Spittle Bug (Cercopoidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) slime. These strange insects seem to prefer composites in the late summer, and their larvae live inside the loogies on several different kinds of plants. The adult insects are not so common, but see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)
ScientificName: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Cercopoidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: fa07d_fa07-018.jpg
Notes: 22002164756 These are ripening berries of native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The plant is distinctive for its narrow leaves which are quite unlike the more typical Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) which has broader leaves and is more common away from the coast, see this photo. The great botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen with narrow leaves from the coast in 1831. The inland variety with wide leaves is more common throughout the area, but the first named collection remains official. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata

File.Name: fa07e_fa07-024.jpg
Notes:
21406900353 This is a horse of a different color - walking across West Camino Cielo road in our mountains!

I believe it's a Plains Zebra (Equus quagga), and it no doubt got loose from its home at the Tanbark Oaks community. I've heard about this zebra, but this is the first time I've actually seen it. Apparently his name is Maynard. It was in the company of a couple of Llamas. Pretty cool, but these creatures shouldn't be wandering on the road like this. They might get hurt! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)

I just noticed looking at my photo on Flickr - I dig that little toe-step dance it's doing!

File.Name: fa07f_fa03-026.jpg
Notes: 21405076834 This is another photo of the mating pair of Ambush Bugs (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's interesting that they are so close to the female/pistillate flower. See this photo for another shot of the same bugs. They look different, but as far as I know, they are both Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica). They are color-shifters. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2015)

File.Name: fa08a_fa08-018.jpg
Notes:
22033176122 I believe this is an extraordinary diurnal (daytime) Lichen Moth (Lithosiini, Arctiidae) - maybe Lycomorpha fulgens or Lycomorpha regulus. I don't know anything about them, except here is this one working staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. When you look close in the woods, you never know what you'll find! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

It was hot today, but a layer of high clouds helped mitigate the heat a bit. When the clouds gave way, the temperature rose to 95∞ on our upstairs porch, no doubt hotter down lower. Tomorrow might be a notch warmer, but then it should cool down a bit.

It was breezy today, but it's working up to downright windy tonight. Hot and windy makes me nervous.

File.Name: fa08b_fa08-033.jpg
Notes:
21856968860 This is another mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) with a clutch of tiny spiderlings. You can see her egg case in the background. This one has made her nest in a shrub of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae), though you can't tell by looking. What a remarkable "face" she has on her abdomen, as do so many of these large spiders who sit on their webs all day. Yep jciv, they come in many colors, green or brown or yellow or almost red like this one. I think they all have green faces. Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

I could probably fill my card for the whole month of Arachtober with these spiders if I really looked. They are quite common in the woods right now.

File.Name: fa08c_fa08-024.jpg
Notes: 22018698396 This is a "butt shot" of a Fly (order Diptera) on staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It does have a bristly butt, so maybe it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? It seems I've been saying that a lot lately, but this is their time. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

File.Name: fa08d_fa06-009.jpg
Notes: 22044755875 This is a "butt shot" of a tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. My first thought was a Braconid Wasp in the family Braconidae - just because it's tiny. But bleu.geo (who knows wasps) suggests it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae. The wasp is grooming. It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch. See this photo for another shot of the same wasp. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

File.Name: fa08e_fa06-032.jpg
Notes: 21856487370 This is another brown female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding "butts up" in the flower scar of a new fruit of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2015)

File.Name: fa08f_fa08-035.jpg
Notes:
21856515018 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say).

Here are the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today in the mountains. They're early. I didn't notice the first flowers last year until November 19, see this photo. I don't know what this means. Maybe I'm looking closer, or the native plants are confused? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 October 2015)

File.Name: fa09a_fa09-007.jpg
Notes:
21876248799 This is another brown female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide "butts up" in the flower scar of a new fruit of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. It's trying to hide "Ostrich-style" - if it can't see you then you can't see it? I've been finding lots of these critters lately - and hearing many more every night in the evening chorus. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2015)

It was wicked hot today - up to 103∞ at the river, where I was for a while. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped by the river to get a few photos. But It was really too darn hot - as Ella used to sing. It was too hot for insects and photographers both.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; too darn hot
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; too darn hot

File.Name: fa09b_fa09-006.jpg
Notes: 21441751333 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I guess it's a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) - not a lovely name, but it's a handsome fly. Hah, there's probably a different name for every bristle on its body! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2015)

File.Name: fa09c_fa08-026.jpg
Notes: 21874861608 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It is rather pretty, and it's walking over flowers instead of your potato salad! I posted another photo of the same fly's bristly butt yesterday for BBBT - see this photo. Maybe it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? It seems I've been saying that a lot lately, but this is their time. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

File.Name: fa09d_fa07-010.jpg
Notes: 21439437734 This is a tiny - but perfectly formed - Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) tucked into its nest on a leaf of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one is only about 750 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which makes it about 2.9 mm long - less than 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I expect these spiders to be much larger - like bottle cap size. Small as it is, this one has markings like a tiny "Cat-faced Spider" - see this photo. It's the size that has me stumped. Pretty cool! Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2015)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa09e_fa08-021.jpg
Notes:
22035739796 I can't resist another photo of the extraordinary diurnal (daytime) Lichen Moth (Lithosiini, Arctiidae) that I found yesterday - maybe Lycomorpha fulgens or Lycomorpha regulus. I don't know anything about them, except here is this one working staminate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

They look remarkably like small Tarantula Hawks (Pepsis, Hymenoptera) with those orange wings and so-blue-it-looks-black bodies. Compare this photo. I wonder if this counts as Batesian mimicry?
CommonNames: Lichen Moth; Coyote Bush; Tarantula Hawks

File.Name: fa09f_fa08-015.jpg
Notes:
21873606600 This is a nice photo of new flower buds (next year) and ripening fruit (this year) on a tree of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. It's nice, because fruit and buds don't usually occur on the same branch. Some native plants form their buds early like this, most other don't. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

The trees are related to avocados, and it shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". The fruit taste very pungent unless they are dead ripe - when they are invariably rotten. But the nuts inside are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right. See this YouTube video. The trees are not producing many fruit in this drought year. We haven't prepared the nuts since the drought began, but every few years we get a bumper crop. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but not too much!

File.Name: fa10a_fa10-006.jpg
Notes:
22054990836 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the lip of the flower scar of a new fruit of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I don't know what it is about these cactus fruit, but other insects and spiders also like them - see this photo from yesterday. It sure looks like another genus Mecaphesa spider. This one is tiny, about 730 pixels long (not counting legs!) in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - less than 1/8 inch long! (See this photo for how I figured.) Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)

The light was fading when I got this photo, but it's OK. You don't realize how fast the sun moves until you try to get a photo when the sun is moving through the trees!

It was a few degrees cooler today, but still hot - up to 92∞ on the upstairs porch and up to 99∞ at the river. I didn't notice any difference out in the sun. The heat makes it hard to think about photos. Tomorrow is expected to be cooler.

File.Name: fa10b_fa10-015.jpg
Notes: 21459853563 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched with wings open (for just a moment!) on a flower head of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis, Asteraceae). See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the flower. I haven't managed to photograph one of these butterflies for a couple of months, though I've seen a few flying in our canyon. This one seems unusually dark, compare this photo. I first thought it was something different. It's not the best photo - it was breezy and the butterfly was quite camera shy. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa10c_fa10-009.jpg
Notes: 22054534646 These are new yellow composite flowers of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a slender plant with long leaves that favors marshy places, but here it is in a bone-dry ditch along East Camino Cielo Road. Down by the river is probably more typical habitat for this plant. It doesn't look anything like the classic goldenrod in my [Previous] photo. These late-flowering composites keep the insects going in autumn - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa10d_fa10-006.jpg
Notes: 22054429146 These are fading late-summer flowers of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. If you look close, you can still see a couple of fresh flowers on the left. Some other plants are still going strong, and others have completely gone to seed. This is the plant that looks like a real goldenrod. Compare my next photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa10e_fa10-019.jpg
Notes: 22067892622 Last flowers on a shrub of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The shrubs are winding down, but they still have a few flowers for the skipper butterflies that mostly visit them. See this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa10f_fa10-011.jpg
Notes: 22080231525 These are new "flowers" of native Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a composite related to sunflowers, though you have to study the flowers under magnification to see the relationship. I found this one in a drainage ditch along it along the road in the mountains near home, but it's more common down by the river. Nobody loves this plant. It's a native that gets weedy, though the flowers are interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa11a_fa11-006.jpg
Notes:
22089758472 This is a small Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae in the order Hymenoptera - almost certainly genus Gasteruption. There's no obvious ovipositor, so maybe this one is male? It's working a still-new pistillate flower of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this one in silhouette and didn't have time to bump up the exposure before it took off. That works well to show that the head is set on a "neck", distinguishing it from other ichneumons. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2015)

It was another hot day - maybe a degree or two cooler, but you sure couldn't tell out in the sun.

I've been mostly listening to Judy Garland and James Mason in the 1954 film of A Star Is Born while I post tonight. The TV is just around the corner where I can't see it while I work. What a great film. It seems they finally got a complete print together. Of course I had to get up a few times,like for The Man That Got Away. Wow.

associatedTaxa: Gasteruptiidae; Hymenoptera; Gasteruption; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa11b_fa11-002.jpg
Notes: 21480947443 I believe this pretty little fly is a Stiletto Fly, probably genus Thereva, in the family Therevidae of the order Diptera. It's perched for just a moment on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. I expect to see these flies in the spring, so this one is interesting in the fall. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2015)

File.Name: fa11c_fa11-013.jpg
Notes:
21914578279 Look close to see the spider at the bottom of its nest!

I believe this is a nest of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. They make a perfect little orb web a few inches across, but build a separate little cobwebby house off to the side, decorated with the dead bodies of past prey. I found this one in a shrub of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). I'll try to get a better photo when it's not so breezy. Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2015)

This might well be the spider I showed a few days ago in this photo that I thought was a tiny Araneus spider. It was just a few inches away from this web. Maybe that one is a male, with a separate "spider cave" from the female in this photo? I'll have to update the previous photo, but tomorrow works fine.
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; Coastal Goldenbush

File.Name: fa11d_fa11-004.jpg
Notes: 21913344098 This is a seed head of humble native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon today. That seed on top is breaking free. The seeds and especially the receptacle are quite beautiful. This plant grows like a weed along roads, but it really is a native plant. See this photo for the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2015)

File.Name: fa11e_fa10-007.jpg
Notes: 21478125094 These are finished nests of this year's wasps (Hymenoptera) under the eave of our downstairs porch. The one of the left is the mud nest of Mud Dauber Wasps (Sceliphron caementarium or kin, Sphecidae). The one on the right is the paper nest of Western Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae). It seems odd to find them so close together, but here they are. Neither of these wasps has ever bothered me - except for refusing to pose! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)
CommonNames: Mud Dauber Wasps; Western Paper Wasps
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Sceliphron caementarium; Sphecidae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Sceliphron caementarium; Sphecidae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae

File.Name: fa11f_fa10-018.jpg
Notes: 21912473570 These are more-than-ripe berries of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing in the mountains. I was surprised to find one of these plants with flowers a few days ago - see this photo. I went looking for more flowers and didn't find them. I did find this shrub with last year's berries. Sure they're old, but the skins are very tasty. Just spit out the big nut with is the hallmark of this species. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2015)

File.Name: fa12a_fa12-005.jpg
Notes:
22096615936 I believe this is an extraordinary Antlike Jumping Spider (subfamily Synagelinae in the family Salticidae) in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I know this isn't the best photo, but what a remarkable ant-mimic spider! If you look at the eye pattern and compare my [Previous] few photos, there's no doubt that it's a Salticid - genus Peckhamia or Synageles. It really looks like an ant and moves like an ant, though maybe a little too fast. This spider is really tiny, only 700 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, only about 2.7 mm across. (See here for how I figured.) Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2015)

I must thank the Arachtober group for getting me to look close for spiders every day. I've been finding them!

It was cooler and breezier today. I never left the garden because of the breeze, but maybe it's the first sign of a change in the weather. There was also a mackerel sky today moving in from the south-east. We have a 40% chance of rain on Wednesday. Bring it on!

File.Name: fa12b_fa12-004.jpg
Notes: 22122219525 Here are two photos of (I believe) an Antlike Jumping Spider (subfamily Synagelinae ) in the family Salticidae in the mint patch of the garden this morning. These aren't the best photos, but what a remarkable ant-mimic spider! Here it is coming and going. If you look at the eye pattern on the left and compare my [Previous] couple of photos, there's no doubt that it's a Salticid - genus Peckhamia or Synageles. It really looks like an ant and moves like an ant, though maybe a little too fast. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2015)

File.Name: fa12c_fa12-015.jpg
Notes: 22095691736 This is a different tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. This one looks like it's plated with silver. We have many different species, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It sure is handsome. These spiders have amazing eyes - lots of them! They seem aware of everything around them. Note the arrangement of all those eyes as you look at my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2015)

File.Name: fa12d_fa08-004.jpg
Notes: 21933447220 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden a few days ago. We have many different species, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It sure is handsome. This one seems to have especially bushy pedipalps! These spiders have amazing eyes - lots of them! They seem aware of everything around them, including me - the photographer. Note the arrangement of all those eyes as you look at my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2015)

File.Name: fa12e_fa12-001.jpg
Notes: 21933256928 This fly is probably just a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) in the genus Lucilia - a trash fly, aka a Greenbottle Fly. But it sure is pretty with those green/blue iridescent colors and that white face. I found this one on a leaf in the garden this morning. I'd rather find it here than on my potato salad! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2015)

File.Name: fa12f_fa12-007.jpg
Notes: 21933106718 Here are new flowers of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These are growing in the garden where they get regular summer water, and they've almost become a weed. With some natives, you can ignore the rule not to water them in summer! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2015)

File.Name: fa13a_fa13-011.jpg
Notes:
21529089034 I found this lovely Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a cactus pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. I'm sure it's genus Phidippus. It seems an add place to find this, but maybe it's a male Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni) as a mate for the female I found a few weeks ago, see this photo. It's looking straight ahead to the right, but it has me nailed with its "posterior lateral eye"! Arachtober 13. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

It was pretty deep inside the cactus plant, so this is a bit of a crop. I couldn't get any closer! The big thorns are a deterrent, but the tiny "glochid" spines are the worst!

Today started cloudy, but then the sun came out and it warmed up. Then it started cooling down in the afternoon, and some clouds started to move in from the SW. There's still a 30% chance for a bit of tropical rain tomorrow. I hope it happens.

File.Name: fa13b_fa13-019.jpg
Notes: 21530579333 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) is quite yellow except for her head. She's sitting on her egg case in a flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). I've been checking in on her every couple days waiting for the eggs to hatch. Any day now, but not yet. I'll keep checking. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

File.Name: fa13c_fa13-017.jpg
Notes: 21964359069 I believe this is a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers hanging out of its "house" - see my [Previous] two photos. Now I ask you - isn't this the same spider that I show in this photo that I first thought was a tiny Araneus spider? (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

File.Name: fa13d_fa13-022.jpg
Notes: 22161251431 This is the nest of (I believe) a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. This photo shows the proper orb web next to the cobwebby web of the spider's house. I found this one in a fading plant of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) and that's what the nest is decorated with. Compare my [Previous] photo from a different shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; California Buckwheat

File.Name: fa13e_fa13-015.jpg
Notes:
21963688609 This is the nest of (I believe) a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. They build these nests a bit away from their small orb webs, and usually decorate them with the bodies of their prey. This one has also chosen to decorate its web with seed and spent flowers of the native shrub of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) where it has it's nest. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

Look close at that dark insect prey near the top of the web. I'm sure it's an adult Froghopper (Cercopoidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) whose larvae are the Spittlebugs that make frothy "loogies" in our summer composites. Compare this photo from a similar plant.
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; Coastal Goldenbush; Froghopper; Spittlebugs

File.Name: fa13f_fa13-023.jpg
Notes: 22160584201 Our native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) trees in the Aceraceae plant family are starting to show some fall color. This is looking down into Kinevan canyon where it becomes San Jose Creek. Sometimes in these drought years the trees just go right from green to brown. I like the fall colors better! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

File.Name: fa14a_fa14-018.jpg
Notes:
21990960558 Household ecology...

I believe this is a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae that has captured (I believe) a common Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera). It's curious because we don't usually have earwigs at home, but here it is - six feet off the ground in the grasp of this spider! I got this photo on the side of our house today between two arms of a tall cactus. It has the shape and almost the size of a Black Widow in the genus Latrodectus, compare Ash's fine photo. See my [Previous] photos for more of the spider. Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2015)

We had an interesting weather day today with thunder and clouds... and just a few 1/100s inch of rain. We have a 40% chance of more rain tonight, so I'm not giving up!
CommonNames: Common House Spider; Earwig; Black Widow
ScientificName: Parasteatoda tepidariorum; Theridiidae; Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Latrodectus

File.Name: fa14b_fa13-002.jpg
Notes: 21555625194 I believe this is a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae on the side of our house between two arms of a tall cactus. It has the shape and almost the size of a Black Widow in the genus Latrodectus, compare Ash's fine photo. This one has a textured abdomen that reminds me of ice breaking up, and her spinnerets point up. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

File.Name: fa14c_fa13-003.jpg
Notes: 21991112699 This is the nest and tangled cobweb of (I believe) a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum in the spider family Theridiidae. You can see a couple of egg sacs - one at the bottom below the nest, and another higher up. I got this photo on the side of our house, between two arms of a tall cactus. See my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

File.Name: fa14d_fa14-007.jpg
Notes: 21990669269 This is a small spider hanging from it's web in the mint patch of the garden. At first I thought it might be a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) because of its flat web, but it sure looks more like a Cobweb Weaver aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) spider up close. Compare the two photos after my [Next]. They both have the same speckled abdomen that reminds me of ice breaking up, and the same shape. Looking closer, the web is not so tight as a sheetweb. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2015)
CommonNames: spider; Sheetweb Spider; Cobweb Weaver; Combfooted Spider

File.Name: fa14e_fa14-028.jpg
Notes:
21989088428 Same or different?

Here are two similar Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) on a pad of Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I'm sure they are both genus Phidippus, but are they the same species? They might be the Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni), and that might be female (left) and male (right), but I'm not sure. They also might be completely different. I got the photo on the right this morning and the photo on the left a few weeks ago. I haven't seen them in the time between. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September and 14 October 2015)

File.Name: fa14f_fa14-004.jpg
Notes: 21555599423 This is the same lovely little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I show in my [Previous] photo, probably a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). Here it is in its "spider cave" tucked back in the pads of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. You can see a bit of the spider's eyes and red abdomen here! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2015)

File.Name: fa15a_fa15-016.jpg
Notes:
22207416675 This is another Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus, the same one I've shown before in this photo and this one, probably a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). But what colors! It has a yellow Milkweed Aphid for prey, blue chelicerae, and green and reddish eyes! I know it's not quite sharp, and I had to help it with a bit of "Unsharp Mask". I got this photo this morning on a cactus pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. The spider was here one moment and gone the next. It's an impressionist photo! Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

We were in a cloud most of the day, with a near-constant rumble of thunder. Despite the clouds and the thunder, we got less than 1/2 of 1/10 inch of rain. *Sigh* This tropical storm was showery, and we weren't in the right place. I actually had to water the garden today.

File.Name: fa15b_fa15-024.jpg
Notes: 22018896938 Here are two photos of what I suspect is a different Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus. This one has long whiskers and neat white trim around its red abdomen. Compare this photo and this one. On the other hand, they might all be the same critter! I found this one today on a new flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). These aren't the best photos, but they show an interesting and beautiful spider. I hope I find this one again with less wind so I can post to the Arachtober group. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

File.Name: fa15c_fa15-012.jpg
Notes: 22216169901 The is the first mushroom I've found in the woods this season! It's growing in a thick patch of wild blackberry and bracken fern, under live oak and bay laural. I didn't climb down to pick it in my sandals, but it's surely an Agaricus mushroom. It looks a bit like a Lepiota with those radiating scales, but the eroding brown cap gives it away. I can also see a brown spore stain on that one exposed gill in the 4:00 position. It takes a mind-shift to go from insects and spiders to mushrooms, but that time seems to be coming! There are rumors of El NiÒo rains this winter, and I hope they happen. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

File.Name: fa15d_fa15-013.jpg
Notes: 21584483873 This is a nice "butt shot" of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a new flower of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. It's sticking its proboscis out as well! I was using the "Exposure Delay Mode" of my camera which raises the mirror then waits a couple of seconds before the exposure is made. The fly moved... into perfect position! This is a great camera feature to minimize blur in low light, but it makes a dark time of a couple of seconds before the photo is taken. I'm not always this lucky! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

File.Name: fa15e_fa10-004.jpg
Notes:
22018257469 This is a "butt shot" of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the lip of the flower scar of a new fruit of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I have another photo of the same spider here. It sure looks like a genus Mecaphesa spider. This one is tiny, less than 1/8 inch long! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2015)

I'm not sure if that's "spider stuff" or "cactus stuff" to the right of its hind leg. Probably cactus.

File.Name: fa15f_fa13-014.jpg
Notes:
21583898173 This is another "butt shot" of the beautiful mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg case in a flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). She's quite yellow except for her head. I've been checking in on her every couple days waiting for the eggs to hatch. Any day now, but not yet. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2015)

It's odd. I checked back today, and she's moved her egg case from the bottom to the top of the flower. That's just a few inches, but I don't know how she does it. Maybe this is a sign that a blessed event is about to happen? I'll keep checking.

File.Name: fa16a_fa16-011.jpg
Notes:
22047536270 This is a tattered Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working autumn flowers of native Longstem Buckwheat aka Wand Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I also found Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies on the native Douglas' Groundsel today. I guess the butterflies will last as long as the flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2015)

Marine layer fog moved in this morning, bringing "fog winds" with it. I drove up into the higher mountains to see what I could find. The clouds seemed to follow me up into the mountains, but I managed to say ahead of them. There were thunderheads over the backcountry, and some thunder, but no more rain.

File.Name: fa16b_fa15-018.jpg
Notes:
22245351281 This is another photo of the beautiful mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg case in a flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). There's a nice seed of the plant to the left of her head. This spider is quite yellow except for her head. I've been checking in on her every couple of days waiting for the eggs to hatch, see here, here, and here. It's a nice thing about these spiders that they stay put - more or less! The eggs will hatch any day now, but not yet. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

It's odd. When I checked back yesterday, she had moved her egg case from the bottom of the flower cluster to the top. That's just a few inches, but I don't know how she does it. Maybe this is a sign that a blessed event is about to happen? I'll keep checking.

File.Name: fa16c_fa15-015.jpg
Notes: 21613552923 This is a perfect little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a new flower of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. It has it's face and proboscis expanded! This fly looks too small for the flower, but it was climbing all over it to reach the stamens. This tiny fly perches with wings closed, which I guess makes it different from the tiny fly in my [Previous] photo. I got this photo on a gloomy morning yesterday after a splash of rain. HFDF!

File.Name: fa16d_fa14-005.jpg
Notes:
22045881650 This is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a new flower of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This plant is growing in the garden it gets regular summer water. They've almost become a weed - see this photo. This tiny fly has a white face and perches with wings open, which makes it different from the tiny fly in my [Next] photo. I got this photo on a gloomy morning two days ago after a splash of rain. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2015)

If you look close, you can see a tiny Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the flower lip below the fly's abdomen. I guess it's a nymph?

File.Name: fa16e_fa14-023.jpg
Notes:
22045780818 I found this extraordinary growth on a twig of the "scrub oak" (Quercus, Fagaceae) in my [Previous] photo - the odd oak I've been calling Quercus pacifica. I guess the growth is a gall - I can't think what else it would be! This is one that I've never seen before. It's about the size of an almond, and just as solid. Ron Russo doesn't mention this one in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), nor does Joyce Gross. Finding this odd growth made my day! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2015)

(Maybe this is the gall of Callirhytis pomiformis - see this photo at CalPhotos.)
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Callirhytis pomiformis
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Callirhytis pomiformis

File.Name: fa16f_fa14-022.jpg
Notes:
22220310922 This is an interesting Oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains that is the host for the most remarkable gall(?) in my [Next] photo. I turned the leaf below the center around, showing it's backside, and you can see that the leaves are flat and shiny underneath, without any sign of "hairy armpits" - very different from Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia). It's definitely a "black oak". (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2015)

This oak mostly grows as a "scrub oak", but occasionally it becomes a copse of small trees. It's been called many names - Quercus pacifica or Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus parvula or Quercus parvula var. shrevei or Quercus shrevei. See the discussion with this photo. I think I prefer Quercus pacifica as a name, though that one usually grows on the Channel Islands as the Island Scrub Oak. Why not? See this photo with an thin-scaled acorn. (And a weevil!)
CommonNames: Oak; Coast Live Oak; Island Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Q. agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Q. agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica

File.Name: fa17a_fa15-010.jpg
Notes:
22073517289 Here are two photos of the same tiny spider, a bit over 1/8 inch long. At first I thought it was an Orb-weaver (Araneidae), and maybe it is, but looking closer I think it might be a Long-Jawed Orb Weaver in the family Tetragnathidae. Maybe it's genus Metellina, in particular Metellina mimetoides. It has very long front legs, a humped back, and black and white bars on its abdomen. I found this one in a shrub of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. In the photo on the left, the spider is hanging on an angular stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) that grows up through the Rosemary. Arachtober 17. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2015)

No time for new photos today, so I'm just posting this one for the Arachtober group. My wife Julie has been off on an adventure with a couple of lady friends for nearly four weeks, and she just got home late this afternoon. I had some housecleaning and garden work to do today. Welcome home, Julie!
CommonNames: Orb-weaver; Long-Jawed Orb Weaver; Rosemary; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: fa18a_fa18-009.jpg
Notes:
22278628852 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) occupying a humble flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon today. I don't think this a a "preying position" - it's more of a "threaten the damn photographer" pose! If you can find flowers at the end of our dry summer, you will probably find spiders. Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2015)

I just noticed that the Arachtober group now allows two photos per day, so I'm making use of it. We were in a cloud today, and it was quite cool with some drizzle. It finally cleared out around 4:00, but the marine layer seems to be coming back this evening - with wind.

File.Name: fa18b_fa18-002.jpg
Notes:
21667688354 This is the nest of (I believe) a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. This photo shows the cobwebby web of the spider's house with proper orb web in front. These spiders decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey and bit of the plant they are on. This one is on a shrub of Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden, and it's decorated with lavender buds. I have other recent photos of these spider nests on California Buckwheat and Coastal Goldenbush, each decorated with the appropriate plants' part. These spiders build two separate webs side-by-side - a proper orb web to catch prey, and a separate cobweb to make a house. Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2015)

The nests themselves can grow quite large, an inch or more long - see this photo. I think of them like the "finger condoms" - properly called finger cots - that watchmakers and semiconductor workers wear. (I had to look up the name, but a search on "finger condom" took me right to it!)
Locality: I had to look up the name

File.Name: fa18c_fa18-007.jpg
Notes:
22102167118 Here are new late-season flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. In a better year, these will flower until the new year, but I'm happy to see these now in October in this drought year. The stems have a white-tomentose coating that usually peels away by the time they flower, but you can see a bit of it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2015)

I love the way that scientific name sounds - "cor-e-thro-gyn-e-fil-ag-in-i-fol-ia" - it kind of rolls off the tongue!

File.Name: fa18d_fa18-006.jpg
Notes: 22289477395 This is a seedhead of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family wearing a shower cap of "drizzle drops" from the moisture last night. It didn't quite rain, but everything got wet. The flowers of this shrub are almost done for the year, but there still are a few. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2015)

File.Name: fa18e_fa18-012.jpg
Notes: 22101056970 Here are ripening berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family, on a gloomy day. Not every tree has berries in this drought year, but hopefully there will be enough for the Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata). See my Christmas photo from last year, actually taken a few years ago. I've been seeing a flock of pigeons waiting for these berries and acorns to ripen! Compare my [Previous] photo of native toyon berries. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2015)

File.Name: fa18f_fa16-017.jpg
Notes: 22100775980 Here are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They are already showing some color, and will earn their other common name of Christmas Berry when the holiday season comes. The berries are edible when ripe, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2015)

File.Name: fa19a_fa19-001.jpg
Notes:
22132404619 This is a small net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. I believe it is an Issid Planthopper in the genus Neaethus of the family Issidae. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

I'm glad to see it, as these critters have been absent so far this year. I often find them in mid-summer, but not this year. Other insects that have been MIA in this drought year are the Bush Katydids (Scudderia,Orthoptera) and the large bristly Spiny Tachina Flies (Paradejeania rutiloides, Tachinidae, Diptera) that I've found for years on the Coyote Bush flowers in the canyon. I'm sure there are many more. I hope these insects can rebuild their populations after the drought.

Today started dead calm - this photo. Then there a slight breeze, enough to keep the leaves moving - my [Previous] photo. Then it was breezy - the rest of my photos today. Finally it has become downright windy. We were in a cloud this morning, and it remained partially cloudy all day. It never got out of the 60s today, which is relatively cool for us. It feels like fall.
CommonNames: Planthopper; Wild Blackberry; Issid Planthopper; Bush Katydids; Spiny Tachina Flies; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Neaethus; Issidae; Scudderia; Orthoptera; Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Neaethus; Issidae; Scudderia; Orthoptera; Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: fa19b_fa19-006.jpg
Notes:
22131044648 Predator becomes prey!

This tiny Spider has become prey for an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - probably a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). Looking close at the face of the spider, I think it might be a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae). I found this scene on a non-native mallow (Malvaceae) in the garden. It was breezy and dark when I got this photo this morning, so I had to bump up my ISO more than I like. Try sitting back from your computer screen. It's still a very cool shot! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)
CommonNames: Spider; Assassin Bug; Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Jumping Spider; mallow
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus renardii; Salticidae; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus renardii; Salticidae; Malvaceae

File.Name: fa19c_fa19-037.jpg
Notes:
22318281775 I found this different Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) on a flowering pistillate (female) shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. The colors of these spiders are remarkable - sometimes green, but often not. This one looks quite tan and red, and hardly even has a green head. Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

I did check in on the yellow-colored mama Green Lynx Spider sitting on her egg case today. I got a nice photo, but there are no spiderlings yet. I'll keep checking.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa19d_fa19-018.jpg
Notes: 21696932053 This is a small diurnal (daytime) Moth (Lepidoptera) working new pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind it is, but it has the same shape and size as a Crambid Snout Moth (Crambidae). I usually find those moths deeper in winter. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)
CommonNames: Moth; Coyote Bush; Crambid Snout Moth

File.Name: fa19e_fa19-020.jpg
Notes:
21694698334 I believe this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working a new pistillate (female) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one is less than an inch long, and it has the same "so blue it's black" colors as our giant Tarantula Hawks (genus Pepsis) which I also find on flowers. This one might be genus Aporus. I admit, I've been wrong about these wasps before! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

This wasp was not large, so it's interesting to compare the tiny wasp in my [Previous] photo. Use the pistils of the flower as your ruler. Wasps are a remarkable group of insects!
CommonNames: Spider Wasp; Coyote Bush; Tarantula Hawks
ScientificName: Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pepsis; Aporus
associatedTaxa: Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pepsis; Aporus

File.Name: fa19f_fa19-032.jpg
Notes: 22327805961 This is the succulent gall of a Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) on a pistillate (female) shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family in full flower. If you look close in the 1:00 position of the gall, you can see a tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) working the flower above it. Hah - use the pistils of the flower as a ruler and compare the wasp in my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

File.Name: fa20a_fa20-009.jpg
Notes:
22158072100 This is a beautiful little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working a still-new flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. If you can find any flowers in our autumn woods, you will probably find this butterfly! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)

It was a beautiful day today, sunny and calm. But I feel crummy. Julie brought came home from her trip with a cold, and I got it. It's not surprising - long airplane trips in a sealed cabin are just the thing to spread infections. Oh well, you can't just sit around feeling crummy! I took a walk and got a few decent photos, though I probably missed more than I found. This cold came on fast, so hopefully it will go away fast.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa20b_fa20-001.jpg
Notes: 22356200091 This is a really tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) snuggled into a little boat-like seedpod of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The spider is about 640 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, only about 2.5 mm across - really tiny! I had to estimate the pixel-size because it's abdomen is obscured. (See here for how I figured.) Arachtober 20. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: fa20c_fa20-022.jpg
Notes:
22355629161 Here is another shot of the beautiful mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) pacing around her egg case in a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). I think she's slowly turning more brown and less yellow, compare this photo. I've been checking in on her every day or two waiting for the eggs to hatch. Any day now, but not yet. I think she's getting anxious too - I watched her circle around her nest twice, like an expectant dad in the waiting room. (Do they still have those?) Arachtober 20. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)

Yeah, I've shown this spider before - but why not? She's large, beautiful, and I know where she lives! I'll keep checking in. We had one of these spiders on a buckwheat shrub by the mailboxes a few years ago. She waited and waited... until December, and finally disappeared . I hope that doesn't happen with this fine spider.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa20d_fa20-013.jpg
Notes:
22156857908 This is the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's about the size of a large grape. That little horn on top - the "beak" - is distinctive. It hasn't been a good year for oak galls, so I was happy to find this one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)

Now why would galls - the induced home of tiny wasp larvae - be so darned pretty? Doesn't that just attract the attention of predators?
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: fa20e_fa20-015.jpg
Notes:
22344165355 Here are a pair of new "oak apple galls" of California Gall Wasps (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae), about walnut-size. They'll get larger. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)

I'm really confused by the timing of these galls. Here they are in autumn, but I also find them in late spring and early summer. The wasps that induce and inhabit them are said to be known only from parthenogenic females. It hasn't been a good year for oak galls, so I was happy to find these.
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fa20f_fa20-011.jpg
Notes: 21721093264 I guess this is a rust fungus (Pucciniales or Uredinales, Basidiomycotina) on a stem and leaves of a flowering plant of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. The fungus infection seems to be doing real damage to the plant, which has smaller than usual flowers and few of them. I don't know anything about it, except here it is. Darn, the insects depend on our autumn flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2015)
ScientificName: Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa21a_fa21-005.jpg
Notes:
22185465878 This is the first of our large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae) that I've found in the woods this year, just in time for Halloween! These are the spiders with webs you don't want to walk into at night - *Where's the spider!?* It's got the right colors for a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but I couldn't get to the other side so I'm not sure. It's got a black ball of prey that I can't identify. And what a remarkable "black hole" it has on its belly - like the entrance to hell, with teeth! Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2015)

It was another lovely day, sunny and not too breezy, but I'm still feeling *blah* with a cold. Oh well, you can't just sit around feeling crummy, so I took a (short) walk today. I'm feeling a bit less "leaky" tonight, so I hope this is the end of it.
Locality: short) walk today.  I'm feeling a bit less "leaky" tonight

File.Name: fa21b_fa21-002.jpg
Notes: 22185885919 This is another shot of the Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus that's become a resident in the Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus growing in our garden. I guess it's a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). Pretty spider, and what an unlikely place to find it! Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2015)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Prickly Pear; Johnson Jumper

File.Name: fa21c_fa21-014.jpg
Notes: 22184739578 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe it's a a Flesh Fly (family Sarcophagidae) - not a lovely name, but it's a handsome fly with those red eyes and red-tipped butt. I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2015)

File.Name: fa21d_fa19-025.jpg
Notes: 21748984004 This is another bristly little fly (order Diptera), no doubt a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be genus Peleteria. This one is working pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Autumn seems to be the time for these flies in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)
ScientificName: Diptera; Tachinidae; Peleteria; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa21e_fa19-027.jpg
Notes: 22345380706 This is a bristly little fly (order Diptera) on pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It sure looks like the species Paradidyma singularis in the family of Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), compare the images at BugGuide. But that one is not reported from California except in May. Whatever it is, it's a striking fly with that pattern of stripes and bars. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

File.Name: fa21f_fa21-012.jpg
Notes:
22381434571 Blowing in the wind...

These are new seeds of female (pistillate) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The tiny seeds have a bit of dandelion-like fluff that carries them away in the breeze. Soon they will be everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2015)

File.Name: fa22a_fa22-024.jpg
Notes:
22211965019 Here are two shots of the same little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers in a nearly done shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). You can see it in its little house on the right, but it came out of its house and posed for me on the left! These little orb weavers make a cobwebby web for their house, with a proper orb web out front. (This one doesn't have much of a house yet, but they can get quite large - see this photo.) These photos look easy, but it was breezy today and I had to take *many* photos to get these two keepers. Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2015)

It was a nice enough day, but it was quite breezy today which always makes my kind of photos difficult. My cold is winding down - less leaky and more congested. I'll be almost normal tomorrow.
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; California Buckwheat

File.Name: fa22b_fa22-002.jpg
Notes: 22211428929 This is another shot of the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. I believe it's a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but I can't see it's upper side so I'm not sure. She's hunkered down in her minimalist nest amid seedpods of a plant of native California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2015)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Cat-Faced Spider; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: fa22c_fa21-007.jpg
Notes: 22384764882 This is an archnobutt shot of a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that I found in the woods yesterday. I believe it's a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but I can't see it's upper side so I'm not sure. Look how she's grasping the web strand with her prehensile spinnerets! See my photo from yesterday. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2015)

File.Name: fa22d_fa22-016.jpg
Notes:
22384481802 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably genus Eristalis. I'll post another photo of the whole fly tomorrow. This one is working pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2015)

I don't know what those little upturned flaps on its wings are.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa22e_fa19-030.jpg
Notes: 22209178210 This is the bristly butt of a little fly (order Diptera), no doubt a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. I found it on pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

File.Name: fa22f_fa22-006.jpg
Notes: 22396908045 There are still pretty flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family, but they are getting scarce. They have long stamens/pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar! The flowers close in mid-morning like real Chicory (Cichorium intybus). I love our autumn flowers - they keep the insects alive during the hardest part of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2015)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Chicory

File.Name: fa23a_fa23-020.jpg
Notes:
22410427852 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly - the first I've seen in a long time. It's not the best photo, but it is a record shot. This one is resting on a dead stem of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family, but it was more interested in the new flowering plants nearby. That's it's host plant. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail - with wings open. It's tricky following these tiny butterflies as they fly remarkably fast and close to the ground. Now that I know they're here, I get back to this place for a better shot. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)

It was another lovely day, with some interesting high clouds. I'm still feeling under the weather, but I'm clawing my way back up.
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed

File.Name: fa23b_fa19-024.jpg
Notes: 22410204992 This is another shot of a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) working pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Nice whiskers! See this photo of the same fly. Looking at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Peleteria. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

File.Name: fa23c_fa19-033.jpg
Notes:
22433956561 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Flies are so interesting and diverse! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2015)

This isn't the fly I showed yesterday. That one has a fat butt, but this one's skinny. Maybe I'll post the other one tomorrow - unless I find something better!

File.Name: fa23d_f106-022.jpg
Notes:
22235751739 I believe this pretty green Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is Diaea livens. This one is perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), where it belongs. I actually first noticed it on the back of my hand. I transfered it to the oak leaf, and it seemed happy there. See my [Previous] photo. Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2015)

I didn't find any interesting spiders today, so I'm posting a couple from the archives that I've been itching to show. I'm pretty sure I haven't put this one up before, though I've posted ones like it here and here.

Yep, I've got more from the archives I'd like to post, but it feels like cheating! I hope tomorrow is more of a spider day.

File.Name: fa23e_f121-002.jpg
Notes:
22433197371 This is the perfect husk of a spider above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods, taken last January. With that color and those legs, I guess this is the shed skin of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), probably the same Diaea livens spider that I show in my [Next] photo. I've never found this spider at home, but I guess they're here. If you look close, you can see that it has its old head (with eyes) on its butt! That must have something to do with the way it sheds its old skin. Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2015)

I didn't find any interesting spiders today, so I'm posting a couple from the archives that I've been itching to show. I'm pretty sure I haven't put this one up before, though I've posted ones like it here and here.

File.Name: fa23f_fa23-021.jpg
Notes: 22408807022 I found this galled flower head on a plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) by the river today. I'm not sure whose gall it is. Ron Russo in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) mentions a moth (Gnorimoschema crypticum, Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera) that causes "soft, fleshy stem galls" on this plant, so maybe that's it. But this looks more like a bud gall to me. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)
ScientificName: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Gnorimoschema crypticum; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Gnorimoschema crypticum; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: fa24a_fa24-007.jpg
Notes:
22459784321 This is looking straight at a beautiful little Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe genus Mecaphesa, see my [Previous] photo. I found it this morning on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's probably the last thing that many small insects have ever seen! Look at that little band of stellate hairs on the leaf - that's my depth of field. Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2015)

I'm curious which photo will get the most hits - this photo or my previous one? I think I prefer the last one, but this is more dramatic. BTW did you notice that this one is missing its right third leg? It's an occupational hazard for these little spiders that grab prey bigger than they are and hang on. I've always wondered if they will regenerate that lost limb with their next molt?

It was another lovely day, with lots of high clouds and a ring around the sun most of the day. My mom used to say that meant rain in a day or two, but nothing is in the forecast. Damn, I'm still feeling under the weather. My cold lingers.

File.Name: fa24b_fa24-006.jpg
Notes: 22448423845 This is looking down on a beautiful little Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe genus Mecaphesa. I found it this morning on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks translucent. This isn't its normal predator pose with the front legs closer together - I reckon it's trying to threaten me for getting too close with my camera lens. Just one more photo from the front and I'll leave you be! Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2015)

File.Name: fa24c_fa24-014.jpg
Notes: 22434882412 This is a different short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) from the one in my [Previous] photo. They were both on leaf clusters of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae), a few inches apart. This one looks different, darker and more blue. I wonder if this isn't an "aprËs sex" photo? (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2015)

Notes: 21825078914 This is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) lounging on a leaf cluster of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). File.Name: fa24e_fa23-017.jpg
Notes:
22447445365 This is a beautiful female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) by the river yesterday. The ladies are quite a bit darker then the males, but they both have little chevrons along their wing margins. Their host plant is Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) which grows nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)

I reckon the butterflies will last as long as the flowers do!
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Coastal Goldenbush; Chaparral Mallow

File.Name: fa24f_fa23-012.jpg

Trying to give names to the variations is like naming shapes in the clouds. This one is working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) by the river yesterday. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)

File.Name: fa25a_fa25-009.jpg
Notes:
22465594872 This is looking closer at the head end of the remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera that I show in my [Previous] photo. I found it on the web of (I guess) a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae. These insects have thread-like rear legs, but they hold their thicker and spined front legs out in front of them like an extension of their face. If you look close, you can see its eyes and even its proboscis folded under its head. These insects are a couple of inches long, but they are almost as thin as the webs they are on! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2015)

This one looks a lot like Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. The species name refers to its short wings, from the Latin: "brevis" = short, and "penna" = a feather or wing. What were you thinking of??

All of my photos today have something to do with spiders, which is appropriate for the month of Arachtober! It was overcast for most of the day, and it even felt like rain for a while. Good call on the ring around the sun, Mom! There is a slight chance of a shower on Wednesday night.

File.Name: fa25c_fa25-015.jpg
Notes:
22488950031 This is a ball of spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) in a plant of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have a question about who the mama is, see my [Previous] photo! Arachtober 25. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2015)

How many spiderlings do you reckon are in there? Hundreds at least. It's interesting to think that every pair of critters has to make just two offspring in their lifetime to keep the population stable. Good luck little spiderlings!

File.Name: fa25d_fa25-017.jpg
Notes:
21856030013 There must be a story here... I wish I knew it.

Starting at the bottom-right, here is a different mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg case on a plant of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. Sitting kitty-corner from her is a hatch of tiny Green Lynx spiderlings from a different egg case, see my [Next] photo. That different egg case is sitting just to the left of the new egg case to the right. Did the same mama make a new egg case? Did another spider move into the same place? I don't know, I'm just the photographer! Arachtober 25. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2015)

File.Name: fa25e_fa25-002.jpg
Notes: 22487679641 I almost sat on this little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) this morning! It's on the seat of the chair I keep in the garden for my close photos, but posting to the Arachtober group has me looking close for spiders, so I noticed it in time. I got the photo instead of sitting down. That saved this one! Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2015)

File.Name: fa25f_fa25-019.jpg
Notes: 22288467850 They shouldn't be out yet, but here are new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today in the mountains. They're early, I found the first new flowers a few weeks ago on October 8 - see my photo. If you look close at the lowest flower on the right, you can see a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with prey. If there are flowers, there will be insects - and spiders! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2015)

File.Name: fa26a_fa26-001.jpg
Notes:
21886777883 Here is one more photo of a remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, taken this morning. This skinny bug is on the same web of (I guess) a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae where it was yesterday. See my photos here and here for more info. "When You've Got 'em, Flaunt 'em!" (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2015)

It was warm today, in the low 80s, sunny and clear. Our chance of rain seems to have evaporated from the forecast. *Sigh*
