File.Name: fa26b_fa26-006.jpg
Notes: 21884908674 This is a tiny Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera ready to launch from a twig in the shady woods this morning. This one seems to be twitching its long proboscis. The legs of these bugs are as skinny as the Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) in my [Next] photo, but they are much smaller, maybe 1/2 inch long. The photos seem to go together though. These slender little insects are plant feeders. Pretty good photo considering the exposure! (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2015)

File.Name: fa26c_fa26-011.jpg
Notes: 22493698982 This is another photo of the same little 7-legged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I found a couple of days ago - see this photo and this one. This one is missing the third leg on its right side - pretty important as it's part of its platform. I got this photo today on the same leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) as my previous photos. I guess these little spiders don't get around much. Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2015)

File.Name: fa26d_f909-008.jpg
Notes:
22318566160 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) that is actually green! I found this one in a plant of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family back in September. In fact, this is the same plant where I found the odd scene in this photo, and maybe she's the original mom. I guess they only change color from green to yellow/brown/red in autumn when they sit on their egg cases. Arachtober 26. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 September 2015)

I've been showing so many Green Lynx Spiders lately that it seemed time to show one that was really green! Yep, it's got a scary face on its abdomen like so many of these spiders that sit on their webs all day. Now why would a spider have a face on its butt that looks scary to us humans??

File.Name: fa26e_f506-009.jpg
Notes:
22319050929 This is a spiky Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae, next to it's shed skin. I found this scene on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). Both the spider and it's husk have the same spines, so I'm sure they belong together. I guess the spider needs some quiet time after molting. I only find these spiders in the springtime. Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

I think you can also see why "the head is on the butt", which was a question in this photo. It's like taking off a wet t-shirt, it just peels back. I can't explain those cavities in the molt. It must have something to do with spider anatomy.

I've been showing so many Green Lynx Spiders lately that it seemed time to show a different lynx spider in the same family. Yeah, this photo is remarkably similar to this photo that I posted last May, but it's technically a different photo and a different crop, so I reckon it follows the Arachtober group rules - technically, at least! It is a nice shot.

Notes: 22505638515 There are still new flowers on native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family, though most plants are done for the year. This is a male staminate shrub growing in our canyon. The flowers look a lot like Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), but the foliage is different. There's an interesting little beetle on the topmost flower. File.Name: fa27a_fa27-015.jpg
Notes:
21911452523 This is something strange and wonderful! Here are a pair of tiny spiderlings caught in their first molt, surrounded by the exuvia of other spiderlings. That one on the right still has its undies around its ankles. These are spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) in a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). This shot is from the bottom of the nest. See my [Previous] two photos for more info. Arachtober 27. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2015)

The molts in the background have the same shape as those in this photo and this one.

It was a tricky day for these photos - sunny in the morning, but clouds moved in early. I used a bit of on-camera "slow flash" for this photo to brighten it up a bit. The NWS isn't giving us a chance of rain any more, que lastima!

Notes:
21911014473 This is a closer shot of the new spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) on a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). Look how they are divided by color between the top and bottom of the egg case - lighter colored on the bottom and darker on top. I believe that the spiders hatched from the bottom, are immediately doing a first molt, and then collecting on top when they are done. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos for more info. Arachtober 27. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2015)

I have another photo of green lynx spiderlings just starting to emerge from their egg case.

22542697621 We have a new clutch of tiny spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) in a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae)! I've been watching this spider all month, and fretting like a nervous dad that the hatch is overdue. See this photo, and here, here, here, and here! Those are just the photos I've posted. I've been checking in every day or two, and getting a photo or two every time, all month long.

22531263395 This is a brown male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide in a leaf cluster of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I was tempted just to forget about this photo, but I take it as my job to document the flora. Is this what global warming looks like up close?

File.Name: fa28a_fa28-001.jpg
Notes:
22372854319 This is the ornate pupal husk of an insect in the woods that I can't identify. My first thought was maybe a beetle because of its c-shape, but a robber fly pupa is a better guess. See Eric Vanbergen's comment below and the images at BugGuide. I found this one today above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2015)

It was cloudy and windy this morning, then clear and windy in the afternoon. The common thread is the wind, up to gale force, too windy for my kind of photos. It might be windy tomorrow too. That's a lot of air going *somewhere*! This was my only outdoor photo today.

File.Name: fa28b_fa28-006.jpg
Notes: 22570152091 It was too windy for my kind of photos today, so I got a photo of a little "bathroom spider" in (of course) our bathroom. At least there's no wind there! I believe its proper name is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae. These little long-legged spiders live in the corners, just about at eye level when I'm doing my business. The legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubes. Who knows what the white balance of this photo *should* be? Our bathroom has red cedar siding, and the only light was a low-wattage LED lamp. Good enough! Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2015)

File.Name: fa28c_fa27-018.jpg
Notes: 21937320423 This is a small white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding from me on a stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I found it working one of the last of the white flowers, but it took one look at me and scooted down the stem. I won't try to guess what kind it is. Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2015)

File.Name: fa28d_c409-003.jpg
Notes: 22370151290 Here's another little spider finishing its molt on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. This is from the archives (2012), but it goes with this photo I posted yesterday. I believe it's a little Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. It's still pulling free on the left, and all done on the right. It looks like something from the ocean! Soon after I got the photo on the right, the spider started twirling around on the end its web, and I don't believe the breeze was enough to do this. Maybe it was drying its new skin out? Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

File.Name: fa28e_fa23-028.jpg
Notes:
22557691075 These are new apple-size gourds of native Calabazilla aka Coyote Melon or Buffalo Gourd (Cucurbita foetidissima) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. Julie call it "stinking gourd" which is a perfect name. The species name tells how it smells - it really does stink, and it's hard to get the smell off your hands. The apple-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, medicine, containers, and rattles. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)

These native gourds are not common. I mostly find this plant on old ranch lands, this one by Rancho San Fernando Rey. This colony near Fremont Camp has been here as long as I can remember. It's quite large, maybe 50x20 feet, though it's not all one plant. (Then again, maybe it is!) I wonder if it dates back to the old days?

File.Name: fa28f_fa23-009.jpg
Notes:
22370544679 This is a new seed pod of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. The leaves smell bad, but the seed pods are wonderful to behold. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 October 2015)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: fa29a_fa29-008.jpg
Notes:
22576092042 Fire on the Mountain!
(Soundtrack - Grateful Dead, "Fire on the Mountain", here)

Here are four shots of different air tankers aka "fire bombers" flying over San Marcos Pass on their way to the Gibraltar Fire in the mountains above Montecito and Santa Barbara today. This fire is about 10 miles from home, so I'm not too worried. It now stands at about 50 acres as far as I can tell on the web, which really isn't much. But the threat of evening "sundowner winds" prompted an all-out air attack with mostly jet bombers! Clockwise from the upper-left, here are (maybe):

* CDF 14 - Lockheed P2 Neptune, props
* CDF 12 - British Aerospace BAe-146 air tanker, jet
* CDF 160 - British Aerospace BAe-146 air tanker, jet - but a different one
* CDF 101 - Erickson Aero Tanker MD-87, jet

It's interesting that so many of the planes I saw flying over today were large jet aircraft, and I didn't even see the giant DC-10 that did a drop - but there are photos here. I wish dragonflies came with ID numbers!

They hit this wildfire with remarkable air power - maybe ten air tankers and eight helicopters! That makes sense to stop a wind-driven wildfire from moving into a populated area, but the cynic in me wonders if the fire would get the same attention if it were threatening Sisquoc or Cuyama instead of wealthy Montecito. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2015)

Today started windy, but the wind backed off to a breeze before noon. The wind still hasn't kicked up in the evening. That's good news for the wildfire, but there's still a concern that it may kick up later tonight.

File.Name: fa29b_fa29-001.jpg
Notes:
21966198014 Here is a close shot of new spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) on a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). These are spiderlings of the same mama spider I've been following all month, see my [Previous] photo. It's remarkable how much the spiderlings look like their mama - except for that circle of dark spots around their chelicerae. I don't know what that is. Arachtober 29. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2015)

Today started windy, but the wind backed off to a breeze by the time I got these photos around 11:00 am. The wind still hasn't kicked up in the evening. That's good news for the Gibraltar Fire, see my [Next] photo.

File.Name: fa30a_fa30-010.jpg
Notes:
22597533016 I'm pretty sure this is another Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - compare this photo. It's another large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). It's got the same "entrance to hell" dark spot on its belly, and I did get a peek at the "horns" on its back. This one has made a large web across a downstairs doorway that we won't be using for a while! These are the large spiders with webs you don't want to walk into at night - *Where's the spider!?* I suppose I should have saved this photo for Halloween tomorrow, but I needed a spider photo today and this is what I had. I'll try to get another photo tomorrow showing the dorsal side. Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2015)

I've heard no news about the Gibraltar Fire, so I assume it's all done. They pounded this one with terrific air power - see this photo! The winds did kick up again last night, but it was pretty calm today. I hope we're all done with that.

File.Name: fa30b_f927-004.jpg
Notes: 22002198833 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Maybe it's a female Thiodina hespera with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes? It's almost translucent, with beautiful blue markings under it's eyes. I have another shot of the same spider from the side in this photo. Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2015)

File.Name: fa30c_c404-042.jpg
Notes:
21999859344 This is a remarkable little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with perfect camouflage on a twig of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I'm sure this one is genus Tmarus, probably Tmarus angulatus. I think this is the best camouflage I've ever seen - good enough that I haven't found another since I found it in 2012! Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

(Yeah, this photo is similar to this one. But it's a better crop and a different original photo, so I reckon it's legal under the Arachtober group rules - technically, at least!)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus

File.Name: fa30d_fa30-005.jpg
Notes: 22434407970 This isn't the best photo - about a full stop overexposed - but it is an interesting little fly (order Diptera)! It's tiny, not much more than 1/8 inch long. I won't try to guess what it is. I found it this morning perched on a grape leaf in the garden, mixed in with all the usual trash flies who hang out in this sunny place every morning. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2015)

File.Name: fa30e_fa22-011.jpg
Notes: 22608399812 This is a little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably genus Eristalis. This one is working pistillate flowers of a late-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo last week and never got around to posting it, so here it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2015)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa30f_fa29-016.jpg
Notes: 22621769235 These are new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The buds wait on the plant for months to flower, until next March or April. The foliage is beautiful too, with thick succulent leaves. I've heard it called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves, like hard taco shells. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2015)

File.Name: fa31a_fa31-001.jpg
Notes:
22644861332 This is one last shot of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) that I've been following all month. She looks fierce with that Halloween mask on her abdomen, but look closer at that tiny spiderling on top reaching out to touch her abdomen! They are really very attentive parents. This one is on a seed head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2015)

This is one last photo of this spider and my last for the Arachtober 2015 group. This is my first year in the group, and it's been great fun hunting for spiders to show every day. Well, I've found them most days at least. I've learned a lot too, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home! I'll start a new folder tomorrow for Arachtober 2016!

It was quite warm today, in the mid-80s. Tomorrow still sounds nice, a bit cooler. But we're in for a big change on Monday, with temps dropping almost 30 degrees from today and a good chance on rain!
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fa31b_fa31-015.jpg
Notes: 22657767585 This is a top-side view of the same large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) in the family Araneidae that I showed yesterday in this photo. I went around to the other side and opened the door to get this photo today. You can see a bit of the "cat face" on it's abdomen, but I still like this photo better! These are large spiders that can span roads and trees with their webs. You don't want to walk into a web at night - *Where's the spider!?* Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2015)

File.Name: fa31c_aa24-007.jpg
Notes:
22668094071 This is a truly scary photo for Halloween! These are prey in the mouth of a large bottle cap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae), no doubt a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides). The prey are three little Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) insects, but it beats me how the spider could catch all three at once. If you look close, the upper two hoppers look sad, like they know their fate. The bottom hopper seems to be missing its face. Did the spider suck it right off? (Can they do that??) Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2010)

I got this photo with my on-camera flash because it was so dark in the woods. I really can't remember the details because I got this one way back in 2010, but I assume it was in the woods near home. You never know what you'll find when you start searching the archives!

File.Name: fa31d_da25-038.jpg
Notes:
22656381275 This is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) under the eaves of our upstairs porch, maybe Araneus andrewsi. These awesome spiders make large 3-4 foot webs that can span roads and trees. You don't want to walk into a web at night - *Where's the spider!?* Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

We used to have lots of these spiders on our upstairs porch, but they've been slowly disappearing during our drought years. Last year I found just one, and this year none at all. I hope they can rebuild their population if the El NiÒo rains really happen this winter.

File.Name: fa31e_f823-023.jpg
Notes: 22467840810 This is an impressive Black and Yellow Garden Spider (Argiope aurantia) in the Oregon woods last summer. Make a circle with your thumb and forefinger - this spider is about that big! This one is wrapping a prey. You can see a bit of the stabilimentum of the spider's web at the bottom - those thick silk bands that zig-zag down. Arachtober 31. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2015)

File.Name: fa31f_fa31-017.jpg
Notes:
22034295103 I guess by the shape of its abdomen that this is another "bathroom spider" - maybe a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae, but maybe not. It does seem larger and less glass-like. I found this one in a downstairs doorway, not very far from the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) in my [Previous] photo. It has very long legs and a disorganized web. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2015)

Yeah, this should go to the Arachtober group, but they have a limit of 5 photos per day and this is number 6. But I do want to post spiders for Halloween!

File.Name: fb01a_fb01-016.jpg
Notes:
22072201403 Here are top and bottom shots of a pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working late-season male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The speckles on the "eyes" of its hind wing are distinctive. The only other butterfly I saw today was a skipper working the last flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa), so I was happy to find this one. It must be male with those blue upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)

We're heading into the toughest time of the year to find new photos every day. Late-season flowers and insects are almost done, and the mushrooms and mosses are still waiting for rain. I'll do my best. We do have a 60% chance of rain tomorrow morning, though there's little sign of it so far tonight. I'll believe it when I feel the drops!
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Coyote Bush; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hazardia squarrosa
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hazardia squarrosa

File.Name: fb01b_fb01-010.jpg
Notes: 22704000781 This is the mushroom-like gall of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see an exit hole in bottom of the gall where the wasp got away. This hasn't been a very good year for oak galls. Sure, galls have good and bad seasons, just like the wasps which make them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb01c_fb01-006.jpg
Notes: 22678873282 This is an unusually red gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see an exit hole in the lower-right of the gall where the wasp got away. It's about the size of a large grape. That little horn on top - the "beak" - is distinctive. This hasn't been a very good year for oak galls. Sure, galls have good and bad seasons, just like the wasps which make them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb01d_fb01-011.jpg
Notes:
22069076744 I believe this is an odd woolly gall of the Woolly Gall Wasp (Heteroecus dasydactyli) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is the first and only one of these galls that I've noticed this year, but this has not been a very good year for oak galls. Sure, galls have good and bad seasons, just like the wasps which make them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)

Heh - Donald Trump's hair??
CommonNames: Woolly Gall Wasp; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Heteroecus dasydactyli; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb01e_fb01-009.jpg
Notes: 22503691780 This is a summer-bare shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family with the sun behind, which shows off the pretty shredding bark on the stems. You can also see many new flower buds. This plant isn't flowering yet, but others are - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)

File.Name: fb01f_fa31-008.jpg
Notes: 22070057543 Here are tiny but pretty flowers and seed heads of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. You can see a translucent seed just above the flowers on the left. The flower stalks are really inferior ovaries that finally split open to release dandelion-like seeds. It's related to common wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry late-summer/autumn soil in our mountains to flower. Most of our autumn flowers are composites, so this one that is something else in interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2015)

File.Name: fb02a_fb02-004.jpg
Notes:
22105528124 This is a lovely Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae) clutching a slender stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). I found her this morning in light rain, on front of our concrete block retaining wall. I moved the stem above the wall to get a better background, and only then discovered that she was ovipositing! See my [Previous] two photos. I hope I didn't interfere too much. Did you notice that tiny red mite walking over her hind leg? (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2015)

We did get some rain today, but only about 1/10 inch. Most of the rain was to the north and west of us, but it was still good for the soul. It was rather cold today - in the 40s for most of the day. I know, that's not cold, but I'm spoiled because we just finished our warmest October ever in Santa Barbara, see this NWS page. (Look quick - I don't know how long these links last.)
ScientificName: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: fb02b_fb02-007.jpg
Notes: 22714104532 This is a Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae) clutching a slender stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). It was only when looked at this photo on my camera screen that I realized she is ovipositing - laying eggs! See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of the eggs. I found her in the pose of my [Next] photo, and had no idea what she was doing. I rearranged the stem a bit and I hope I didn't interfere too much. There was light rain as I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2015)
ScientificName: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: fb02c_fb02-009.jpg
Notes: 22701247326 Here are "just laid eggs" of a Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae)! See my [Next] two photos for the rest of the story. She's laying her eggs on a slim stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). It's interesting that the first two eggs (from the right) appear malformed. I guess it takes a couple to get started. Also compare my [Previous] photo for the wing details. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2015)
ScientificName: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: fb02d_fb01-001.jpg
Notes: 22105829203 I found this detached "butterfly wing" on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods yesterday. I assumed without thinking too much that it was the wing of a Painted Lady butterfly. But looking closer at my [Next] few photos, I'm pretty sure it's really the wing of a Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae). Very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arachnis picta; Arctiidae
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arachnis picta; Arctiidae

File.Name: fb02e_fb02-016.jpg
Notes:
22103485194 This is a large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) in the family Araneidae with a few raindrops. This is the same spider I posted the other side of in this photo. You can see a hint of the cat face on her abdomen. How delicately she hangs from her web with those tiny claws! I wonder whose disconnected leg that is on the upper-right? She rearranged her web a bit, and I can now get behind it. But a moment after I got this photo, the rain and wind kicked up, so it marked the end of my short photo day. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2015)

The month of Arachtober is over, but I can still post spider pics!

File.Name: fb02f_fb02-002.jpg
Notes: 22737166071 This is a Common House Spider (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae snug in its shelter on a rainy day. You can see a bit of its face if you look close. The month of Arachtober is over, but I can still post spider pics! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2015)

File.Name: fb03a_fb03-012.jpg
Notes:
22577665590 I found this handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe genus Mecaphesa, sprawled across a narrow leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family today. I don't know what it hopes to capture on this leaf, but flowers are scarce at this time of year, and you have to perch somewhere. The month of Arachtober is over, but I can still new post spider pics! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2015)

The wind picked up early today as our storm started to blow through. We ended up with just 0.18 inch of rain yesterday, not much. Our total for the rain year (since Sep.1) is 1.07 inches, just 61% of average. It feels like another drought year so far, but maybe the El NiÒo rains need some time to kick in. I have hopes! It's still cool, in the 40s as I write this. It feels like autumn.

File.Name: fb03b_fb03-009.jpg
Notes: 22738966736 This is a striking moth on nearly dead foliage of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. (Look close - there's some green in there!) Maybe it's an Alfalfa Looper Moth (Autographa californica, Noctuidae) with those distinctive "punk" tufts along its back. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2015)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Alfalfa Looper Moth
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Autographa californica; Noctuidae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Autographa californica; Noctuidae

File.Name: fb03c_fb03-004.jpg
Notes: 22750879812 Here's one more photo taken today of the lovely Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae) who's finished ovipositing on a slender stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). You can see her new clutch of eggs on the right and (closer) in my [Previous] photo. She must have laid exposed like this all day and night through our small rain yesterday, but she's not dead - I saw her move! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2015)
ScientificName: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: fb03d_fb03-001.jpg
Notes: 22576058520 Here is another photo of the "just laid eggs" of a Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae) on a slim stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae), taken this morning. I count 19 eggs in this clutch. Compare this photo from yesterday. It's interesting that the malformed eggs have fallen away. I made a point of getting this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can measure the eggs: 212 pixels = 0.83 mm. (See here for how I figured.) Mama moth is still there, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2015)
ScientificName: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Arachnis picta; Arctiidae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: fb03e_fb01-008.jpg
Notes: 22140773704 This is a ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. The trees are related to avocados, and it shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". The fruit taste very pungent unless they are dead ripe - when they turn black. But the nuts inside are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but not too much! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)

File.Name: fb03f_fb01-013.jpg
Notes: 22763074955 This is non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. It's an invasive weed that you should know. The stems are tough and wiry, but at least the seeds don't stick in your socks. (But see this photo - they collect in your shoes!) It's impossible to pull this grass by hand from dry ground. I reckon this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains, that spreads along trails into the backcountry. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2015)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: fb04a_fb04-006.jpg
Notes:
22780629022 This is a small Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) nicely posed on an oak stump on the edge of our garden. The stump has been here since we moved in 30+ years ago. I've only been seeing the small lizards since cooler autumn weather kicked in. I reckon it takes longer for the BIG critters to warm up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. A lizard that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2015)

It was clear but chilly today, in the 40s and 50s and dipping into the 30s at night. Unfortunately for new photos, it was also quite windy. I hope we're done with the wind tomorrow.

File.Name: fb04b_fb04-016.jpg
Notes: 22767691256 I'm not sure what kind of Wasp (Hymenoptera) this is, but I found four of them working these late-season flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) along East Camino Cielo road today. I reckon it's a Vespid Wasp in the family Vespidae, and those long antennae seem distinctive - maybe genus Vespula? These non-native weeds are good for something - they keep the insects alive as we move towards winter! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Vespidae; Vespula
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Vespidae; Vespula

File.Name: fb04c_fb04-008.jpg
Notes: 22767361306 Here is another mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg case. She still shows some green, though these large spiders tend to turn rusty-brown as they protect their nests. Her egg case is disguised by spent flowers and seeds of the native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae) plant where she lives. I wonder if she worked to disguise her nest? The month of Arachtober is over, but I can still new post spider pics! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb04d_fb04-017.jpg
Notes: 22605203370 This is a tiny gall of the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It really is tiny, only about 1/8 inch across. The wasp larva lives in that little bump in the center. Strange and wonderful! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)
ScientificName: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb04e_fb04-027.jpg
Notes:
22169876414 These are aborted acorns of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family growing in the mountains. These acorns are tiny and they should be much larger by now, see this photo and this old photo (from Fotolog 2008). (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)

I realize now that it's been quite a few years since I last got a photo of acorns on these trees, though I have found a few spiky acorn cups on the ground at home. I guess they are remnant trees from wetter times in the past that have managed to hold on against climate change. But I don't think they are reproducing much. That said, I should add that we have a nice seedling tree in the garden at home that came up on its own!

File.Name: fb04f_fb04-022.jpg
Notes:
22373913398 This is a gnarly cone, dripping with resin, of a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. It's a three-needle pine. The huge cones can weigh as much as 10 pounds, according to Wikipedia, which calls them "the largest cones of any pine tree species". Widowmakers for sure! I don't recommend camping or parking under one of these trees! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)

Many of the Coulter Pines in our mountains are dying in these drought years from (I guess) Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). Other trees nearby like this one look OK. See this photo. I need to get a more recent photo of the damage.
ScientificName: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: fb05a_fb05-034.jpg
Notes:
22424375389 These beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies are courting - and I think the lady on the left just said yes! She's wagging her tail and fluttering her wings in a way that can't mean anything but yes in butterfly lingo. A moment later they flew away. See my two [Previous] photos. The scene of this encounter is a still-flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

I don't think this is a proper "butt shot" - but the lady on the left is sure "shaking her booty" to set her wings all a'twitter. What the heck - HBBBT!

It was clear but chilly again today, and the breeze was manageable. The Santa Ynez Valley has been under a frost warning, but it's not quite that cold at home. I went to the river today and got enough photos for a couple of days.
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; yes; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; yes; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb05b_fb05-031.jpg
Notes: 22398108207 These Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies are courting! I guess that's the female in the back pretending to be interested in food, while the male get closer. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. The scene of this encounter is a still-flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's interesting that the lady (back) appears newly emerged with brighter colors while the male (front) is more ragged and dull. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb05c_fb05-033.jpg
Notes: 22827247961 These Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies are courting! I guess that's the female on the left pretending to be interested in food, while the male circles around and around. See my [Next] two photos. The scene of this encounter is a still-flowering plant of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's interesting that the lady (left) appears newly emerged with brighter colors while the male (right) is more ragged and dull. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb05d_fb05-021.jpg
Notes: 22802335172 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly perched for a moment on a dead stem of its host plant, non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail - with wings open. I reckon this one is a female with those brown wings. I saw a few of them today, but they are darn hard to get close to - tiny but wary! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed

File.Name: fb05e_fb05-015.jpg
Notes: 22423325759 I was already focused on this late-season flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) when this small Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) stuck his head out! The flower seems unusually pale, but it is one of the last of the year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

File.Name: fb05f_fb04-015.jpg
Notes: 22801775602 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Vespid Wasp (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) working late-season flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) along East Camino Cielo in the mountains. It has a face on its butt! I have a photo that shows more of the wasp here. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 November 2015)
ScientificName: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: fb06a_fb06-013.jpg
Notes:
22215363424 The is only the second mushroom that I've found in the woods this season. (See this photo for the first.) I'm sure it's an Agaricus species, but I'm not sure what kind. It smells good, and has a white cap that is not at all fibrillose. This one came up after our feeble 0.18 inch of rain a few days ago, so I reckon the mushrooms are ready to pop. It takes a mind-shift to go from insects and spiders to mushrooms, but that time seems to be coming. Now all we need is some real rain... (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2015)

Today was another lovely day, calm winds and just a bit warmer than yesterday.

File.Name: fb06b_fb06-005.jpg
Notes: 22811775446 This is another brown male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) tucked into a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have faces like little crawdads. These males have broader wings than the females. They are one of our nighttime songsters, but they've been quieter since the chilly weather settled in a few nights ago. I reckon their time is coming to an end. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2015)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb06c_fb06-002.jpg
Notes:
22445417739 This is another photo of the large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) that has taken up residence in our canyon. This shot shows a bit of its large web, which can span trees and roads! This is one spider web you don't want to walk into at night - *Where's the spider!?* I have a closer shot of that mysterious dark area on its abdomen in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2015)

Maybe that little fly in the lower-left is too small to bother with?

File.Name: fb06d_fb06-008.jpg
Notes: 22823885242 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's grooming itself. I believe this is a Flesh Fly (family Sarcophagidae) - not a lovely name, but it's a handsome fly with those red eyes and red-tipped butt. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2015)

File.Name: fb06e_fb06-004.jpg
Notes:
22444653179 This is the tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. Notice that this gall does cause a color change in the leaf surface. I guess it's a "nutrient sink". (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2015)

This is probably the smallest "detachable" gall I've found in the woods. I made a point of getting this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. It's about 335 pixels across in the original photo, which makes it 1.3 mm wide, about 0.05 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Like I said, it's tiny!

My book (Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls", UC Press, 2006) says that the galls form in late spring, but that doesn't seem right for our area. The author does add that "fresh and active galls can be found just about any time of year as you go from one region to another.
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb06f_fb05-008.jpg
Notes: 22444234669 Here are a pair of large "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I can see a couple of exit holes. They are not quite tennis ball size, but larger than ping pong balls. This particular tree down by the river usually has a heavy load of these galls down where I can reach them with my camera, but there seem to be fewer this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: fb07a_fb07-002.jpg
Notes:
22238492684 I believe (after some research) that this is a male Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus) spider in the family Theridiidae. You can see its enlarged pedipalps which show that it's a male, and the eyes and bare legs are right for the family. This spider is about 1/4 inch long. I wasn't even sure if it was alive when I found it, but I poked it and it responded. I found it on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) near home. It's curious, because I haven't seen a distinctive shiny black female for many years, when we used to have one that lived almost as a pet in a corner of our bathroom. Males and females are certainly different! I guess both have the hourglass-shaped mark under the abdomen, but this one didn't want to turn over for me. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)

See this web page to distinguish the Brown Widow (Latrodectus geometricus). This is an introduced spider which has spread into Santa Barbara county. At first, I thought this is what I had.

Today was another lovely day, calm winds and even a bit warmer than yesterday. There is a chance of rain Sunday night and Monday. Bring it on!
Locality: Latrodectus geometricus). This is an introduced spider which has spread into Santa Barbara county. At first
CommonNames: Western Black Widow; Wild Blackberry; the Brown Widow
ScientificName: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Latrodectus geometricus
associatedTaxa: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Latrodectus geometricus

File.Name: fb07b_fb07-008.jpg
Notes: 22860990445 I guess this is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working a still-new flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. If I'm right, this is the first one of these butterflies I've seen for a while. The flower is also remarkable, not least because it's here in November! These flowers have a family resemblance with their cousin Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in my [Previous] photo. Both have long stamen/pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar crystals. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Rod Wirelettuce
ScientificName: Poanes melane; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa
associatedTaxa: Poanes melane; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa

File.Name: fb07c_fb05-039.jpg
Notes: 22442431457 This is a new flower of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a weedy looking plant, but it is native, and the small flowers are remarkable up close. They have a family resemblance with their cousin Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in my [Next] photo. Both have long stamen/pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar crystals.
Locality: Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a weedy looking plant
CommonNames: Rod Wirelettuce; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea

File.Name: fb07d_fb07-012.jpg
Notes:
22238972763 These are the already-done flower heads of the first Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family to flower in our mountains. (See this photo from September7!) Most other plants of this kind are just starting to show buds or still waiting. I don't know why this one plant flowers and finishes first, but it did last year too. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)

"The slow one now will later be fast...
And the first one now will later be last,
For the times they are a-changin'.

File.Name: fb07e_fb07-016.jpg
Notes: 22846383692 This is another succulent gall of a Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) on a pistillate (female) shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you compare the flowers with this photo of a similar gall, you can see that the pistils of the flowers have faded and been replaced with the pappus hairs of the developing seeds. Soon they will be blowing in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb07f_fb05-010.jpg
Notes: 22846082542 This is a large flower of ill-smelling Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing down by the river. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. The flowers sure are beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

File.Name: fb08a_fb08-001.jpg
Notes:
22493278429 This is another photo of the (I believe) male Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus) spider in the family Theridiidae that I found yesterday. See my [Previous] shot and this photo. This time I crawled around to the other side, and got this photo showing the hourglass-shaped mark on its abdomen. What's more remarkable are those very male pedipalps twisted into little spirals! I've never noticed this before. It's also a good view of its tangled cobweb. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2015)

It was another lovely day, with a few high clouds in the afternoon. It's hard to believe there's a 60% chance of rain tonight, but that's what the NWS says. I'll believe it when I see it. (But I did dump out the rain gauge - always hopeful!)

File.Name: fb08b_fb08-003.jpg
Notes:
22466720198 This is another photo of the (I believe) male Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus) spider in the family Theridiidae that I found yesterday. See this photo. The spider is only about 1/4 inch long, but it looks much larger with those long legs. I found it this morning on the same leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) where it was yesterday. I hope it stays here for a while. It doesn't look at all like the shiny black females, but it has the right shape and it does have an hourglass-shaped mark on it's abdomen that's visible in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2015)

What an interesting "elbow" it has on its legs! I believe this is the patella that connects the femur and the tibia. Maybe all spiders have this, but I've never noticed it before.
ScientificName: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; patella
associatedTaxa: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; patella

File.Name: fb08c_fb08-007.jpg
Notes: 22492454969 This is looking close at an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family - and it's wonderful large pollen grains which look like little donuts. A few have migrated over to the stigma of the flower by insect or wind. Thus pollination happens. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2015)

File.Name: fb08d_fb08-014.jpg
Notes: 22884374345 This is one of the last flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a bit of a seed head. I'm sorry to see them go, as they are the choice of many late-season butterflies, see this photo. If you look close, you can see a couple of orange spots of the rust fungus (Pucciniales or Uredinales, Basidiomycotina) that effects these plants, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2015)
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae; Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae; Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina

File.Name: fb08e_fb08-013.jpg
Notes: 22895184991 Here is an abundance of new flowers on a shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Yeah, the white balance is a little funky, but this is pretty much what I saw in this north-facing place where the sun won't shine for a few more months. A few of these shrubs have already been flowering for a month, see this photo from September 7! Other shrubs haven't yet begun forming buds. Some of these plants will still be flowering in March or April! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2015)

File.Name: fb08f_fb07-009.jpg
Notes: 22857844826 These are no-longer-beautiful galls of Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera on a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. Compare this photo taken last spring. Now the galls look abandoned. Specialized aphids used to live inside those pockets on the leaves, but no more. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)
CommonNames: Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: fb09a_fb09-001.jpg
Notes:
22289831443 Here are raindrops on the tiny "glochid" spines of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus growing in our garden. These cacti have regular large spines that are easy to avoid, but these tiny ones are the ones that devil me. They get in and you need tweezers to get them out. Look close at that large drop on the left - it's been skewered! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2015)

Yep, we did have a bit of rain last night, but just a bit - only 0.03 inch. We have another chance of a bit more rain tonight, but I'm not holding my breath. For an El NiÒo year, this one is starting pretty slow.

File.Name: fb09b_fb09-003.jpg
Notes: 22289216763 This is a gorgeous Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera with gold on its wings and a green body. I found it catching some sun on a dead leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) this morning. It might be another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), but it looks different. Maybe it's a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus)? I think I can see a hint of two extra spines on its back. Whatever it is, it's beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus renardii; Zelus tetracanthus
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus renardii; Zelus tetracanthus

File.Name: fb09c_fb09-006.jpg
Notes: 22518026109 This is another photo of the large bottlecap-size Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides, Araneidae) that has taken up residence in our canyon. There's even a few raindrops in it's webbing from our sprinkle last night. The spider's main orb-web is off to the right. It's perched on a dead stem of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2015)
ScientificName: Araneus gemmoides; Araneidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneus gemmoides; Araneidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: fb09d_fb09-012.jpg
Notes:
22491026728 This is disturbing...

I found this native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in full flower by our mailbox today. The trees shouldn't be flowering until March or April, but here it is. Yeah, I have found "oddball trees" flowering early before, see this photo, but this seems pretty extreme. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2015)

I think the native plants are confused by the on-going drought. I've found Chaparral Currant in early September, a Bigberry Manzanita in early October, and a Holly-leaved Cherry in late October. "Something is happening, and you don't know what it is..."

(I got this photo in the wind today, so please excuse a lack of sharpness. On the other hand, the wind-blown catkins look pretty nice!)
Locality: I got this photo in the wind today

File.Name: fb09e_fb09-009.jpg
Notes:
22883029966 Here are two new plants of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. They have wonderful summer flowers that many insects like, see this photo. The plants seem to grow as true biennials, forming leaves their first year, flowers in their second year, and ... that's it. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2015)

This is not a weed! If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. It would be a shame to pull these natives by mistake. If in doubt, leave it be!

File.Name: fb09f_fb07-018.jpg
Notes: 22287056003 Here are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They are getting closer to being ripe, and will earn their other common name of Christmas Berry when the holiday season comes. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2015)

File.Name: fb10a_fb10-004.jpg
Notes:
22311198363 I believe this is a colony of Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a dead twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. The lichens have been shriveled up in our summer drought, but they have opened up a bit now. They look like something you'd see in the ocean. I guess the orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the lichenized fungus and algae. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 November 2015)

This was my only photo today, but I did my duty and got at least one. The rest of my photos today are from last Thursday. I had things to do in the Santa Ynez Valley today - including meeting with a fine old friend for coffee and talk. We meet every year or two, and it's always a pleasure.

It was really too windy for photos today anyway, as our meager storm blew through. We're under a NWS wind advisory until early tomorrow morning, but later in the day doesn't sound too bad. It's been chilly - only in the 40s with a wind chill, and no doubt heading for the 30s tonight. We haven't had frost yet, but it feels close.
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia

File.Name: fb10b_fb05-040.jpg
Notes: 22918743472 Here is an abundance of tiny flies (order Diptera) on still-fresh flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I can't ID the flies, but it's interesting that this shrub is still producing flowers as most of these plants are done for the year. I guess there's always one that's first and one that's last. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

File.Name: fb10c_fb05-044.jpg
Notes: 22310706603 This is one of the last Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). This was the only one of these butterflies that I saw last week when I got this photo, and I didn't see any on the higher mountains over the weekend. I think their time has come. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Coastal Goldenbush
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb10d_fb05-037.jpg
Notes: 22918276802 This is an uncommon galled flower head of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) by the river. Compare my [Next] photo of a normal flower. I'm not sure whose gall it is. Ron Russo in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) mentions a moth (Gnorimoschema crypticum, Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera) that causes "soft, fleshy stem galls" on this plant, so maybe that's it. But this looks more like a bud gall to me. See this photo of maybe the same gall a couple of weeks ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)
ScientificName: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Gnorimoschema crypticum; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Gnorimoschema crypticum; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: fb10e_fb05-013.jpg
Notes: 22931456705 This is a lovely unwinding flower bud of ill-smelling Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing down by the river. The flowers were a favorite of the American artist Georgia O'keeffe datura - see here. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. The flowers are large and beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

File.Name: fb10f_fb05-014.jpg
Notes: 22917832292 This is a flower group of ill-smelling Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing down by the river, showing the progression from buds to open flower to spent flower. The flowers were a favorite of the American artist Georgia O'keeffe datura - see here. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. The flowers sure are beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 November 2015)

File.Name: fb11a_fb11-018.jpg
Notes:
22336111943 Here are two photos of an inch-long Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae of the order Hymenoptera on a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) shrub. This was just about the only insect I saw on this chilly day. I wonder what it was looking for? (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

The wind went away today, and it was sunny - with an autumn chill in the air. Very nice! Maybe it will warm up a bit tomorrow?

File.Name: fb11b_fb11-022.jpg
Notes: 22538721877 These are new flower buds of a native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family just waiting for rain. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, and I believe his one is male, though I've also found buds on the female plants. Last year, the male flowers never opened - maybe a casualty of the drought. That didn't stop the ladies from making seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

File.Name: fb11c_fb11-002.jpg
Notes: 22930856736 Here's another photo of the native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family that's in full flower by our mailbox today, without the wind blowing. The trees shouldn't be flowering until March or April, but here it is in November. I reckon the native plants are confused by the on-going drought. See This photo for another example, and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

File.Name: fb11d_fb11-016.jpg
Notes:
22767725250 These are interesting galls on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They have a little "nipple" like the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (here), but they have a completely different texture. Maybe they are the galls of a Bullet Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis mamillaria)? That's another Cynipidae wasp in the order Hymenoptera which make most of our oak galls. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

I don't think I've found this gall before. I haven't seen many oak galls in this drought year, but I've found several that are new to me.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Bullet Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis mamillaria; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis mamillaria; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: fb11e_fb11-026.jpg
Notes: 22966599141 This is a galled flower of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The plant flowered last spring, so this gall has been here for some time. I'm not sure what critter is in this gall, as it's not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). (A flower hanging on to the stem way past its time is a sure sign of being a gall.) It's pretty as well as interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

File.Name: fb11f_fb11-007.jpg
Notes:
22941539442 No wonder that damn fennel is so hard to pull out!

This non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant was unearthed along East Camino Cielo road recently by a large road grader. That really is a monster root that would be hard enough to dig out with a shovel! It doesn't look anything like the edible "fennel root" you see in the market. *Sigh* It will be here forever. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2015)

File.Name: fb12a_fb12-007.jpg
Notes:
22987094051 This is a beautiful emerald-green Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) snuggling into a late-season flower of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). I believe the bee is a Sweat Bee (Halictidae), maybe a Metallic Sweat Bee (Agapostemon. It seems pretty late in the year to find bees like this, but I guess they will be out as long as there are flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)

Today was another beautiful clear day with little breeze, so I went to the river to see what I could find. It's been tough posting tonight because TCM has been showing a reconstruction of the remarkable film "In the Land of the Head Hunters" made by photographer Edward S. Curtis in 1914. It's an amazing film, part ethnography and part Hollywood (like its title). I'd like to see the whole film instead of going back and forth from my computer table.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coastal Goldenbush; Sweat Bee; Metallic Sweat Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Halictidae; Agapostemon
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Halictidae; Agapostemon

File.Name: fb12b_fb12-014.jpg
Notes: 22975677495 This is an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly perched by a "flower" of non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. The butterfly was really interested in the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae) growing next door, but it settled here after I bothered it. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name, but there's nothing toxic about it. For some reason, these butterflies are having a good year in this time of drought. I've never found one so late in the season as this. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)
CommonNames: Fatal Metalmark; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed; Coastal Goldenbush
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb12c_fb12-011.jpg
Notes: 22557229408 This is (most of) a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). It's a veteran, missing several chunks of both wings, but still getting by. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fb12d_fb12-017.jpg
Notes: 22986312631 This is a butt shot of another Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides, Asteraceae). That one I found last week was not the last! HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Coastal Goldenbush
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae
