File.Name: fb12e_fb12-003.jpg
Notes: 22582558579 This is the beautiful blue butt of a common Blow Fly (Calliphoridae, Diptera) - a trash fly. This one was perched on a grape leaf in the garden this morning. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2015)

File.Name: fb12f_fb12-034.jpg
Notes:
22786183680 I'm afraid this photo shows the slow death of a landmark oak tree. It's growing at the bottom of the Snyder Trail that starts on East Camino Cielo near Knapps Castle and ends up here by the Santa Ynez River, right along Paradise Road. It's a notable native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that has a huge burl at the base - see this photo. The tree appears to be dying, but it still has one green branch on top. Maybe the El NiÒo rains this winter will revive it - IF they appear! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)

I've been finding many dead oaks by the river, almost all right along Paradise Road. I reckon the road compromises their root systems.

File.Name: fb13a_fb13-005.jpg
Notes:
22580114837 This is a mighty Vespid Wasp (Vespidae, Hymenoptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I guess it's a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis), though this one is down by the creek and not under the front porch. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

I went down to the creek this morning with the sole purpose to get a few fly photos for "Flyday the 13th". That worked, but I also found this nice wasp perched for a moment on the same oak tree where I was photographing flies. It almost always works to just sit by a plant and wait and see what comes along.

It was warmer today with little breeze. Tomorrow might be more of the same. Then we have a good chance of another small rain on Sunday. I'm still waiting for some decent rain...
CommonNames: Vespid Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Western Paper Wasp
ScientificName: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis
associatedTaxa: Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis

File.Name: fb13b_fb13-007.jpg
Notes:
22579734178 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it's different from the flies in my [Previous] two photos. What a nice beard it has! It also has longer bristles on its abdomen. I guess that this one is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but I really don't know. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

I went down to the creek this morning with the purpose to get a few fly photos for "Flyday the 13th". It worked!

File.Name: fb13c_fb13-002.jpg
Notes: 22997960745 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a dead leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Neither its common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it's a handsome fly with those red eyes. I photographed several of these this morning. They seem especially common near the creek in our canyon. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

File.Name: fb13d_fb13-018.jpg
Notes: 22971698216 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Neither its common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it's a handsome fly with those red eyes and red-tipped butt. I photographed several of these this morning. They seem especially common near the creek in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

File.Name: fb13e_fb13-011.jpg
Notes: 22605209309 These are still-new late-season flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. In a better year, these will flower until the new year, but I'm happy to see them now in November in this drought year. A moment before, I saw a little uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly on this same flower, but the photo didn't come out. See this photo from yesterday. It's good to know they're still out there. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica

File.Name: fb13f_fb12-026.jpg
Notes:
23008098331 This is another dead native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree along Paradise Road that follows the Santa Ynez River. I reckon the drought did it in, but I have no doubt that the road had something to do with it, compromising the trees roots. I've seen quite a few of these dead trees. This one is near the second crossing, but trees near the White Rock picnic area are also dying. Damn, we need those El NiÒo rains that are promised this winter. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2015)

You can see some clumps of dead mistletoe in the branches of the tree. This seems to be the first sign that a tree is in trouble.

File.Name: fb14a_fb14-015.jpg
Notes:
22632529689 This was my only insect photo today - a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. D'oh - I increased my aperture for another photo before this and forget to set it back. I'm sure it's not the last time I'll do this! At least the bee's proboscis is sharp. A few of these shrubs have already been flowering for two months, see this photo from September 7! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

Today might have been the last perfect day for a while - almost warm, and not too breezy. We have a good chance of another "small rain" starting tonight before dawn, followed by a day or three of high winds - up to 60 MPH in the forecast. It will also be quite cold - the forecast this morning set the snow level at 2500 feet, which is us! Now they've backed off from that, but I still expect it will be cold. We'll bring some plants into the greenhouse.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: fb14b_fb14-002.jpg
Notes: 22606210178 Here are new wonderful flowers of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family - wonderful because they're still putting on a show in November! It does the soul some good to find these flowers at the very end of our season. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this hard time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

File.Name: fb14c_fb14-008.jpg
Notes: 22605691708 This is a *new* photo of a flower head of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. The real flowers are those small greenish tubes with goodies sticking out, the rest of it is red bracts. Sure, the real question is what's it doing here now, in November?? This plant is growing in a place that was weed-whacked by a Santa Barbara County fire crew last January - see . That may explain why it's still flowering - weed-whacked plants sometimes come back and flower late. Or is it genuinely early like a few other natives I've found this year? Either way, it's here now, and I found this one today! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

File.Name: fb14d_fb14-006.jpg
Notes: 22631550809 Here are still-new flowers of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. This plant is growing in a place that was weed-whacked by a Santa Barbara County fire crew last January, which may explain why it's still flowering. Most of the plants are already done for the year, but weed-whacked plants sometimes come back and flower late. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

File.Name: fb14e_fb14-021.jpg
Notes:
22401597603 "By their galls, you shall know them..."

This is another one of the interesting scrub oaks (Quercus, Fagaceae) that I've been calling Quercus pacifica for lack of a better name. It has glabrous green leaves both above and below. But I'm pretty sure that Quercus pacifica is a "white oak" with lumpy acorn cups, and this one in our mountains seems to be a "black oak" with smooth acorn cups - see this photo. This branch has a stem gall which appears to be the gall of a Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). It's a gall that is only found on black oaks. I'm pretty sure now that this oak is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei). It's quite common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

I've been see-sawing over the name of this oak for some time. I hope that this is right.
CommonNames: Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp; Shreve Oak
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Quercus pacifica; Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus pacifica; Quercus pacifica; Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei

File.Name: fb14f_fb14-009.jpg
Notes: 23033233071 This is the start of a new infestation of non-native Pampas Grass aka Jubata Grass (Cortaderia jubata or kin) in the Poaceae plant family. There's been a colony of this grass on the south-facing side of San Marcos Pass for years, but this is the first time I've seen it on the north-facing side of the pass. This plant has become a horrible weed in Big Sur, taking over whole hillsides. These plants are growing on a roadside cut along Stagecoach Road, their typical habitat. They are tough to remove when they get established like this. Shovels work, but removal may require Roundup (glyphosate) to be effective. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2015)

File.Name: fb15a_fb15-007.jpg
Notes:
23025628916 Here are raindrops on leaves of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family - with more rain falling from the sky! It's hard to capture falling rain, and I'm not sure what I did right is this photo - but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)

My theme music for today is "Oh the Wind and Rain" by Jody Stecher available here. Compare other versions here, here, and here. What a haunting old folk song this is, good for a gloomy day.

"Oh the Wind and Rain" - but we've had more wind than rain today. We had about an hour of rain this morning, less than 1/4 inch total. This wasn't the rain we need, but every little bit helps. At least it doesn't hurt. I could do without the wind that follows these storms. The NWS forecast says there might be winds of 60-80 MPH over the mountains. Yipes!

File.Name: fb15b_fb15-014.jpg
Notes: 23024712046 This plastic trash can is our rain barrel catching rain water from our roof this morning. Yeah, our rain gutters overflow, but the water is great for our small bromeliad collection now in the green house. We had about an hour of rain this morning. Less than 1/4 inch total, but it's remarkable how much comes off the roof. This is on the corner of our porch, near the "cactus corner" of our garden, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)

File.Name: fb15c_fb15-034.jpg
Notes:
22631675057 This is another remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, taken today. This one looks a lot like the species Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. The species name refers to its short wings, from the Latin: "brevis" = short, and "penna" = a feather or wing. What were you thinking of?? These true bugs look a lot like Walkingstick (Phasmida) insects, but there are differences, especially in the mouthparts. My son found this one near the top of his downstairs screen door this morning. Good eyes - these are hard to spot! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)

I found this one not far from a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) web, see this photo. I believe these bugs steal prey from spider webs. It was a rainy morning, and I wasn't expecting to find any insects today. This one was a bonus.
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Walkingstick; Cat-Faced Spider
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis; Phasmida; Araneus gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis; Phasmida; Araneus gemmoides

File.Name: fb15d_fb15-029.jpg
Notes: 22428594853 This is a woodpile with reviving lichens, and with old stems (and some new ones!) of native Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus, Poaceae) grass. It remarkable how quickly these lichens revive after even a small rain like we had this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)

File.Name: fb15e_fb15-019.jpg
Notes: 23060678811 This is a fading plant of native Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden, in the rain today. Most of our native ferns wake up with winter rains, but these ferns go down. There's also Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) and Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae) in here. Yeah, we have that kind of garden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)
CommonNames: Western Bracken Fern; Wild Blackberry; Giant Rye Grass
ScientificName: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae

File.Name: fb15f_fb15-017.jpg
Notes: 22630659858 This is a flower of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the edge of our garden today - with raindrops! If you look close, you can see a drop perfectly balanced between its two stamens. It was still raining a bit when I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2015)

File.Name: fb16a_fb16-014.jpg
Notes:
22456835233 This is a small Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) nicely posed on a sandstone outcrop with small shriveled Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants. They look dead, but they will revive when we get some *real* rain, more than the 1/4 inch we got yesterday. I'm not sure what kind of liverworts they are, but I've had Targiona hypophylla suggested for a name. They dry out very quickly, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

It was very windy this morning as our small storm passed through, but the wind died down in the afternoon. It's also been quite chilly - in the mid-30s last night, and never out of the 40s today. No frost yet, but it feels close. Tomorrow is expected to be a bit warmer and less windy.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Liverwort

File.Name: fb16b_fb16-005.jpg
Notes:
22659421058 This is another shot of the remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera near the top of the downstairs screen door this morning. This one looks a lot like the species Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. The species name refers to its short wings, from the Latin: "brevis" = short, and "penna" = a feather or wing. What were you thinking of?? These true bugs look a lot like Walkingstick (Phasmida) insects, but there are differences, especially in the mouthparts. I found this one not far from a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) web, see this photo. I believe these bugs steal prey from spider webs. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

It was windy enough today to bother my extended tripod as I tried to get this photo. I took many photos to get this keeper. I tried to get a closer shot of its head, but they're not good enough. I'll try again tomorrow now that the wind has eased up.
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Walkingstick; Cat-Faced Spider
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis; Phasmida; Araneus gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis; Phasmida; Araneus gemmoides

File.Name: fb16c_fb16-022.jpg
Notes:
23051095476 This is another native shrub flowering before its time - a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I got this photo with my long lens through the upstairs window. I can think of two reasons why this shrub is flowering now instead of February or March when it should be flowering. First, the shrub is in our garden where it gets pretty regular summer water. Second, the stem seems to be galled, perhaps by a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella) in the order Lepidoptera. Maybe the gall interfered with the plant's internal clock? See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

I've found a few more native shrubs and trees flowering way too early this year - Coast Live Oak, Holly-leaved Cherry, Bigberry Manzanita, and Chaparral Currant.
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Lepidoptera

File.Name: fb16d_fb16-001.jpg
Notes:
23087741841 It was very windy this morning as our small storm passed through! Here the wind is whipping around the leaves of the native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I see from our upstairs porch. You can't photograph the wind, only something in the wind! We had a huge amount of air moving past today doing *somewhere*. Were under a high wind warning until midnight tonight, but the worst seems to have passed. I have hopes for tomorrow as a photo day. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

Wouldn't it be interesting if we could see a false-color photo showing air-speed. I think we would see ever-changing narrow currents of wind winding through the trees like a braided river. It's interesting to try to visualize.

File.Name: fb16e_fb16-017.jpg
Notes: 22683963399 These are seed pods with seeds on a shrub of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family growing in our canyon. *Sigh* I think the plant has finally given into the drought and died. Other plants are showing some green buds, but this plant is gray and the stems are brittle. This photo from last year might be the same plant. At least it left behind some potential progeny. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

File.Name: fb16f_fb16-009.jpg
Notes: 22683425819 Here is a white-barked Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family showing some fall color in our canyon. The trees are not being particularly brilliant this year. Like the maples, many trees are going straight from green to brown. I reckon this has something to do with our on-going drought. There is still beauty here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

File.Name: fb17a_fb17-001.jpg
Notes:
22686156797 This isn't quite a head shot, but it is looking closer at the remarkable Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera that's taken up residence near the top of the downstairs screen door. This one looks a lot like the species Emesaya brevipennis, but the photos at BugGuide are mostly East Coast, so I'm not sure. I got this photo this morning in deep shade. It shows the bug's head as well as it's spiky front legs and a bit more of its thorax. This critter was near vertical on the top of the door screen, so I raised my tripod arm as high as I could and tilted my camera - with some shutter-delay to absorb the vibration of the tripod. You can see a band of dust on the screen across the photo - that's my depth of field! Sure, I took many shots to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2015)

Today was lovely, a calm morning with just the usual afternoon breeze to deal with. It was still chilly, but a bit warmer than its been. The rest of my photos today show some fall color starting to happen near the river.
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Emesaya brevipennis

File.Name: fb17b_fb17-009.jpg
Notes: 23115491281 Here are new seed pods on a white-barked Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family. This is the same tree I show in my [Previous] photo, showing fall color foliage. I don't actually see the seed pods very often in our mountains. They are like little catkins strung with one-inch diameter balls. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2015)

File.Name: fb17c_fb17-004.jpg
Notes: 23090208962 These are beautiful fall color leaves of a white-barked native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family. Beautiful, but better from a distance. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 November 2015)

File.Name: fb17d_fb17-033.jpg
Notes: 22685037678 These are fall color leaves of a Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family catching a beam of sunlight, with seeds. The foliage looks a lot like Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae), but the leaves are "cleaner" without the usual sycamore fuzz. See my [Next] two photos. I got this shot today looking into the sun, with my hand shading the lens, in front of Cold Springs Tavern. There's just a trickle of water in the creek. I've never seen it so low. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2015)
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: fb17e_fb17-028.jpg
Notes: 23077180276 The trees of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii, Salicaceae) by the river are showing some fall color. Beautiful, but better from a distance. You can see a bit of a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) tree on the right. Also beautiful, but the colors are different, as well as the leaves. Maybe the chilly weather lately has helped the develop color? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 November 2015)
ScientificName: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: fb17f_fb17-014.jpg
Notes: 22710773179 The trees of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii, Salicaceae) by the river are starting to show some fall color. Beautiful, but better from a distance. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 November 2015)

File.Name: fb18a_fb18-030.jpg
Notes:
22507413534 This is a handsome Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today. This one shrub is particularly early-flowering, since October 25 at least, see this photo. It just had a few flowers at first, but now it has lots. I've found a few early flowers or buds on other shrubs as well. I even heard a hummingbird visiting the shrub today! These early-flowering shrubs are important for many different critters. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)

It was a bit warmer today, but also rather breezy. "I don't mind the weather so the wind don't blow" - as the old song goes.

File.Name: fb18b_fb18-038.jpg
Notes: 23115797042 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today. This one shrub is particularly early-flowering, since October 25 at least, see this photo. It just had a few flowers at first, but now it has lots. I've found a few early flowers or buds on other shrubs as well. I even heard a hummingbird visiting the shrub today! These early-flowering shrubs are important for many different critters. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)

File.Name: fb18c_fb18-027.jpg
Notes: 22809249240 This is a fresh-looking Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today. This one shrub is particularly early-flowering, since October 25 at least, see this photo. It just had a few flowers at first, but now it has lots. I've found a few early flowers or buds on other shrubs as well. I even heard a hummingbird visiting the shrub today! These early-flowering shrubs are important for many different critters. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)

Notes: 22709506787 This is another native shrub that shouldn't be flowering yet - but here it is. This is Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. File.Name: fb19a_fb19-001.jpg
Notes:
22765133379 This isn't the best photo or the best butterfly, but what the hay - it's a butterfly in November! I feel like I should document them all.

This is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly on a native flower of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). Apparently these butterflies are not native to North America, but they do have their charms - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2015)

Today was a beautiful photo-friendly day with calm winds and warm temperatures, in the low 70s, but I didn't get many new photos. I reckon I'm getting distracted with our upcoming trip to Oregon. So much to do!
CommonNames: Cabbage White; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: fb19b_fb19-003.jpg
Notes: 23168743491 Here are very glandular new flowers on a shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Even the flower petals have little pin-head glands, like cloves on a baked ham. These were the first new flowers of the season to appear this year - see this photo from September7! I've shown them before, but they are very beautiful, and no doubt I'll show then again! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2015)

File.Name: fb19c_fb19-013.jpg
Notes:
22836281840 This is a large gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Look at all the exit holes on this gall - it's a regular gall hotel! The gall is smaller than a tennis ball, maybe the size of a tangerine or golf ball. I've found very few of these in this drought year, and I suppose this might be an old gall from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2015)

These galls look superficially similar to those of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in my [Previous] photo, but they are made by different critters with different lives on different oaks. They also tend to have a more interesting texture. These galls are made by fully sexual wasps, and they go through alternate generations that look completely different - see this photo. The galls of the California Gall Wasp are apparently completely parthenogenetic. Neither males nor an alternate generation is known.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus

File.Name: fb19d_fb17-012.jpg
Notes:
22737633527 This is a cluster of large "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a small Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The same gall wasps make galls on the Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia) in the mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 November 2015)

The galls look superficially similar to those of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in my [Next] photo, but they are made by different critters with different lives on different oaks. These galls are made by wasps that are apparently completely parthenogenetic. Neither males nor an alternate generation is known. The Live Oak galls have both.

I can't see a stinger. These wasps look ferocious, but they've never bothered me. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

Notes: 23131168655 This is another beautiful butt shot of a fly (order Diptera) about to say goodbye on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might well be the same fly I showed in this photo last week, probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. The red eyes and red-tipped butt of these flies seems distinctive. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2015)

File.Name: fb20a_fb20-001.jpg
Notes:
22535193774 This is looking close at a large Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) perched on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree this morning. You can see the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes and the "stitch-face" bristles on its head. Yeah, I've been showing lots of these flies lately, but they're what I've been finding, and I try to do these posts day-by-day. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2015)

Today was another beautiful photo-friendly day with calm winds and warm temperatures, in the mid 70s, but I didn't get many new photos. I reckon I'm getting distracted with our upcoming trip to Oregon - not to mention that TCM has just started my all-time favorite movie "The Quiet Man"!

File.Name: fb20b_fb20-007.jpg
Notes: 22536428573 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Neither its common name nor scientific name are lovely, but it's a handsome large fly with those red eyes and red-tipped butt. (See this photo<.) Photographing flies is always tricky, but these flies seem to stand their ground when I get close with my camera. That helps! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2015)

File.Name: fb20c_fb18-007.jpg
Notes: 22769164637 This is another shot of the golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). See this photo. Beautiful fly and flower! You can see the "spurious vein" on its upper wing if you know what to look for. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: fb20d_fb18-015.jpg
Notes: 22534521394 Here are blushing new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road. Most of the flowers are still closed with a little flap over the end, but the one at the bottom is fully open. This one shrub is particularly early-flowering, since October 25 at least, see this photo. It just had a few flowers at first, but now it has lots. I've found a few early flowers or buds on other shrubs as well. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)

File.Name: fb20e_fb19-015.jpg
Notes:
22533856634 This is native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family showing some fall color. This shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers (here), edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2015)

Actually every winter-deciduous shrub in our mountains is a stumper. After all, winter is when our rains come and plants should start to grow. It makes sense for river trees like Willows and Sycamores and Maples to be deciduous - foliage would drag them downstream, and they get summer water anyway. We also have summer-deciduous plants like Deerweed and Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry - that makes sense too, going dormant during the harsh time of year. But winter-deciduous shrubs are all interesting. A few off the top of my head are Poison Oak, Elderberry, Foothill Ash, Thimbleberry, and False Indigo. I know that some of these (at least) have a more tropical connection, but I'm not sure what that explains.
Locality: at least) have a more tropical connection

File.Name: fb20f_fb16-019.jpg
Notes:
22535038333 These are galled flowers - not berries- on a shrub of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See this photo that I got last spring. The berries are long gone. That odd "crook in the neck" of the gall in the back is typical. I got a fresh photo today, but I like this one from a few days ago better. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2015)

Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it's probably the ovary of a flower.)

File.Name: fb21a_fb21-002.jpg
Notes:
23192663095 I wasn't expecting to get any new photos today, but this little spider crawled on to a page in my son's sketchbook and died there. Sure, he sketched it! It looks a lot like this photo at BugGuide of a Steatoda nobilis spider in the Theridiidae family of Cobweb Spiders, known as a Noble False Widow spider. If so, it's interesting because it's apparently a recent introduction, first found in the U.S. at Port Hueneme in Ventura county just to the south of us in 2011. I can tell from another photo that this is a male spider with enlarged pedipalps. I'm not sure of this ID of course. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2015)

Our official plan is still to leave for Oregon tomorrow morning, but maybe we'll delay for a couple of days. There's a cold winter storm moving in on Monday, and even if we get to our cabin, it won't be very pleasant. My vote is that we wait until Tuesday to leave. Julie's still down in Santa Barbara, so I'll wait to decide.
-------
Julie finally did get home. We'll leave on Tuesday. That gives me a couple more days to post photos. The good news is that I'm already packed and almost organized.

File.Name: fb21b_fb18-031.jpg
Notes: 22896162410 This is another photo of the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road a few of days ago. This will have to stand in for the fresher looking butterfly I saw in our canyon yesterday. I tried and tried to get a photo of it, but it seemed to sense when I was looking at it! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 November 2015)

Notes: 22895290520 These are new berries on a stem of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for some discussion of the plant. These berries actually look pretty sad in this drought year - small and dark. I wonder if they have fertile seed? Notes: 22894671160 These are the pretty twining stems of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The leaves of this one are pretty narrow, so maybe it has some influence from the narrow-leaved Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in this photo and this one? I notice that this one has glabrous stems while the other's are pubescent. This plant usually grows as a stand-alone shrub, but it does have vining tendencies. File.Name: fb21f_fb17-025.jpg
Notes: 23164131156 This is the fabulous foliage of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family on a dry hillside above the (equally dry) Santa Ynez River. It is probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus), but I'm not sure. The plants seem to lose their pretty woolly-white leaves when they flower in early spring, compare this photo of what I'm pretty sure is the same species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 November 2015)

File.Name: fb22a_fb22-021.jpg
Notes:
22602799883 This is a different Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly perched for just a moment on the trunk of a small tree of native Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). I believe those are also old stems of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) in the photo. I went through my photo archives to find when I've photographed them before - 5 photos from March to July, and 16 photos from September to January. This seems to be their time in fall and winter, at least here in the mountains. They are beautiful, with a target-like shape. There seems to be a hint of iridescence in it's forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

It was another lovely day for getting photos, warm and calm - really too lovely for November! I wish there were more insects to photograph, but this was almost the only critter I found today. No problem, that forces me to be a naturalist and find other things to photograph! We weren't really ready to leave for Oregon yesterday, so we'll leave on Tuesday - and hopefully miss the worst of the cold winter weather that's expected.

File.Name: fb22b_fb22-014.jpg
Notes: 22602141283 Here are golden drops on the underside of a new (?) leaf of native Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. Similar drops on it's acorns give this interesting oak it's other common name Goldencup Oak. It's a confusing oak because most other leaves don't have the gold drops, and some are smooth edged while others are toothed. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

File.Name: fb22c_fb22-023.jpg
Notes: 22860762029 This swelling is an integral stem gall on a twig of native Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess it's the gall of a Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The leaves of this oak are confusing - sometimes toothed and smooth smooth. It looks quite different from our other native oaks, but it also has galls - sometimes, like this one, different galls. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

File.Name: fb22d_fb22-016.jpg
Notes:
23228222185 Hard passing...

This is some critter's bathroom in the woods. Note all the pits of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae)! Passing through an animal is sometimes just the thing to force native seeds and berries to sprout. The turds are coyote-size, but I've never seen or heard coyotes up here in the mountains. This pile might be pretty old, as I last saw the ripe cherries last July - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

File.Name: fb22e_fb22-038.jpg
Notes:
22832994167 Here are old leaves and new buds of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The leaves are mostly gone now, but these are still showing some fall color. These plants are true winter-deciduous shrubs in our mountains, and I think that every one is a stumper. After all, winter is when our rains come and plants should start to grow! See my comments on this photo for some discussion of this. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

The 3-part leaves look a lot like their cousin Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum), but the leaves are dull not shiny, the flowers are different, and the middle leaf grows down to the base without a separate stalk. You can impress your friends by calmly handling this plant with no ill effect - IF you get the ID right!
CommonNames: Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush; Poison Oak

File.Name: fb22f_fb22-010.jpg
Notes: 22599669513 This is a galled leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's a midrib gall, and this one looks ripe with some critter's larva. I'm not sure whose gall it is. When I try a Google Search on "midrib leaf gall Ceanothus spinosus" I mostly get links to my photos! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

File.Name: fb23a_fb23-010.jpg
Notes:
23262394455 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. In a blink it was gone. I won't try to guess what kind it is. It was breezy this morning, but this photo came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2015)

It was breezy today and about 10 degrees cooler than yesterday. That may be the first sign of a change in the weather, with cold and some rain coming. Despite that, we're heading up to Oregon tomorrow - driving into the storm, and hopefully driving past it as we get to Mt. Shasta and the Siskiyou Summit. Sure, we have chains and a shovel, but I'd rather not fight the snow if it comes to that. We'll be back in a couple of weeks, hopefully with some photos of mushrooms - not frozen ones!

File.Name: fb23b_fb23-003.jpg
Notes: 22966028750 I believe this is a colony of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a number of the actual aphids, mostly to the right of the woolly mass in the center. You can also see odd white growths in the wool that might be egg masses? This is another one that's hard not to over-expose. I got this photo from our upstairs porch this morning. Yep, the tree is that close! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2015)

File.Name: fb23d_fb23-005.jpg

File.Name: fb23f_fb22-034.jpg
Notes: 22633014303 I don't know whose galls these are, but I call them "Lumpy Galls" because of their shape. They are "integral stem galls" on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They usually cause a bend in the stem, and sometimes kill the leaves beyond - though these leaves look fine. I have more photos of these galls here and here. This appears to be a fresh gall. We had an infestation of these galls a few years ago, but I haven't seen many new ones since then. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2015)

File.Name: fc03a_fc03-005.jpg
Notes:
22878142844 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best - especially when it's a beautiful bug butt shot on a Thursday!

This is a "butt shot" of a small net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. It seems late in the year to be finding these, but here it is. I believe this is an Issid Planthopper in the genus Neaethus of the family Issidae. See my [Previous] photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2015)

We came back a few days early from our Thanksgiving trip to Oregon. It was very cold the whole time we were there with lows around 25∞ and highs around 35∞, there were few mushrooms to photograph, a week of rain and snow was coming, and Julie had to get home. Our Vermont Castings Vigilant wood stove kept us warm in the cabin. All of our family - kids and grandkids - were there for Thanksgiving dinner. I give my thanks!

File.Name: fc03c_fb30-079.jpg
Notes:
23479366166 This is a cluster of Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms growing from under fallen Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae) leaves in the Oregon woods. They are the only such mushrooms I found this year - after Thanksgiving, and not enough to harvest in any case. Compare this photo from last year! They are absolutely delicious when there are enough of them to collect. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

A funny thing happened in the Oregon woods last week - there were very few mushrooms to be found. We've always collected mushrooms for our family Thanksgiving feast, but not this year. The woods seemed quite dry - places that are usually pools of water were dry. And it was very cold all week, with lows around 25∞ and highs around 35∞. I hope the mushrooms are still coming later. Despite the lack of mushrooms, all of the family - kids and grandkids- were there for our Thanksgiving dinner. I give my thanks!
CommonNames: Black Chanterelle; Horn of Plenty; Bigleaf Maple

File.Name: fc03d_fb29-055.jpg
Notes: 22877933683 This is a fantastic Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) in the Oregon woods last week. If you look close, you can see that this one is just thawing from being frozen. It's been cold! These mushrooms are easy to recognize and hard to identify, and I won't try to guess what kind it is. It looks like it belongs under the ocean. They are usually pretty common at this time of year, but I was happy to find this single specimen this year when mushrooms are scarce. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

File.Name: fc03e_fb30-080.jpg
Notes: 23136708689 I believe these are odd Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushrooms growing under (mostly) conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet, but I haven't tried them. They look a bit like narrow Chanterelles, but they are related to the Coral mushrooms in my [Next] photo. I only found a few this year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

File.Name: fc03f_fb27-012.jpg
Notes: 23136480669 These are frozen mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. I'm not sure what kind they are growing from the fallen log of a conifer, maybe genus Tricholoma with those notched gills? I think the ice crystals on the caps are the highlight of this photo. It was sunny but very cold last week in Oregon - lows around 25∞ and highs around 35∞. It felt downright balmy getting home last night with temps in the 50s! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2015)

File.Name: fc04a_fc04-032.jpg
Notes:
Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a tiny Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road. The beetle is picking up some of the blushing red colors of the new flowers. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. It's about 900 pixels across in the original photo, which makes it 3.5 mm long, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Like I said, it's tiny - also rather cute! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 December 2015)

I still have lots of Oregon photos to post - despite the lack of mushrooms this year. But I'm still getting out with my camera every day looking for new photos. I'm retired now, and that's what I do. We actually had a trace of rain last night - not enough to measure, but enough to get my seat cushions wet. *Sigh* We stand at 1.33 inches of rain this season, just 24% of normal. I hope the much-hyped El NiÒo rains finally come.

File.Name: fc04b_fc04-022.jpg
Notes:
Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I guess this is a Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) in the Chrysomelidae family of the order Coleoptera, though it seems greener than most. It's a common garden pest, but it also shows up in the woods. This one is on a spent flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road. This is the same early-flowering shrub I've been showing - see this photo from October 25! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 December 2015)
CommonNames: Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: fc04c_fb30-074.jpg
Notes: This is a group of small mushrooms on a stump in the Oregon woods. They seem to be dancing around that central nub with squamulose thalli of a Cladonia lichen. I'm never sure with fungi, but these mushrooms look a bit like the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis aka Craterellus tubaeformis). If so, they are NOT the chanterelles we were looking for this Thanksgiving! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

File.Name: fc04d_fb28-020.jpg
Notes: This is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing on a fallen conifer in the Oregon woods. There are lots of these, so I won't try to guess what these are. Is that a hint of a pink spore color on the top-most caps? You can see a bit of ice on one of these mushrooms to the right of center. It's been cold! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

File.Name: fc04e_fb28-038.jpg
Notes: These are neat little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family. It's a family shot, with unopened caps on the left, an open cap with tiny "eggs" inside in the middle, and an empty cap on the right. The eggs are really spore sacs called peridioles. These are growing on a fallen branch in a conifer woodland. The mushrooms are "splash cups" - raindrops knock the eggs out of the nest to scatter the spores. The empty cups persist for some time. I'm not sure what that root-like thing on the left is, but maybe a root? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

File.Name: fc04f_fb29-052.jpg
Notes: This is a pair of Cup Fungi (Pezizaceae or kin) growing from moss in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure what kind they are, so I'll leave it at that. All mushrooms were scarce this year, maybe due to the combination of drought and cold, so I was happy to photograph any fungi! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

File.Name: fc05a_fc05-005.jpg
Notes:
23525753116 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I found this handsome "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) this morning. It looks a lot like this photo of Banasa dimidiata at BugGuide. It's quite pretty, and it's neat how it has its left wing open showing the half-sclerotized front wing and fully membranous hind wing. This critter seems to have a wound on it's left shoulder, but it was gone when I came back after a couple of hours, so It's still getting around. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2015)

It was mostly cloudy today, and it feels a bit like rain. But it probably won't get down to us. Point Conception has been a real barrier to our small storms so far this year.
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Banasa dimidiata
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Banasa dimidiata

File.Name: fc05b_fc05-009.jpg
Notes:
23255805600 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

I don't have to go to Oregon to find interesting lichens! Here is a micro-world of different lichens growing on a fallen twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) at home yesterday morning after we had a trace of rain to refresh them. I won't try to guess what they are - or even how many there are. I do hope to learn our native lichens this season, armed with Stephen Sharnoff's "Field Guide to California Lichens" and the author's web page. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2015)

File.Name: fc05c_fb30-071.jpg
Notes: 23182713929 These are a "British Soldiers" type of lichen in the Oregon woods - probably the Toy Soldiers Lichen (Cladonia bellidiflora) with "squamules on the podetia". That means that the stalks are shaggy. The red caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen symbiosis between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. Cladonia lichens are interesting because they have a foliose or "squamulose" base, but they produce fruticose stalks called podetia. These are quite beautiful. They are growing on an old stump. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

File.Name: fc05d_fb27-005.jpg
Notes: 23524251996 I believe this is the Lung Lobaria aka Lettuce Lichen (Lobaria pulmonaria or Lobaria oregana) lichen in the Oregon woods. I think "dragon skin lichen" would be a good name for it! It should be growing up in the trees, but I found this piece on the ground on a fallen branch. This is a three-membered lichen symbiosis of an ascomycete fungus and a green algal partner and blue-green cyanobacteria. The cyanobacteria can fix atmospheric nitrogen, enhancing nutrient availability for the lichen and enriching the forest when it falls.
Locality: Lobaria pulmonaria or Lobaria oregana) lichen in the Oregon woods. I think "dragon skin lichen" would be a good name for it! It should be growing up in the trees

File.Name: fc05e_fb29-057.jpg
Notes: 22922885963 This is some kind of Tube Lichen (genus Hypogymnia) growing on a winter-bare Hazel (Corylus cornuta, Betulaceae) shrub in the Oregon woods. You can see some apothecia at the top of the lichen. The fronds of this lichen are too contrasty to photograph in the bright sun, but there I was. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

File.Name: fc05f_fb30-068.jpg
Notes: 22921376684 This is a crustose lichen on a trunk of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii, Pinaceae) in the Oregon woods. Maybe it's a Frosted Wart Lichen ( Pertusaria subambigens)? These lichens seem to make the white bands on the fir trees that I always thought were part of the tree - until I looked closer. I believe the "warts" are modified apothecia, the fruiting bodies of the fungi that make up the lichen along with algae. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

File.Name: fc06a_fc06-015.jpg
Notes:
23465650322 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is the gorgeous gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They come in many different colors between red and green, and usually have spots on their surface. It's about the size of a grape. There's a single wasp larva inside this "monothalamous" gall, which should emerge soon as an adult. Or can the wasps skip a bad year? There's another tiny leaf gall visible just to the left above the big gall. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2015)

It was a lovely day today - cloudy, though the sun did come out at times. It's been a struggle to concentrate on my comments tonight, as TCM is showing "The Twelve Chairs" (1970) directed by Mel Brooks. I've been listening to the sound around the corner, and now I can get to the film!
Locality: 1970) directed by Mel Brooks.  I've been listening to the sound around the corner
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fc06b_fc05-010.jpg
Notes:
22946852183 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

Look on the stem below the leaves to see a new gall on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks just like the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera - but it sure doesn't seem to be growing on a bud. Compare this photo. This is the first one I've noticed this year, and this is the time of year when I've found them in the past. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2015)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: fc06c_fb28-032.jpg
Notes: 22946618603 This is the empty shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails in the lowlands back home. The shell looks almost translucent. I posed it on these mosses to bring it into a patch of sunlight in the dark woods. I didn't see any live snails or slugs in the woods on this trip. Maybe it was too cold? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

File.Name: fc06d_fb28-025.jpg
Notes: 23464667982 This is an unusual fern that I've only noticed in this one place in the Oregon woods, and I find it every visit. I guess that it's a Leathery Grape Fern (Botrychium multifidum) in the Opioglossaceae plant family. It has a basal compound leaves and a flowering scape with spore-bearing sporangia rising separately from the ground. This one seems to have fallen over, but it's still ripening it's "fern seeds". The leaves look more like parsley than a typical fern. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

File.Name: fc06e_fb27-019.jpg
Notes: 23277199410 Here are neat rows of ripe sporangia on leaflets of Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum, Dryopteridaceae) in the Oregon woods. This is by far the most common fern in the woods, growing quite large - three to six feet tall. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2015)

File.Name: fc06f_fb29-062.jpg
Notes:
23464225602 I believe this is the sexual stage of a Moss (Bryophyta) in the Oregon woods. Instead up growing upward to make a sporophyte, these mosses are making little "splash cups" to spread their little sperm or eggs. They will mate to produce new plants that will make upward-growing asexual sporophtes to start the whole process over again. See my [Previous] photo. Life is complicated! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

*Sigh* It's been way too many years since I took a biology class. One of these years, I want to learn more about mosses - but after lichens!

File.Name: fc07a_fc07-030.jpg
Notes:
23302918520 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best! This goes for my [Previous] two photos as well.

This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly working a still-new flower of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. The butterfly is on the flower, but I reckon it was more interested in the non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae) plants nearby - its host plant. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail - with wings open. I reckon this one is a female with those brown wings. I had to stalk these butterflies for 1/2 hour to get this photo. They are very wary! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 December 2015)

It was another lovely almost-winter day, warm with just a bit of breeze. This is getting boring to report. I'm ready for some real weather, but I guess I can't complain too much about butterflies in December!
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Coastal Goldenbush; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed
ScientificName: Brephidium exile; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Brephidium exile; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae

File.Name: fc07b_fc07-016.jpg
Notes: 23515693871 This is another plant that is flowering way before its time - native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. "Flowering before its time" - both by the calendar and the clock. These flowers are still looking sharp at 11:00 am, but they should open in the afternoon and close in the morning before this. I expect to find these plants in February and later, but here it is in December. And it's not just one plant, but many of them in this place along Paradise Road along the Santa Ynez River. The native plants are confused by the drought and warm weather we've been having this year. The problem is that the flowers are appearing way before their pollinators, like the pretty micro-moth in this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 December 2015)

File.Name: fc07c_fc07-006.jpg
Notes: 23515515491 This is an unusually pretty tree of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family showing fall colors. These trees usually just turn brown, but this tree along Paradise Road is putting on a show. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 December 2015)

File.Name: fc07d_fb28-022.jpg
Notes: 23301559840 At first I though these were Stropharia mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week, but there's no hint of purple-brown spores. Maybe they are Floccularia albolanaripes aka Armillaria albolanaripes with white spores? They have pretty scales on the stem below the ring. Mushrooms were scarce in the Oregon woods this year, but I found a few. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

File.Name: fc07e_fb29-060.jpg
Notes: 23514428391 These are pretty little brown-spored mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week - maybe genus Hebeloma? These are growing between a moss and a spent frond of Western Sword Fern. I found a fair number of these. Mushrooms were scarce in the Oregon woods this year, but I found a few. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

File.Name: fc07f_fb29-051.jpg
Notes: 23596501405 I'm pretty sure these mushrooms in the Oregon woods are genus Laccaria, but I won't try to guess which kind they are. They are quite pretty with those slightly-lavender colors. Hah, I think they are frozen - look close to see the ice crystals in the gills. It was cold last week! Mushrooms were scarce in the Oregon woods this year, but I found a few. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2015)

File.Name: fc08a_fc08-003.jpg
Notes:
22995061043 Here are acorns, caps, and fallen leaves of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, near home. These trees aren't Quercus oaks, but they make fat satisfying acorns which are remarkable with those scaly chestnut-like cups. There aren't a lot of the acorns in this drought year, but there are a few. Most of them are aborted like those in the "pinched cap" in the upper-right or this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2015)

It was another lovely almost-winter day, warm with high clouds and a bit more breeze than we've been having. This may be the first sign of our next "little storm" that's due on Thursday, but it doesn't sound like much. *Sigh*

File.Name: fc08b_fc08-006.jpg
Notes: 23253970079 This is a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It really looks like a gall, in fact I took that black spot on the top as an exit hole when I got this photo. I didn't realize that it wasn't until I got the photo on my computer screen at home! I wonder if that's an adaptation, part of the illusion? I found this one on a leafy stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2015)
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fc08c_fc08-005.jpg
Notes:
23325673700 This is the tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. Look at that neat perfect exit hole where the tiny wasp chewed its way out! I got this photo in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2015)

The gall is tiny - it's the smallest "detachable gall" I know of. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, as close as I can get. The gall is about 490 pixels across, which makes it about 1.9 mm across - less than 1/10 inch. The exit hole is just 160 pixels across, which makes it about 0.6 mm across - about 1/32 inch. (See here for how I figured size.) That's a tiny wasp to wiggle out of that hole!
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fc08d_fb28-035.jpg
Notes: 22993790253 This is a Jelly Fungus in the Tremellales group of mushrooms growing on a fallen conifer trunk in the Oregon woods. The mushroom is just thawing out after being frozen solid overnight. It was cold during our Thanksgiving trip, with lows around 25∞ and highs around 35∞. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2015)

File.Name: fc08e_fb27-014.jpg
Notes: 23252637729 I guess this is an Amanita mushroom because of the veil fragments on the left photo, but it's thoroughly engulfed by another Hypomyces fungus or kin as you can see on the right. Yep, even the fruiting bodies of fungi get fungus infections! I found this mushroom in the Oregon woods on our Thanksgiving trip. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2015)

File.Name: fc08f_fb27-017.jpg
Notes: 22993300233 This is a still-frozen mushroom in the genus Amanita section Vaginatae - the mushrooms that used to be known as genus Amanitopsis. I won't try to guess which one this is. They have a membranous "universal veil" at the base but no ring or "partial veil" at all. I found this one in a place in the Oregon woods where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2015)

File.Name: fc09a_fc09-031.jpg
Notes:
23562235231 Here is a horde of tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess the thrips are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae - see Bug Guide. I find these critters every year on these flowers, and nowhere else. I watched them for a while - they are like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend most of their time chasing each other away! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 December 2015)

I got another photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. They are about 390 pixels long in the original photo, which comes to 1.5 mm - about 1/16 inch. Like I said, they are tiny! By the way, this is a different manzanita shrub than the ones I've been showing. They are just starting to really flower.

The NWS still has us down for a 70% chance of a bit of rain tomorrow, with another chance on Sunday. These will both be small storms, not the rains we really need.
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: fc09b_fc09-042.jpg
Notes: 23618158926 Here are two photos of a remarkable little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road. Look at it's long labia on the left! I wonder if this is the bee that cuts those slits in the flowers before they open to gain entrance to the sweet stuff inside? It seems to know about them at least. It also has long antennae and mandibles. I have no time to hit the books tonight, but it looks distinctive. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 December 2015)

File.Name: fc09c_fc09-012.jpg
Notes: 23016825103 This is the same mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans or P. longipalpis, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg sack that I show in this photo and this photo. It's getting so late in the year that I suspect she's guarding an empty nest. At first I wondered if she was alive. I poked her and she came to life in a threatening pose! I got a closer shot of her face, maybe I'll post it tomorrow. She has her nest in a plant of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 December 2015)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: fc09d_fb30-069.jpg
Notes: 23015427284 These are new pollen cones on a fallen branch of native Incense Cedar (Caalocedrus decurrens) in the Cupressaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. The branch is sitting on the sexual stage of a Moss (Bryophyta), see this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2015)

File.Name: fc09e_fb28-030.jpg
Notes:
23275342359 These are Lactarious or Russula mushrooms in the Oregon woods. My first thought when I found them was genus Lactarious, but they had no latex in the gills when I split them. So maybe they are genus Russula? Whatever they are, they are solid and rather pretty. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

Every year I forget my mushrooms and have to relearn them. And after four year of drought in California, I *really* have to relearn them! If you recognize these, please let me know.

File.Name: fc09f_fb28-037.jpg
Notes:
23616940136 This is a troop of little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family in the Oregon woods. I got my family shot in this photo just to the right. Most of these caps have tiny "eggs" inside . The eggs are really spore sacs called peridioles. These are growing on a fallen branch in a conifer woodland. The mushrooms are "splash cups" - raindrops knock the eggs out of the nest to scatter the spores, and you can see a few on the log. One cap near the bottom still has a cover. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2015)

Note the mosses on the old caps at the bottom. This is kind of the opposite of a lichen where fungi colonize algae. In this case the mosses are colonizing the fungi!

File.Name: fc10a_fc10-002.jpg
Notes:
23557433452 I noticed this tiny spider on the plastic end cap of the roof rack of Julie's car this morning. I assumed without thinking too much that it's a Jumping Spider (Salticidae). Then I got it on my computer screen, and realized that it's bristly and the eyes are all wrong for that family. Maybe it's a Ghost Spider in the family Anyphaenidae? I got his photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. It's about 1130 pixels long in the original photo, which makes it 4.4 mm wide, about 5/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Like I said, it's tiny! Sure, I moved it to a nearby plant - after I got the photo! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2015)

We did get a bit of rain today, but just a bit so far, about 1/10 inch. This is not the much-hyped El NiÒo rain that the NWS still says is coming, but anything helps. We still have a chance of more rain tonight and tomorrow, and again on Sunday. We really need it.

File.Name: fc10b_fc10-013.jpg
Notes: 23297766539 Here are strange flowers and new berries of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. tomentosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The berries have a glow about them. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2015)
ScientificName: Phoradendron leucarpum; tomentosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron leucarpum; tomentosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: fc10c_fc09-032.jpg
Notes: 23369684920 Here is a bigger horde of tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess the thrips are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae - see Bug Guide. See my photo yesterday for more info. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 December 2015)
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: fc10d_fc09-019.jpg

File.Name: fc11a_fc11-007.jpg
Notes:
23059760604 This is a closer shot of the tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms in my [Previous] photo. They are sprouting from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens as close as it can focus. The white mushroom cap in the center is about 350 pixels across in the original photo, which makes it 1.37 mm, less than 1/16 inch across. (See here for how I figured.) Like I said, they are tiny! The mushrooms seem to sprout from those dark spots on the leaf. Our rain last night revived the mushrooms, but I guess they've been around for a while. This was the only leaf I found them on today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2015)

It was cold, dark, and very windy today after our small rain yesterday. We ended up with just 15/100 inch, but even that wimpy rain was enough to bring out some new life. Our winter-wet season in the woods is a different world. I hope the NWS is right with their El NiÒo rain predictions so that we get more of it this winter!

File.Name: fc11b_fc11-005.jpg
Notes: 23687660785 I think these are tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Note the mushroom buds and long dark brown stems, also the dark stains in the background that will become more mushrooms. They seem to sprout from those dark spots on the leaf. Our rain last night revived these mushrooms, but I guess they've been around for a while. This was the only leaf I found them on today. See my [Next] photo for their size. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2015)

File.Name: fc11c_fc11-013.jpg
Notes: 23391602080 This is the electric white growth of a fungus growing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Actually it looks more blue than white, but I think that's a white balance thing. It looks like a fungus mycelium, but this is all of it I've ever seen. I don't think it's leading up to any kind of visible mushroom. I got this shot above our retaining wall that makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level. That small rain last night did some good! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2015)

File.Name: fc11d_fc11-001.jpg
Notes: 23391334560 Here are matching top and bottom shots of odd "things" on a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The left shot looks a bit like a fungus, but I guess they are galls - maybe mites (Acari), though they don't look like typical "erineum galls" that are usually depressed on top. There's a nipple-like opening on the bottom gall on the right, and I can see a group of tiny "critter-husks" on the midrib below the right-most gall. I'll try to get a closer photo tomorrow if I can find the leaf. It's been windy enough today to blow the leaves around. I can't recall finding these before. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2015)

File.Name: fc11e_fc11-004.jpg
Notes: 23059529333 This is a surprisingly early vine of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) already with leaves. D'oh - I broke the stem as I was trying to get it in a position to photograph. It's quite brittle, like glass tubing. This was the very end of this stem, so I didn't do too much damage. This one is climbing in a tree of Coast Live Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2015)

File.Name: fc11f_fc07-023.jpg
Notes: 23577872922 This is a handsome Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the Santa Ynez River a few days ago. Looking at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Peleteria. Yeah, it's too contrasty and not quite sharp, but so it goes. I have another photo of the rear end of this fly here. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 December 2015)
