File.Name: fc15a-dsc_0048.jpg
Notes:
23410294469 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. It was cold today, freezing last night, but the sun was out today and so was this butterfly. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 December 2015)

I've been editing this photo and this Flicker page on Julie's laptop computer. I don't like it, but I loaded some software and at least it's usable. The screen isn't as sharp as I'm used too, and way too narrow. I hope the photo is OK. Maybe a photo of a dump truck would have been more appropriate!

I finally got my stricken computer into the hands of people who know about them this morning, and it sounds like I might be OK. He's cloning the drive right now, and hopefully my files are safe. I believe the problem is a corrupt boot sector which means that the computer doesn't know where to start on the hard drive, and just hangs in a startup loop. Sure, I have backups, but "too little, too late" - actually too little, too early. There's stuff there I don't want to lose. I took my computer to Channel Data Systems in Santa Barbara, and I'll very likely have them build me a new computer to my specs.
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; too little, too late
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; too little, too late

File.Name: fc16a-dsc_0032.jpg
Notes:
23502882470 I believe this is a beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's perched for a moment on the gravel by our mailbox this afternoon. These are our usual winter resident dragonflies. I saw this one... and lost it, and saw it again... and lost it. But finally I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2015)

I've been using Julie's laptop computer to do these photos. Everything feels wrong, but I loaded some software and at least it's usable. The screen isn't as sharp as I'm used too, and way too narrow. I hope the photos are OK.

There's no joy in Mudville tonight... I got word this morning that my old hard drive is corrupted and the main partition is gone. Right now the repair people are running data recovery software to try to uncover the directory structure, so there's still a slim hope. Oh well, I mostly photograph the same things over and over - I've doing it for years, and I'll keep doing it. Like today! Try searching "Sympetrum corruptum" in my archives!

I do have backups, but "too little, too late" - actually too little, too soon. Be careful - it could happen to you!
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; too little, too late
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; too little, too late

File.Name: fc16b-dsc_0033.jpg
Notes: 23690037882 This is another photo of the pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family from yesterday, showing her wings below. What pretty gold speckles. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 December 2015)

File.Name: fc17a-dsc_0024.jpg
Notes:
23451069689 This is looking eye-to-eyes at a small male Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) that I found on a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. See my [Previous] photo. It's a spider with 8 eyes, but the outside eyes are so close together that they seem to merge. It's looking at me! This is a very male spider with those swollen balloon-like pedipalps which are its sexual parts. Please excuse the white-balance, as I got this shot in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, which pretty much includes all of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2015)

Hooray! Dan at Channel Data Systems in Santa Barbara managed to recover many of the lost files on my stricken hard drive. I feel so much better. I thought I was in for a new computer, but Dan is putting a new hard drive and a SSD boot disk into my old machine. I know, that's like dropping a new engine into an old truck - before you know it the clutch gives out. But this will buy me some time to think about what I really want before I plunk down the coin for a new computer. It should be ready tomorrow. I've been doing today's photo-editing on Julie's laptop, and it will be a real pleasure to get back to my workstation. Sure, I'll still have software to reinstall. There's probably no time for new photos tomorrow. But it will be good to be home again!
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Prickly Pear

File.Name: fc17b-dsc_0071.jpg
Notes: 23523013950 I believe this is a small Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) trying to hide under the spines of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. I believe that is the right ID because of the downward-pointing shape of its abdomen and that speckled pattern that reminds me of ice breaking up - compare this photo. Please excuse the white-balance, as I got this shot in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. That pretty much includes all of our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2015)
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Prickly Pear

File.Name: fc17c-dsc_0076.jpg
Notes: 23735936851 This is a tiny Looper or Inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a new urn-like flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I'm not sure who drilled the hole in the not-quite-open flower. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 December 2015)
CommonNames: Looper; Inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Bigberry Manzanita

File.Name: fc17d-dsc_0123.jpg
Notes: 23191288713 Here are new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family flowering in the mountains - and there are already new cherries! Yipes. I found the first flowers on October 25, see this photo. I usually find the first flowers in late January or February. I reckon the natives are confused. That's Mission Pine Mountain in the background. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 December 2015)

File.Name: fc18a-dsc_0075.jpg
Notes:
23728971512 This is another handsome gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). What a remarkably strong pattern it has! If you look close, you can see that some of the spots are actually lifting up. I can't explain this. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2015)

My computer (and all my files!) is stuck in the shop over the weekend, so I'm still editing my photos of Julie's laptop. I don't like it - the mouse is twitchy and the screen isn't sharp - but I'm doing the best I can. The good news is that we have good chance of rain tonight and tomorrow! It's another small storm. We have a better chance of real rain next Friday, Christmas Day. What a present that will make!
Locality: and all my files!) is stuck in the shop over the weekend

File.Name: fc18b-dsc_0031.jpg
Notes: 23468839109 This is a handsome crustose lichen on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe it is a Rim Lichen in the genus Lecanora. That name comes from the rim of "lecanorine" thallus-like growth around the apothecia of the lichen. Sorry, it's hard not to use "lichen-talk" with these creatures! The apothecia are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen - little disk fungi (Ascomycetes). The thallus is the main body of the lichen, and these lichens have their cups rimmed with that tissue. That's a different lichen in the right which I don't yet know, maybe genus Flavopunctelia? I'm just starting to learn the lichens and lichen terminology, but I've been distracted by computer problems this week. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2015)
CommonNames: crustose lichen; Coast Live Oak; Rim Lichen

File.Name: fc18c-fc13-dsc_0031.jpg
Notes: 23208525984 This is a golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a moment on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in a sunny place in the canyon. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2015)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: fc18d-fc13-dsc_0043.jpg
Notes: 23754067071 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in a sunny place in the canyon. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2015)

File.Name: fc19a-dsc_0067.jpg
Notes:
23561538150 This is an apple tree with apples in the rain today on the wild edge of our garden. It might be hard to see the raindrops unless you zoom in, but they are here! This small apple tree is flanked by two native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) trees, with the dark woods behind. This apple tree came up as a seedling 30+ years ago when we built our home from a spit seed or tossed core. Even in a bad year, we always get a few of the tart green apples that the deer can't reach, yum! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2015)

It was still sunny when I got up this morning, and I first thought that the weather service had dropped the ball. The rain came in a bit after 11 am, but it was a fast moving "small storm" that only lasted an hour or so and dropped just under 2/10 inch of rain. We have a better chance of real rain next Friday, Christmas Day. What a present that will make!

I'm still doing my photos on Julie's laptop computer. I don't like it - the mouse is twitchy and the screen isn't sharp - but I'm doing the best I can. Hopefully I get my computer (and files!) back on Monday.

File.Name: fc19b-dsc_0005.jpg
Notes: 23230180683 This is looking into a broken cap of a "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" in the garden today, posed on a volcanic rock that it resembles. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone". The fungus gets its name from the pea-shaped peridioles which are visible in my photo. They very slowly break down to release the spores. This fungus may well date back to last year - see this photo. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. I got another photo of the same mushroom after the rain today, but I think this one shows more detail. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2015)

File.Name: fc19c-dsc_0039.jpg
Notes: 23748004322 Here are crustose lichens growing on a sandstone boulder in the canyon. They both might be Rim Lichens in the genus Lecanora, see my photo yesterday - but that one was on bark, and these are on rock. The lighter-colored lichens on the right might be the Stonewall Rim Lichen (Lecanora muralis), but I'm not sure about the one on the left and upper-right. That one almost looks like the rock itself, and you have to get close enough to see the apothecia to be sure it's a lichen! Sure, there are other small lichens here as well. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, but I've been distracted by computer problems this week. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2015)
CommonNames: crustose lichens; Rim Lichens; Stonewall Rim Lichen

File.Name: fc19d-dsc_0081.jpg
Notes: 23830048756 This is a juicy-looking lichen growing on a fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I don't know what kind of lichen it is, but it appears to be foliose and quite different from any other I've found. I believe the green caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae, but they look full of green algae too. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, and I've acquired a few new books. I've been distracted by computer problems this week, but I'm sure I'll find this one again when I have time to study it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2015)

File.Name: fc20a-dsc_0020.jpg
Notes:
23878216835 Here are tiny drizzle-drops on a spiderweb on a dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. I guess the spider is a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) which make those disorganized webs. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2015)

It was quite cold today after our small storm yesterday, temps in the 30s all morning, finally up to about 40∞ in the afternoon. It was really a crummy day for new photos - cold and cloudy and windy - but I got a few anyway. * Sigh* Our chance of a Christmas rain seems to be fading. We stand at 1.81 inches so far, just 23% of normal. Even Lompoc and Lake Cachuma have received more rain than us this year. Point Conception has been a barrier for these small storms. Are the El NiÒo rains still coming??

I'm still doing these photos on Julie's laptop computer. I don't like it - the mouse is twitchy and the screen isn't sharp - but I'm doing the best I can. I expect to pick up my rebuilt computer tomorrow and get back to work, but I'll still have some work reinstalling software. I'm hoping for the best!

File.Name: fc20b-dsc_0119.jpg
Notes: 23851914326 I guess these are new Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. Mushrooms are still scarce, but the small rains we've been having help. One characteristic of these mushrooms is the large knob of soil or leaves they bring up when picked, held together by the mycelium of the fungus. These are the first ones I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2015)

File.Name: fc20c-dsc_0040.jpg
Notes: 23250995603 This is an old gall of the Gouty Stem Gall Wasp (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera riddled with the exit holes of the tiny gall wasps and covered with new lichens. This one is on a dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, and I've acquired a few new books. I've been distracted by computer problems this week, but I'm sure I'll find these again when I have time to study them. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2015)

File.Name: fc20d-dsc_0044.jpg
Notes: 23509749299 This is a small universe of lichens and moss on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. How many lichens can you see? There are always more than I expect! I'm just starting to learn the lichens, and I've acquired a few new books. I've been distracted by computer problems this week, but I'm sure I'll find these one again when I have time to study them. Mosses will come some time after lichens. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2015)

File.Name: fc21a-dsc_0007.jpg
Notes:
23789758792 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushrooms in the woods, and this is a contender! I noticed these tiny 'shrooms on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. I put my pen in the photo for scale. My pen has a 0.7 mm ball, and that's bigger than these these tiny fungi. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the fungus to the right of my pen is about 125 pixels across - that's only about about 1/2 mm across or 1/64 inch! (See here for how I figured.) Nah, I wasn't grubbing in the mulch to get this photo. This is above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden, a perfect height for photos and my window into the tiny world of the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2015)

My rebuilt computer was still uploading updates when I went down today, so I'll pick it up tomorrow. I'm not surprised - I have a new install of Windows 7, a five year old OS with many updates every month since it was released! No problem, but I'm *still* doing these photos on Julie's laptop computer, which I don't like. We actually had a bit of rain today - another "small storm" that caught me by surprise on my trail today! We've had about 1/10 inch so far.

The true Winter Solstice happens tonight at 8:49 pm PST, but that happens to be tomorrow by UTC time, so tomorrow is the official first day of winter. This is the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice everyone!

File.Name: fc21b-dsc_0018.jpg
Notes:
23815390891 I believe these are new Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up in the woods today, though they are appearing in a place that's overgrown with non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family rather than under the oak trees where they should be. One field trait of these mushrooms is the large knob of soil or leaves at the base when picked, held together by the mycelium of the fungus. Compare my photo yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2015)

At least there are these mushrooms in the woods! It's been a tough time for mushrooms through our many drought years.
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Stipa miliacea; Piptatherum miliaceum; Poaceae

File.Name: fc21c-dsc_0046.jpg
Notes: 23871403006 Here are small Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) and Mosses (Bryophyta) growing on a bank where the sun never shines at this time of year along my trail today. Hah - it was starting to drizzle as I got this photo! The liverworts are probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2015)

File.Name: fc21d-dsc_0053.jpg
Notes: 23814697821 I found this native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing in the woods today. I haven't noticed these for a while, so maybe the fronds are new? At least they are newly revived by our "small rains" this year. I bent a frond over to show the back side, which get powdery as they mature. You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2015)

File.Name: fc23a_fc23-007.jpg
Notes:
23309183503 This is a small stand of tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves. They are just starting to appear after our rain ended yesterday, and there might be *many* of them tomorrow! Compare this photo from last year. I got this photo above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden, a perfect height for photos and my window into the tiny world of the woods. Is that a new leaf of a Sanicula , Apiaceae) on the right? Rain yesterday, new mushrooms today! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2015)

It was windy enough today to wiggle the mushrooms hiding in the oak mulch! Windy yesterday, windy last night, and windy today - that's a lot of air moving somewhere! I did take a walk today, but mostly I worked on my rebuilt computer. I have it set up now pretty much as I like it, and it's a real pleasure to edit these photos on my large sharp screen! I'll try to get back to my usual six photos a day tomorrow IF the wind dies down. There is a chance of another small rain tomorrow. I'll do my best.
ScientificName: Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sanicula ; Apiaceae

File.Name: fc23b_fc23-006.jpg
Notes: 23935759885 These are more tiny Mycena mushrooms growing in a mulch of fallen leaves of (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. They look a bit different from the slightly larger mushrooms in my [Previous] photo. Note that fallen acorn in the background with an electric-looking fungus mycelium growing over the cup. Rain yesterday, new mushrooms today! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2015)

File.Name: fc23c_fc23-012.jpg
Notes: 23935372115 These are tiny Mycena mushrooms growing among fallen leaves of (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Hah - I had to bump up the ISO for this photo because it was so windy today that even the mushrooms were wiggling! They look a bit different from the even smaller mushrooms in my [Next] photo. Rain yesterday, new mushrooms today! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2015)

File.Name: fc23d_fc23-015.jpg
Notes: 23307031324 These are lovely little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on sandstone in the woods. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. If you look close, you can see lots of new stalks coming up from the leafy base. These aren't new, but they are newly revived after our decent rain yesterday. There are several different kinds, so I won't try to guess which one this is. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2015)

File.Name: fc24a_fc24-011.jpg
Notes:
23326054074 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. I drove up into the mountains today looking for these berries, and I didn't find as many as I usually do. I reckon the birds have been having their Christmas early. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2015)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: fc24b_fc24-017.jpg
Notes:
23928039456 Here are new flowers and foliage of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family flowering in the mountains. This is still the only shrub I've seen with flowers this year, see this photo from late October! It's not real holly, but the holly-like leaves are perfect for the holidays. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2015)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

This is still the only shrub I've seen with flowers this year, see this photo from late October! The holly-like leaves are perfect for the holidays. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2015)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: fc24d_fc24-012.jpg
Notes:
23871169321 Here are new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family - like little bells ringing for the holidays! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 December 2015)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: fc24e_fc24-005.jpg
Notes: 23845150422 I found these tiny hair-like fungi on a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. They are less than a quarter inch tall, and I don't know anything about them. I got this photo above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden, a perfect height for photos and my window into the tiny world of the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2015)

File.Name: fc24f_fc16-005.jpg
Notes:
23657575050 This is a lovely Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I got his photo last week. It's a "butt shot" of a fly, so it will do for both groups - HBBBT and HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2015)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all

File.Name: fc25a_fc25-026.jpg
Notes:
23346692594 Here are new mushrooms of tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) sprouting against the backdrop of a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. They're small, only a few millimeters across. The stems are tough and wiry. They can handle drying out and reviving with the next rain. I usually find them sprouting from fallen leaves, and maybe these are. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2015)

Mushrooms and lichens for Christmas! We had just a bit of rain last night, less than 1/10 inch, but enough to revive the lichens. It's been quite cold with ice on the car windshields until after 11 am at our house on the shady side of the mountains. I had commitments today, so I'm back to just four photos.

File.Name: fc25b_fc25-007.jpg
Notes: 23866150112 This is a universe of tiny Lichens growing on a finger-thick fallen twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's a whole world in there! I especially like those deep green lichens on the right with obvious apothecia - the spore-bearing structures of the fungal part of the lichen association between fungus and algae. I have new copies of "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and the massive "Lichens of North America" sitting on my desk, but with my computer problems recently, I've hardly glanced at them yet. Meanwhile I'm just enjoying their diversity! Lichens for Christmas! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2015)

File.Name: fc25c_fc25-015.jpg
Notes: 23866089142 This is a universe of tiny Lichens growing on a pencil-thin fallen twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's a whole world in there! I have new copies of "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and the massive "Lichens of North America" sitting on my desk, but with my computer problems recently, I've hardly glanced at them yet. Meanwhile I'm just enjoying their diversity! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2015)

File.Name: fc25d_fc25-013.jpg
Notes: 23865793142 This is a universe of tiny Lichens growing on a pencil-thin fallen twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's a whole world in there! I have new copies of "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and the massive "Lichens of North America" sitting on my desk, but with my computer problems recently, I've hardly glanced at them yet. Meanwhile I'm just enjoying their diversity! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2015)

File.Name: fc26a_fc26-010.jpg
Notes:
23995145595 This is another Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant growing on a mossy sandstone outcrop in the canyon today, with larger leaves than the one in my [Previous] photo. I believe this one is the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). See this photo for a look at the sporangia. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

It's been too cold for insects, so I've been looking for things that the rain brings - mushrooms, lichens, mosses, and liverworts. So far I've been finding them, but the winds have been drying everything out as fast as I find them. I wonder what I'll find tomorrow?

File.Name: fc26b_fc26-021.jpg
Notes: 23994733545 These are a small-leaved Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. Compare my [Next] photo. I'm not sure what kind of liverworts they are, but I've had Targionia hypophylla suggested for a name. They dry out very quickly, see this photo. I'm not sure if these are coming out or retreating after our rain (and wind) a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

File.Name: fc26c_fc26-030.jpg
Notes: 23367493413 I found this flowering vine of Cape Ivy aka German Ivy (Delairea odorata aka Senecio mikanioides, Asteraceae) at the beginning of West Camino Cielo road today near the bridge over San Jose Creek today. It's been rated as CalEPPC List A-1, top of the list as a nasty invasive that climbs over trees when it has a chance. Illegal dumping? This invader is still in a small area, but some restoration work is called for before it spreads down the creek. Fortunately they are relatively easy to remove. See my [Previous] photo to see its partner in crime. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

File.Name: fc26d_fc26-032.jpg
Notes:
23911044991 Yipes! This is a double infestation of both English Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) - mostly below and to the left - and Cape Ivy aka German Ivy (Delairea odorata aka Senecio mikanioides, Asteraceae) - mostly above and to the right. It is kind of neat to find them together like this. I found this infestation at the beginning of West Camino Cielo road today near the bridge over San Jose Creek. Illegal dumping? These invaders are still in a small area, but some restoration work is called for before they spread down the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

I found Cape Ivy in the same place way back in 2006 - see my old photo at Fotolog. I meant to pull it out, but I never got around to it and it disappeared on it's own. I reckon our cold weather in the mountains did it in. I wouldn't want to bet on that happening again in this year!
CommonNames: English Ivy; Cape Ivy; German Ivy
ScientificName: Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Delairea odorata; Senecio mikanioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Delairea odorata; Senecio mikanioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: fc26e_fc26-008.jpg
Notes: 23697605610 Despite the drying wind, I found these nice Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. They are the only "larger than tiny" mushrooms I've been finding lately. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

File.Name: fc26f_fc26-001.jpg
Notes: 23625172689 Here's what the constant drying wind does for mushrooms... These are the same tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) sprouting against the backdrop of a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed in my [Previous] photo yesterday. This morning they look shriveled and droopy, but they might revive with the next rain. *Sigh* We stand at 2.4 inches of rain this year, just 27% normal. Most of the small storms have pretty much stopped at Point Conception, and even when one gets by - it gets blown away! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2015)

File.Name: fc27a_fc27-026.jpg
Notes:
23389672763 This is a remarkable little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road. It's remarkable because it looks like its cast in bronze, but also because it's out working on this cold winter day! I guess it's trying to warm up in the sun. I won't try to guess what kind of bee this is. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)

It was cold today with frosty nights. It's cold enough to turn the mountains blue on my empty cans of Coors beer out on the porch during the day! We do have a small chance of rain tonight from another of those storms that will peter out as it gets around Point Conception. Why is this our weather pattern so far this year?

File.Name: fc27b_fc27-021.jpg
Notes: 23720200920 Here are two photos of a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae) in the order Diptera that I found today on a leaf of a flowering Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the mountains. It was breezy when I got these photos this morning, so neither photo is quite sharp - but together maybe they show the fly. I got the photo of the left while the fly was grooming, showing its remarkable patterned eyes. The photo on the right shows the remarkable pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. I guess this fly is genus Trupanea of the tribe Tephritini. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Trupanea; Tephritini
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Trupanea; Tephritini

File.Name: fc27c_fc27-002.jpg
Notes: 23388780713 Here are new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that have been opened up by (I guess) Wild Bees. Look close - there are tiny thrips hiding inside the flowers! I believe these are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae of the order Thysanoptera. It's been too cold for most insects, but see this photo when they all come out to play! It's interesting that that lower open flower has holes in the stamens. That was one impatient bee! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae; Thysanoptera

File.Name: fc27d_fc27-008.jpg
Notes: 23906931562 Here are new "flowers" of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The tassels still aren't quite open yet - showing their stamens - but they're getting close. I've seen this particular shrub before, see this photo. The shrubs are dioecious, but I'm sure this is a male staminate plant. The flower-catkins look like ornaments! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)

File.Name: fc27e_fc27-007.jpg
Notes: 23386751064 This extraordinary little Foliose Lichen is covered with monochrome apothecia - the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. I found these covering the dead twigs of a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the mountains today. I won't try to guess what kind of lichen they are, but they sure are striking! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)
ScientificName: Foliose Lichen; apothecia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Foliose Lichen; apothecia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: fc27f_fc27-018.jpg
Notes: 23718861730 I believe this is a colony of the Emery Rocktripe Lichen (Umbilicaria phaea) growing on the sunny side of a sandstone rock face in the mountains. They are considered a foliose lichen, and they must be tough critters to survive in this exposed place! The black spots are the apothecia - the fruiting bodies - of the fungus part of this lichen. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)

File.Name: fc28a_fc28-002.jpg
Notes:
23740291580 This is another tiny Mycena mushroom poking its head up in a mulch of fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Maybe that leaf has provided some shelter from the near-constant drying winds we've been having lately? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2015)

We did have a sprinkle of rain this morning, but just a trace - not enough to measure. It was followed by our usual chaser of wind wind wind. I'm still waiting for the El NiÒo rains that are expected this season. We still stand at just 27% of normal rainfall.

File.Name: fc28b_fc28-016.jpg
Notes: 23927317282 I came home from my walk this afternoon and just happened to notice this tiny micro-moth (Lepidoptera) sitting on our outdoor propane BBQ upstairs. It's about 1/4 inch long, micro-lepidoptera for sure. This isn't the best photo, but you can clearly see its antennae and those "horns" over the top of its head which I guess are labial palps. Yeah, this is in the shade. Our porch is always in the shade this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2015)

File.Name: fc28c_fc28-007.jpg
Notes:
24035124895 I found this small translucent grub on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning before the wind kicked up. You can see the larva - and its inner-larva inside! I guess it's perched head-down? I was just watching the Bushtit birds this morning working the oak leaves of the tree off our upstairs porch, and maybe this is what they were looking for? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2015)

As Alice Abelia says below, this may well be the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). Compare this photo at BugGuide, and note the two little projections on the big end.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: fc28d_fc28-005.jpg
Notes: 23408170233 I reckon this leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) has been eroded by a fungus, creating a network of little canyons. I guess this is how oak leaves get "skeletonized". Compare this photo which may show the fungus in action. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2015)

File.Name: fc28e_fc27-028.jpg
Notes: 23952144551 This is looking close at new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family flowering in the mountains. They look like they belong in the ocean! This one shrub near Arroyo Burro Saddle is still the only plant I've seen this year with flowers, see this photo from late October! Flowers are scarce right now, so I keep going back to it. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)

File.Name: fc28f_fc27-014.jpg
Notes: 23407659193 Here are hairy new leaves and hopefully flower buds of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. The "ssp. mollis" refers to the long white non-glandular hairs on the petioles and stems. This is our other native manzanita, different from the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in my [Previous] photo. A big difference is that these make burls, while the other one doesn't. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: fc29a_fc29-007.jpg
Notes:
24057079885 I found this new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) in the garden today! I've been looking for them. This clump is coming up from an old stump of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) in the garden that flowered faithfully for many years and grew as tall as our upstairs deck. It finally died a few years ago, but it's supporting new life in its death. I can't explain the yellow color of the gills of the one mushroom I picked, except that these fungi are notoriously variable. The name comes from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)

The wind finally died down today, but it remains cold. I had to scrape the ice off my truck windshield at 11:00 am today! The mountains on my empty Coors beer cans out on the porch are still blue, so "It's as cold as the RockiesÆ". Well, maybe not that cold, but it's cold for us!
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: fc29b_fc29-044.jpg
Notes: 23948406062 I found these little mushrooms in the woods today! I guess they are some kind of genus Clitocybe because of the decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe Clitocybe gibba? I don't know anything else about them, except that I've probably found them before on the same trail - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)

File.Name: fc29c_fc29-019.jpg
Notes: 23429495783 These are small cup-like Cladonia lichens growing from moss on a sandstone outcrop in the shady woods. There are several candidates for what they are: Cladonia asahinae, C. chlorophaea, and C. pyxidata. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, so I won't try to guess. I found these growing on thin soil over sandstone, with manzanita debris. They have copious squamules - those foliose-looking "leaves" at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)
ScientificName: Cladonia asahinae; C. chlorophaea; C. pyxidata
associatedTaxa: Cladonia asahinae; C. chlorophaea; C. pyxidata

File.Name: fc29d_fc29-022.jpg
Notes: 23427820224 These are small Cladonia lichens growing from moss on a sandstone outcrop in the shade. I guess they are Cladonia furcata or maybe C. scabriuscula. I can see a different Cladonia cup in the top-center. This one doesn't have copious squamules at the base like the one in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)

File.Name: fc29e_fc29-038.jpg
Notes: 23947555252 This is looking closer at a stem of Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing in a crack on a sandstone outcrop. See my [Previous] photo. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this. It looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places like this. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. Compare this photo from a year ago before the rains. This is one tough plant! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)

File.Name: fc29f_fc29-035.jpg
Notes: 23687527469 This is a surprisingly robust colony of Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing in a crack on a sandstone outcrop. (It's surprising in this drought year!) Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this. It looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places like this. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. See my [Next] photo for a closer look. You can see a few Emery Rocktripe Lichens (Umbilicaria phaea) growing on the rock above, see this photo. Hah - the rocks are covered with lichens! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2015)

File.Name: fc30a_fc30-045.jpg
Notes:
24076640335 I found this tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly working a still-new flower of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today on a dry bank above the river. This is the first photo I've gotten that shows that these butterflies are actually working the flowers. The butterfly might be more interested in the non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus, Chenopodiaceae) plants nearby - its host plant. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail - with wings open. I reckon this one is a female with those brown wings. I saw the butterflies when I first got to this place in the morning, but I couldn't get a photo. They are very wary! I came back after an hour or two, and got this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)

Butterflies and flies and flowers in December! But it was a notch warmer today, about 40∞ when I got up this morning instead of in the 20s like yesterday, and it was in the low 60s by the river today. I'm old enough to feel the cold in my joints, so I appreciate this!
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Coastal Goldenbush; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed
ScientificName: Brephidium exile; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Brephidium exile; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae

File.Name: fc30b_fc30-024.jpg
Notes: 24050321356 This is a very bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the Santa Ynez River today. You can see its long proboscis probing the flowers. Looking at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Peleteria. It's a handsome fly that seems unusual for almost the new year, but here it is - along with the flower! I took "a few" photos, and saved a couple for BBBT and FDF. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria

File.Name: fc30c_fc30-004.jpg
Notes: 24076034735 This is a beautiful new flower of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. I went down to the river today to find this plant in flower, and I almost missed it. The flowers open in the afternoon and shut down in the morning, and I didn't get to them until almost 11:00 am today. I managed to find this one flower that was still presentable. I expect to find these plants in February and later, but here they are in December. See this photo for my first sighting this year, on December 7. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)

File.Name: fc30d_fc30-035.jpg
Notes: 23993285511 This is a closer shot of green Bigleaf Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. macrophyllum) in the in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae). You can see the white-barked trunks of the sycamore in the background. This photo shows the odd "flowers" that this plant has. Usually this mistletoe is high up in the trees, see my [Previous] photo. You have to hunt around to find one low enough to photograph like this! This is a plant parasite that favors these trees, along with alder, ash, cottonwood, and willow - anything but oak! The oaks have their own mistletoe with furrier leaves, see this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)
ScientificName: Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: fc30e_fc30-041.jpg
Notes: 23448649693 This is an almost winter-bare tree of native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae). The tree is in the sun with a shady background. You can see a couple of clumps of green Bigleaf Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. macrophyllum) up in the canopy. See my [Next] photo for a closer look. This is a plant parasite that favors these trees, along with alder, ash, cottonwood, and willow - anything but oak! The oaks have their own mistletoe with furrier leaves, see this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)
ScientificName: Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum
associatedTaxa: Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum

File.Name: fc30f_fc30-039.jpg
Notes: 23707389849 This is a winter-bare tree of native Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii, Salicaceae) by the river today. There's a branch of a white-barked native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) tree visible on the right. I think I have a photo of the same trees showing fall color from last month. These trees show their bones in winter. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)
ScientificName: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: fc31a_fc31-012.jpg
Notes:
23800555170 Crouching tiger, hidden dragon(fly)...

I believe this is another beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's perched for a moment on a twig in the mountains today. It's looking at me - as I try to get closer through a pile of old brush, not very stealthily! These are our usual winter resident dragonflies which come out when the sun is shining. I worked to get this photo - wading through the dead brush. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2015)

I believe the real new year started a week ago on the solstice, and I've always wondered why the official new year begins on January 1 in our calendar.

Happy New Year!

File.Name: fc31b_fc30-018.jpg
Notes: 23467786164 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a very bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the Santa Ynez River yesterday. If you follow the arc of the fly's butt down, you can see another tiny fly on the flower - like elephants and rabbits! Looking at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Peleteria. See this photo from yesterday of the same fly. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Peleteria

File.Name: fc31c_fc31-024.jpg
Notes:
23987409312 This is another photo of the nice cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that's coming up from an old stump of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) in the garden. The mushrooms make beautiful clusters, and they are edible as well - see the comments on this photo. The name comes from the color, not the taste. My son found a second cluster of them near home today, but it's down in a hollow that's thick with brambles. Maybe I'll try to get a photo tomorrow - maybe not! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2015)

It's interesting that for gardeners, these honey-mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "Oak Root Fungus". They seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps.
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Chaparral Currant; Oak Root Fungus
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: fc31d_fc31-010.jpg
Notes:
23466751154 This is a handsome lichen growing on a dead stem of of an interesting oak in our mountains that I believe is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei, Fagaceae). I believe this might be a Tube Lichen in the genus Hypogymnia. Look how black the lichen is on the underside, and compare this photo from Oregon a month ago. There are large brown apothecia, the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. I don't believe these lichens are common in our part of the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2015)

I have new copies of "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and the massive "Lichens of North America" sitting on my desk, but with computer problems and the beautiful weather lately, I've hardly glanced at them yet. That's one real problem with the still-persistent (here at least) California drought - there are no rainy days to catch up on reading!
ScientificName: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Fagaceae; Hypogymnia; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Fagaceae; Hypogymnia; apothecia

File.Name: fc31e_fc31-009.jpg
Notes: 23799014240 This is looking close at glandular new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This is one of the new year's wildflowers even though they started flowering way back on September 7. (See this photo!) Most of the shrubs still just have buds, but a few plants like this one are very early. We get an overlap between last year's flowers and the new year's - appropriate for New Year's Eve. Think of the cartoon old year welcoming the new year baby! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2015)

File.Name: fc31f_fc31-011.jpg
Notes: 23986445552 This is one of the last of the 2015 wildflowers - native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family, still going strong. We get an overlap between last year's flowers and the new year's - appropriate for New Year's Eve. Think of the cartoon old year welcoming the new year baby! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2015)

File.Name: e101a_e101-calendar year rainfall.jpg
Notes:
I've been crunching numbers today instead of looking for insects and wildflowers. I collected data about San Marcos Pass rainfall from the Santa Barbara County Hydrology website. I got the data into Excel, and then rearranged it a bit to show Jan-Dec calendar years rather than the usual Sep-Aug rain years.

This chart shows total rainfall per calendar year (Jan-Dec) on San Marcos Pass since records began in 1966. If you look at the bar for 2013 on the very right, you will see that this is indeed the driest calendar year by quite a margin. We had just 7.3 inches of rain in all of 2013, well below our average of almost 35 inches. The next lowest is 12.6 inches in 1989. No wonder it feels so dry! I seen in the news that this is the driest year ever recorded in Los Angeles and San Francisco. Well, it is on San Marcos Pass as well.

There is still hope - see my [Previous] chart.
Locality: Jan-Dec) on San Marcos Pass since records began in 1966.  If you look at the bar for 2013 on the very right

File.Name: e101b_e101-sep-dec rainfall.jpg
Notes:
I've been crunching numbers today instead of looking for insects and wildflowers. What does a dry autumn mean for total rain overall for the rain year?

I collected data about San Marcos Pass rainfall from the Santa Barbara County Hydrology website. I got the data into Excel, and did quick and dirty spreadsheet. Then I collected the data for September-December rainfall vs. total rainfall. I did this a month ago for September-November rainfall, and found no correlation. Now there appears to be a slight one.

If you look at my chart, you can see that there is a cluster of years on the left that start and finish dry, and the is a slight upwards trend to the data. It's not a strong trend, but it shows that more Sep-Dec rain often means more total rain for the rain year. But there's still hope. The green line shows where we stand this year, with just 1.82 inches of rain so far. Other years have looked just as bad, and had near-normal rain. (Our average in about 35 inches.)

There is still plenty of wobble in the data. There are years like 1991 "March Miracle" storm dumped 20+ inches of rain in March after an even drier start to the year. (That's the second data point from the left.) There's still hope for a normal rain year!

But it is a very dry year. See my [Next] chart to appreciate just how dry it is.

File.Name: e102a_e101-014.jpg
Notes:
11720986473 This is another small Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) hunting flies on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I dig the shape of its hind feet! I only see the small ones in winter, even on warm days like this. The big ones take longer to warm up. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2014)

I'm more interested in the crusty black plants on the the rock. These are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants that shrivel up when it's dry and comes back when it rains - see this photo after our one real rain this year. These are real "resurrection plants".

Yes, we're in another drought year. See my [Previous] two photos/charts.
Locality:
Marchantiophyta) plants that shrivel up when it's dry and comes back when it rains - see this photo after our one real rain this year. These are real "resurrection plants".

Yes
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Liverwort

File.Name: e102b_e102-001.jpg
Notes: 11720783254 These are new leaves of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) plant family. These shrubs are drought-deciduous, and the plants looked quite dead a month ago. There's hope for the new year! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2014)

File.Name: e102c_e101-004.jpg
Notes: 11720252855 This is just another fly (order Diptera) perched on a dead leaf of Live Oak. I'm pretty sure it's a Scavenger Fly aka Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae with those red eyes and red bottom. This is the first one I've seen in quite a while. It's larger than many flies and has a buzzy flight. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2014)

File.Name: e102d_dc17-002.jpg
Notes:
11720349183 This is a handsome Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera spread-eagled on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. It was a cold morning and the wasp is trying to catch some rays. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2013)

I got this photo a few weeks ago when I was still posting mushroom photos from Oregon. That's the trouble with trips - you show trip photos and then have to make up the home photos, and sometimes you forget!
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: e102e_e102-011.jpg
Notes: 11719734085 These are clumps of the native perennial grass Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family, greening up for the new year. These are growing in a place that was cleared but didn't burn. I love these native grasses, and find them here and there in the mountains - despite the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2014)

File.Name: e102f_e101-020.jpg
Notes:
11719684574 This is a distinctive seedling of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. (Those darned botanists!) Here you can see it's paddle-like "seed leaves" or cotyledons and the first of its odd true leaves. I guess the other seedlings are non-native Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These are growing on the very dry edge of our garden. It's a wonder to find them in this drought year, and I wish them well! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2014)

I got this photo in the shade. We never see the sun on the ground this close to the solstice at our house on the north-facing slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains. It looks a bit too blue, but I figure photos taken in the shade should look like they were taken in the shade. You wouldn't want to "color correct" a beautiful sunset!
ScientificName: Pholistoma auritum; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; cotyledons; Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Pholistoma auritum; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; cotyledons; Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e103a_e103-034.jpg
Notes:
11743005735 This is the wonderful new foliage of a California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, sprouting like a mushroom on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. This plant is growing under a tree that burned in the fire. I've seen them nearby, but this is a new one for me. It's beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

I hiked along the "river trail" today. It runs through a section of the Santa Ynez River that was burned by the White Fire. Actually it's been burned twice - by the fire and by the drought. There are few seedlings in this drought year, but it's a pleasure to see these perennials.

File.Name: e103b_e103-060.jpg
Notes:
11742831655 This plant is not quite flowering, but it's showing some color on its bracts! It's a Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceaeplant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. The real flowers can't be too far behind. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2014)

It's actually kind of sad. I only found this one branch showing color in a place where there are several different plants. This drought year is really taking a toll, but it's interesting how the natives try to flower.

File.Name: e103c_e103-027.jpg
Notes: 11742699195 This is an empty shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) - maybe genus Helminthoglypta. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands. It's definitely a native. I found this one on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. The shell doesn't look burned, but maybe the char wore away? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e103d_e103-024.jpg
Notes:
11742683954 Of all the photos I wanted to come out sharp today, this was the one. But it failed, probably due to tripod shake. Well, it happens. My old Gitzo Explorer tripod is on it's last legs, held together with epoxy and pony clamps. But you can still make out what this is...

I believe this is a "Gastroid Agaric" mushroom in the family Podxales or kin. They are gilled mushrooms that don't open properly and don't forcibly discharge their spores. I found this one on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. The mushroom looks burned too, or is that just how it is? I really thought it might be a burned ping-pong ball or similar trash when I found it today. I have no idea how old it is - somewhere between now and May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e103e_e103-053.jpg
Notes: 11742908896 This is a spring-fed pool on the mostly dry Santa Ynez River. It does the soul some good to see real water in a drought year! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e103f_e103-001.jpg
Notes:
11741852473 I noticed this odd "reverse rainbow" around the sun this morning, over our rooftop. You can see the sun at the very bottom poking through the oak trees. Then right above the TV antenna you can barely make out a rainbow-like sundog, maybe part of a 22 degree halo . The real surprise is about that much higher, near the top of this photo. It's a rainbow curving away from the sun, like a smile! It may be a circumzenithal arc according to Robert Greenler's book Rainbows, Halos, and Glories which I happen to have on my book shelf. See Wikipedia. Both rainbows were brighter a few minutes ago, but I had to run down to my truck to find a lens that was wide enough to capture the scene. A few minutes later and they were both gone. I can't recall ever seeing this before. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2014)

It's not as early as it looks in this photo! I got the photo at about 9:40 am, when the sun starts to rise above the oak trees in the winter. I played with brightness and contrast a bit to try to bring out the colors. My Mom used to say that a ring around the sun means rain in a day or two. That's a good thought in this drought year, and there is a "slight chance of showers" next week.

File.Name: e104a_e104-009.jpg
Notes:
11765253924 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) suckling on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Look close - the bee has forced open the closed cap of the new flower to get the first taste! I assume it's getting nectar, but it also has a full load of whitish manzanita pollen on its hind leg. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 January 2014)

I went up into the mountains today hoping to get photos of other insects working the manzanita flowers. I saw other insects - hummingbirds too - but couldn't get the photos to prove it. It was cooler today, and more breezy.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; other
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; other

File.Name: e104b_e103-037.jpg
Notes: 11765003934 These are burned out stems of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family against a background of Monterey Shale, after the White Fire last May. It almost looks like a cubist painting of the gnarled stem on the left falling over to the right. These manzanitas don't stump-sprout, and there's no sign of new growth except for a bit of green seedlings in the lower-right. Wildfire and drought is a tough combination. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e104c_e103-020.jpg
Notes: 11765181416 This is a Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plant in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae on a dry flat by the Santa Ynez River. It was fire-trimmed by the White Fire last May. It looks like a pineapple! You can see the green new growth below the dead when the fire happened. These wild plants have been hit with the double whammy of wildfire and drought. I hope it makes it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: e104d_e103-043.jpg
Notes: 11764576864 This is a stump-sprouting shrub of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, growing on charred soil after the White Fire burned through this section of the river last May. That blue-green foliage is distinctive. It's a stand-up shrub, but it does have climbing tendencies given the opportunity. That large burl is doing it's job of producing new sprouts. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e104e_e103-047.jpg
Notes: 11764432874 Here is a stump-sprouting shrub of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's growing on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. I didn't realize that these shrubs stump-sprout, but here it is, growing from the base of burned stems. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e104f_e103-012.jpg
Notes: 11764078743 I was surprised to find these new flowers on the native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a rocky flat by the river. Here they are, along with the ripe dandelion-like seeds. I reckon this shrub isn't widely known. It's one of several summer-flowering yellow-flowered composites that make their home on the dry river bed, and here it is in the new year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 January 2014)

File.Name: e105a_e105-018.jpg
Notes:
11790332233 Even Lichens burn in a wildfire! These lichens are growing on a rock that's facing the way the White Fire came. I'm not sure, but they might be coming back. As Lillian Gish says at the end of the great film "The Night of the Hunter" (1955) - "They abide, and they endure." (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

I hiked up the Aliso Canyon trail today, the first time since just after the White Fire burned it out last May. See my photos from June 2, and [Previous] to that. It's one of my favorite walks, and I really haven't wanted to go back to see it in bad shape. I'm glad I finally made it. I was mostly looking for stump-sprouting plants. I'll have photos tomorrow, unless I find something more interesting!

File.Name: e105b_e105-032.jpg
Notes:
11790684226 This is native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family, coming back after the White Fire came through last May. I wouldn't recognize it, except that I knew it was here. See this photo of another plant before the fire. I'm curious about the distinctive gray ashes of this plant. I wonder if they have a special use? (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

This grass does grow on San Marcos Pass in a few secluded places, but it's more common in the interior backcountry. It likes a well-watered spot by a creek, but not in the creek. It was an important plant for native American basketry, and it's widely used as a landscaping plant.

File.Name: e105c_e105-030.jpg
Notes:
11790138986 This is all that's left of a remarkable clump of native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family after the White Fire came through last May. It's a large perennial bunch grass - three feet tall and twice that across - now reduced to a mound of ashes. I wouldn't recognize it, except that I knew it was here.

I put my "walking stick" in the photo for scale. My cane is about 3 feet long, which gives an idea how large the mound is. See this photo of the same grass before the fire. I'm curious about the distinctive gray ashes of this plant. I wonder if they have a special use? (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

This grass does grow on San Marcos Pass in a few secluded places, but it's more common in the interior backcountry. It likes a well-watered spot by a creek, but not in the creek. It was an important plant for native American basketry, and it's widely used as a landscaping plant.

I don't need my cane, but it helps me avoid false steps which aggravate my sciatica pain. It's a sturdy oak cane that I found second-hand in Glide, Oregon last summer when I really needed it. I wonder what it's history is.

File.Name: e105d_e105-021.jpg
Notes:
11789513584 This hole is the ground is (I guess) the ghost of the tree which used to live here, burned out by the White Fire last May. The hole is about 2 feet across. You can see small holes at the bottom where the roots took off. Maybe it was already a stump when the fire came through? I can't remember, though there are Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) trees nearby. It left a pile of white ashes, that's all. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

I was hiking today with just my 105 mm macro lens, like usual. I need a wider lens to show this better. I'll return to this place.

File.Name: e105e_e105-013.jpg
Notes: 11789694976 This is the burned up stump of a good-sized Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family growing along the Aliso Trail. It burned in the White Fire last May. Usually a wildfire leaves the trunk, but not this time. Compare my [Next] photo! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

File.Name: e105f_e105-035.jpg
Notes: 11789337556 Native trees are distinctive even when they are burned. That's Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) on the left, Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on the right, and Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala, Oleaceae) in the background. The sycamores have the richest colors, then the oaks, and finally the ash. Other shrubs have their own colors - for example, Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) is a rich deep brown. I doubt that any of these plants will recover after being burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)
CommonNames: Western Sycamore; Coast Live Oak; Foothill Ash; Toyon
ScientificName: Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Fraxinus dipetala; Oleaceae; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Fraxinus dipetala; Oleaceae; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: e106a_e105-048.jpg
Notes:
11809509115 These are ripe berries on (probably) Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on a tree Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Birds love the sticky berries. They are so sticky that the birds wipe their bills on tree stems, and thereby pass on the seeds to a new host. Ain't nature grand! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

I didn't notice the berries when I first got a photo on Saturday. I saw them on my computer screen and went back to get a better photo on Sunday. This plant is loaded with berries! This tree was on the lucky side of the White Fire last May - close, but not burned. The mistletoe is rather high in the tree, so the photo is a crop.

Update (Jan. 7): I first had this identified as Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum), but John Rusk graciously corrected me. P. macrophyllum usually grows on sycamores, and P. villosum is partial to oaks. It's confusing because they both grow side by side along the river. P. villosum is said to have hairier leaves and short-hairy berries near the tip. I can't see any hairs on the berries in my photo, but I'll try to get back and look closer.
CommonNames: Oak Mistletoe; Coast Live Oak; Update (Jan. 7); Big-leaved Mistletoe
ScientificName: Phoradendron villosum; Viscaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Phoradendron macrophyllum; P. macrophyllum; P. villosum; P. villosum
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron villosum; Viscaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Phoradendron macrophyllum; P. macrophyllum; P. villosum; P. villosum

File.Name: e106b_e105-007.jpg
Notes: 11809375313 Here are new stump-sprouts of a native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. It was burned by the White Fire last May. One reliable test is that the leaves smell like wild cherry when you crush one. Compare my [Previous] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

File.Name: e106c_e105-005.jpg
Notes: 11809549656 Here are new stump-sprouts of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It was burned by the White Fire last May. The leaves look a lot like Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae, but there are differences. For one thing they don't smell like wild cherry when you crush one. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)
ScientificName: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: e106d_e105-017.jpg
Notes:
11808729973 Here are new stump-sprouts of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. They are growing from a massive burl. The burned leaves are visible on the right - rich deep brown in color. This is the same "Christmas Berry" that I showed on Christmas Eve in a happier state. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

Stump-sprouting is an adaptation that some natives have and some don't. See this photo and this one.

File.Name: e106e_e105-043.jpg
Notes: 11808698064 Here are new stump-sprouts of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It was burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

File.Name: e106f_e105-036.jpg
Notes: 11808115835 It's interesting to find stump-sprouting shrubs and trees after a wildfire, but it's more interesting to find the plants that aren't sprouting. This is native Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. There's not a sign of a stump-sprout - which is doubly odd given such an obvious burl at the base. Maybe it's just waiting for real rain, but it sure looks done for. This one burned in the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 January 2014)

File.Name: e107a_e107-013.jpg
Notes:
11829818356 Here are the first new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains. We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains. This one is distinctive for its white flowers and alternate leaves. In a good year, it's the shrub that covers the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow. This is not a good year, but they are still appreciated. The individual flowers are intricate, like little origami boxes. There's a tiny fly (order Diptera) working a flower in the upper-left. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

I reckon it's a three-way tie for 3rd flower in our mountains, after the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). Finding new flowers is like following bouncing ball - it starts slow, but the speed picks up!

It really is remarkable that these native shrubs are flowering despite the drought. The woods are very dry, but here they are. Julie said this afternoon that "If it wasn't this year, I'd say it was going to rain!" It did feel a bit like rain this afternoon, but there is no rain in the forecast. We need rain!

I figure any day (even two days) with photos of new flowers, mushrooms, insects, and galls is well-spent!
Locality: even two days) with photos of new flowers
CommonNames: Bigpod Ceanothus; fly; Chaparral Currant; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Diptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: e107b_e107-005.jpg
Notes: 11828835575 These are the first real flowers I've seen this year of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceaeplant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. The red color is from the leafy bracts. The real flowers are the yellow-green tubes, with an exerted pistil. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e107c_e107-031.jpg
Notes: 11828993483 This is the first new flower I've seen of Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's just starting to flower in the mountains. The heart-shaped stamens are particularly attractive. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e107d_e107-022.jpg
Notes: 11828699793 I'm pretty sure these are some kind of Psathyrella mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. They are the first mushrooms I've found in the woods in this drought year! They are not very exciting or elegant, but here they are - and there are lots of them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e107e_e106-003.jpg
Notes:
11828849896 I believe this is a furry Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera, perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a rather large honeybee-size fly. Maybe it's the species Brachypalpus alopex in the subfamily Eristalinae? I've found these large flies before, in January last year - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2014)

I know it's a crappy photo. This is a 1:1 crop - an 800x800 crop from my 4800x3200 original photo in DX mode - with my with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens, from the shaky upstairs porch. But I got a photo!

To quote Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California" (2006) - "Little is known about this species' biology." It's small, less than 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e108a_e108-007.jpg
Notes:
11847271134 I'm not sure, but this may well be an infestation of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Eriosomatinae, Aphididae, Homoptera). They are producing masses of waxy wool on leaves of this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I can make out "bokeh-like" doughnut shapes under the leaf on the right which I guess are the aphids. This infestation was about 8 feet in the air, so I couldn't get closer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2014)

There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF). This appears to be a useful document.

The wind kicked up today, kind of startling after two weeks of calm days. I figure any change in the weather is a good sign, but the forecast is still dry.

File.Name: e108b_e108-009.jpg
Notes:
11846759254 I have no doubt that these are galled berries of a native shrub of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The normal berries turned blue-black and were gobbled up by birds months ago. See this photo. These galled berries are still on the shrubs long after their season. They all have an odd "crook in the neck" like this one. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2014)

Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one.

File.Name: e108c_e106-004.jpg
Notes:
11846377823 This is a gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The gall is quite shriveled up, like one of Julie's apple dolls. You can see another "maybe gall" on the folded leaf directly below, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2014)

This gall is 20 feet high in the oak tree. I got the photo with my with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens, from our shaky upstairs porch.
