File.Name: e108d_e108-001.jpg
Notes: 11845902205 This is new growth on a native shrub of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. Look how the stem on the right is swollen compared to the stem on the left. I reckon it's been galled by an insect, possibly a Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaca) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The leaves also look different on the galled and ungalled stems. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2014)

File.Name: e108e_e107-019.jpg
Notes: 11846054644 This is a dead-looking frond of Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. "Mucronate" means that it has a sharp point, and you can see the points on the little leaves, especially along the left edge of my photo. Hopefully it's not dead but just waiting for some rain, as I am. Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) is a relative without points - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e108f_e107-034.jpg
Notes: 11845509855 I really thought this Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family would be the next native shrub to flower in our mountains. You can see the inner flower parts just waiting... The buds been hanging on this shrub since early October - see this photo. Several other natives beat them to the punch this year, see my [Previous] three photos. The leaves are densely furry-tomentose underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

File.Name: e109a_e109-003.jpg
Notes:
11863610365 This is a female Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) looking right at me along our road this morning. She's crouched down like she's ready to run. She's been nibbling the understory plants in our woods, under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees, mostly Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) and Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in this place. It looks like pretty poor forage. I don't know how these wild deer survive in this drought year, but eating our garden must help! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

It was a windy morning and a windy afternoon, but there was a moment of calm at midday when I happened to be out taking these photos.
CommonNames: Southern Mule Deer; Coast Live Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Quercus agrifolia; Symphoricarpos mollis; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: e109b_e109-005.jpg
Notes:
11863892434 This is a tiny bisexual winter gall of one of the tiny Cynipidae wasps of the order Hymenoptera that will mate and produce larger summer galls on our Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia shrubs in the Fagaceae plant family - with an exit hole. The gall is quite small. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The exit hole is about 86 pixels wide in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the hole is 23.4x86/4800 = 0.42 mm. That's really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

These wasps have a complicated life cycle. The bisexual winter galls produce male and female wasps which will mate and lay eggs that will produce the unisexual summer galls. The summer galls will hatch only females that produce the bisexual galls that produce males and females, and the cycle repeats. Nature is sometimes complicated like that.

File.Name: e109c_e109-012.jpg
Notes: 11863732084 This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop. It's showing some drought stress in this very dry year. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this. The drought seems to be taking its toll on this plant. It looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

File.Name: e109d_e109-017.jpg
Notes:
11862874565 "N.P. Hockinson

Wahoo

Neb."

This old inscription is on a sandstone outcrop along a piece of the 150 year old stagecoach road between Cold Springs Tavern and Paradise Store which was my trail today. This is not the modern Stagecoach Road, but the real one. The first two lines look old judging from the dark lichens in the carving. The bottom line looks more recent and not as careful, but maybe it's just the lighting. It would be an interesting "forensic" project to try to date the inscription by the slow spread of lichens. I guess it dates back to 1900 or earlier. I wonder what happened to Mr. Hockinson who so carefully carved his name? (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

There are other interesting inscriptions on the rocks on the pass that date way back.

File.Name: e109e_e109-024.jpg
Notes:
11862652975 These are the first new flowers I've seen of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. The "ssp. mollis" refers to the long white hairs on the petioles and stems. These flowers aren't quite open, but they will be soon. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

Compare this photo with the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in my [Previous] photo. Note that the stems and petioles of this one are very hairy and the leaves are not blue-green. This shrub has berries with several separate nutlets and grows from a burl. The shrubs are similar but different, and it's still easy to get confused!
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in my [Previous] photo. Note that the stems and petioles of this one are very hairy and the leaves are not blue-green. This shrub has berries with several separate nutlets and grows from a burl. The shrubs are similar but different

File.Name: e109f_e109-020.jpg
Notes:
11862484805 These are old berries on a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. What's missing is any sign of new flowers, and the plants are already flowering elsewhere - see this photo. I guess these are skipping this drought year. These berries are dry, but still rather tasty if you just suck the skins. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2014)

I really posted this one to compare with the Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in my [Next] photo. Note that the stems are almost hairless and the leaves are blue-green. This shrub has berries with one large nut and doesn't grow from a burl, though you can't see that here.

File.Name: e110a_e110-004.jpg
Notes:
11880351024 I've been trying to capture new flowers as the appear in the mountains, but this is an old one. This is a new flower and buds of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family growing along the river. This is a real workhorse of a shrub that just keeps producing new flowers. They are beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

It was very windy last night. I hoped the wind would knock down some Mistletoe (Phoradendron, Viscaceae) at the river so I could finally figure out which one is in this photo, but no such luck. I guess I'll have to go back with a pole saw to get a sample.

File.Name: e110b_e110-002.jpg
Notes:
11880171384 This is a galled and/or fasciated flower head of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I posted a photo of this on November 7, 2013 before the flower buds had opened - see this photo. Now the unusually large flower buds on the stem have opened and mostly finished. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). The lower flower buds seem too large, and the upper flower buds seem too small! This is on a stump-sprouting plant that was burned by the White Fire last May. There are still a few flowers on the shrubs, but no butterflies.
Locality: Isocoma acradenia). The lower flower buds seem too large

File.Name: e110c_e110-016.jpg
Notes:
11879772473 This is a mostly dead-looking tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in this drought year. There is one sprout of green leaves at the bottom of the trunk. The brown leaves at the top are a Mistletoe (Phoradendron, Viscaceae) which is also quite dry and dead. It breaks my heart to see this, and I hope it's not an omen of things to come. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

I got this photo along Paradise Road between the 2nd and 3rd river crossings. This area is especially hard hit by the drought. I showed this same tree in November. It looks worse now.

File.Name: e110d_e110-022.jpg
Notes: 11879839704 These are dead-looking trees of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family growing along Paradise Road between the 2nd and 3rd river crossings. This area is especially hard hit by the drought. It breaks my heart to see this, and I hope it's not an omen of things to come. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

File.Name: e110e_e110-025.jpg
Notes: 11879445613 These are winter-bare trees of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family, with a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) on the left. You can see some water below them if you look close. Standing water is scarce in this drought year, even along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

File.Name: e110f_e110-009.jpg
Notes: 11879366733 Here is a whole flock of seedlings of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. You can see the paddle-like "seed leaves" or cotyledons and the very different true leaves. Shrubs and some perennials are doing OK in this drought year, but annuals like this are scarce. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 January 2014)

File.Name: e111a_e111-017.jpg
Notes:
11898429193 Here is delicate Lace Lichen - aka California Spanish Moss and Treebeard Lichen, my namesake! - Ramalina menziesii, hanging from mostly winter-bare stems of a native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This lichen usually favors deciduous trees like this one. You can see many other lichens on the stems. This lichen favors the valley. I don't think I've ever seen it in the mountains. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 11 January 2014)

*Yipes* We're only at 14% normal rainfall according to the official Santa Barbara County Flood Control District Rainfall and Reservoir Summary today. The percent drops rapidly as we move into what are historically our wettest months, with no rain in the forecast. This has all the makings of being a historic drought, the kind that changes the landscape forever.
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; California Spanish Moss; Treebeard Lichen; Blue Oak

File.Name: e111b_e111-015.jpg
Notes: 11898380934 Here is a "microverse" of tiny lichens growing on a dead branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see a few leaves in the lower-right. I can make out at least three different kinds of lichens, crustose and foliose. I don't know why this evergreen shrub attracts lichens, but it does. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 11 January 2014)

File.Name: e111c_e111-021.jpg
Notes: 11897826865 Here are old galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can make out an "exit hole" made by the wasp which matured inside on the left side of the top gall. When they are fresh, the galls produce real "oak honey" on their surface, which attracts ants and many other insects. See this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 11 January 2014)
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: e111d_e111-011.jpg
Notes: 11897687475 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera resting for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's the first one of these I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2014)

File.Name: e111e_e111-001.jpg
Notes:
11897929004 This is a sad-looking frond of native Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It's the only living frond on a plant with many stems, visible in the background. This drought year is hard on our ferns. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2014)

Compare its relative Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata) in this photo.

File.Name: e111f_e111-004.jpg
Notes: 11898282366 Here is new foliage on green stems of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber, which is a much less harmonious name and harder to remember. I'm happy to see this, because so many of the plants look dead and dry in this drought year. What will the spring butterflies do? (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2014)

File.Name: e112a_e112-013.jpg
Notes:
11920482166 This is a brand new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon. Leaves, petioles, and sepals are very glandular, which separates it from the Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum). I walked the same path yesterday and didn't notice the flowers, yet they were out today. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

This completes our Ribes species on the pass - Chaparral Currant (R. malvaceum), Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (R. speciosum), Canyon Gooseberry (R. californicum), and this one. The is also the White-flowering Current (R. indecorum) in the higher mountains, which I haven't seen yet this year. They're not having a great year, but they are here!

Please don't get the wrong idea from my photos. Wildflowers are not enjoying this drought year. I've lived in these mountains for 40+ years, so I have a good idea where to find the early ones. I'm hunting for these photos every day.
CommonNames: Bitter Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Chaparral Currant; Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; White-flowering Current

File.Name: e112b_e112-001.jpg
Notes: 11920103376 *Sigh* These are new staminate catkins on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see that they are already dropping pollen by the yellow smudge on the leaf below. I'm not surprised the trees are confused as we've had uncommonly warm weather since Christmas in this drought year. Last year, I found the first catkins on February 9 - see this photo. I don't expect the main flowering until March. This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens from the upstairs porch, then cropped. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

File.Name: e112c_e112-007.jpg
Notes: 11919480804 This is a beautiful "skeletonized" leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure if this is the work of insect or fungus, but see this photo. Note that the tip of the leaf is folded over. I'll have a photo of what's inside tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

File.Name: e112d_e112-023.jpg
Notes: 11919097423 This photo is looking closer at new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains. The individual flowers are remarkably intricate, with a deep blue nectar disk in addition to the usual floral parts. If you look close, you can see a few tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects on the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

File.Name: e112e_e112-020.jpg
Notes: 11918888993 These are new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains - with an old seed pod. The plant lives up its species name, with seeds much larger than the flowers. We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains, but this one is distinctive for its white flowers and alternate leaves. I guess many shrubs won't flower in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

File.Name: e112f_e112-015.jpg
Notes: 11918725883 This is a whole plant of native Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It's growing on a rocky hillside in the mountains. See this photo for a closer look at the "mucronate" (with a point) leaflets, and compare this photo" of its relative Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2014)

File.Name: e113a_e113-022.jpg
Notes:
11940072495 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly hanging on the narrow leaves of Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (See my [Previous] two photos.) It's the first one I've seen this year, so no wonder it looks freshly emerged. I didn't see the first one until March 1 last year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2014)

It was breezy again this morning, but the wind finally died down this evening. I hope that's the end of it. Other than the wind, the weather is perfect - and perfectly dry, with humidity down to 9% at Paradise. There is no rain in the forecast.

(See my [Previous] photo.) It's the first one I've seen this year, so no wonder it looks freshly emerged. I didn't see the first one until March 1 last year. See my [Next] photo. File.Name: e113c_e113-025.jpg
Notes:
11940019234 These are the shiny leaves of Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This variety with narrow leaves is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. See my [Next] two photos of a beautiful new butterfly perched on this plant. The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2014)

This plant is listed on the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants as 1B.2 (rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere), but it is pretty common in our mountains. No worries.

File.Name: e113d_e113-006.jpg
Notes: 11939465995 This was almost a lost photo. It's an immature Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with a moth for breakfast. I saw it capture the moth in the open, but it ran under this nearby shrub when I tried to get close. I got this photo through the plant stems. It devoured the moth, wings and all. Cottonmouth? (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2014)

File.Name: e113e_e113-012.jpg
Notes:
11938907795 I believe these are galls of the Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). It looks like critter tried digging in, but apparently the gall larvae form capsules that somehow rise to the surface and fall to the ground to mature.

These are growing on an unusual Scrub Oak which may be Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus parvula. See the discussion with this photo. There's another tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) on the top leaf, but they are Black Oaks with galls that are also found on Coast Live Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2014)

File.Name: e113f_e113-020.jpg
Notes:
11938988324 I believe this is an old furry gall of the Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, or kin. This gall is growing on a dying shrub of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This gall is larger than the similar galls I find on C. spinosus, and I guess it's the work of a different critter. Compare this photo from a different shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2014)

This isn't the first dying shrub I've seen this year. I'm sure the cause is drought stress. I'm afraid we'll see a lot more of this.
CommonNames: Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge; Hairy Ceanothus
ScientificName: Asphondylia ceanothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; C. spinosus

File.Name: e114a_e114-026.jpg
Notes:
11957720346 These are the first new flowers of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae plant family) that I've seen in this drought year. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat the spiny fruit. These plants are "monoecious" with separate male and female flowers on the same plant, and you can see them both in this photo. The upper flowers on the stem are all male or pollen-bearing. The lower flower with the fat bottom is female or seed-bearing. We also have Marah fabaceus which has yellowish flowers and mostly grows further back in the mountains. It usually flowers later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

The wind picked up again in midday, but then died down to a dead calm. It's dry and warm, the relative humidity at Paradise got down to 5% today. This is so wrong for January. It's a wonder that anything is flowering.

File.Name: e114b_e114-014.jpg
Notes: 11957588506 These are new flowers of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The leaves and petioles are quite hairless and without glands, which distinguishes it from the Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in this photo. It is a gooseberry. You can see an old spine in the lower-right. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

File.Name: e114c_e114-011.jpg
Notes: 11957089974 Here are more lovely new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. This shrub was the first native plant to flower, starting way back on the Fall Equinox on September 22 - see this photo. Other shrubs keep starting to flower. I guess this is the longest flowering native plant in our woods, from September until March. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

File.Name: e114d_e114-023.jpg
Notes:
11956857354 This is a new sprout of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae or Agavaceae plant family. The wavy blue-green leaves are distinctive, and distinguish it from Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae aka Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

I have a photo of the same plants in flower last June here, and a closer photo of the flowers here. It's one of those plants that is "rarely common" in our mountains. The plants were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the large hairy bulb messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.
CommonNames: Soap Plant; Amole; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: e114e_e114-008.jpg
Notes:
11956142555 This is a remarkable gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

I've shown these galls before (most recently here), but I don't think I've ever shown this one. They have deepened in color. The galls look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf, but they are really the "nests" of tiny wasps - made by the oaks in a bit of gene or hormone shifting induced by the wasp. It's fascinating that the gall has "points" just like the spines of the leaves. The gall is really part of the oak in a different form, so perhaps I shouldn't be surprised.
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e114f_e114-016.jpg
Notes:
11956872086 This is a tiny bisexual winter gall of one of the tiny Cynipidae wasps of the order Hymenoptera that will mate and produce larger summer galls on our Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia shrubs in the Fagaceae plant family - with an exit hole. The gall is quite small. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The exit hole is about 90 pixels wide in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the hole is 23.4x90/4800 = 0.43 mm. That's really tiny, almost exactly the same size as a different gall in a previous photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)

These wasps have a complicated life cycle. The bisexual winter galls produce male and female wasps which will mate and lay eggs that will produce the unisexual summer galls. The summer galls will hatch only females that produce the bisexual galls that produce males and females, and the cycle repeats. Nature is sometimes complicated like that.

File.Name: e115a_e115-050.jpg
Notes:
11974513904 Lizard love!

These fully mature Blue-belly Lizards aka Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) seem to be snuggling after their mating dance. I'm not sure if they consummated their relationship, but I like to think they did. I saw the whole thing through my viewfinder, and it happened very fast. But this photo may be the proof. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2014)

I was surprised to find these lizards out in the open today, but here they are. They have been dancing under plants of the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that grows by our mailbox. I wished I could reach out and remove the stems in the way, but I didn't want to queer the encounter. See my [Previous] photos.

*Sigh* It was another beautiful warm day. It got up to 86 degrees at Paradise today, with just 5% relative humidity and 2% fuel moisture. I don't know how the trees survive. When will it end?
Locality: Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that grows by our mailbox. I wished I could reach out and remove the stems in the way

I guess that's the male on top doing the biting with deeper blue colors, and the female below with more cyan colors. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2014)

File.Name: e115e_e115-005.jpg
Notes: 11974294836 This is the first new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family that I've seen this year, growing in our canyon. Usually I find the first flowers on the sunny south slopes of the mountains, but not this year. The plants don't look so good in this drought year, and I doubt I'll find many more flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2014)

File.Name: e115f_e115-018.jpg
Notes: 11973807654 This is a rather large Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These shrubs have just started to flower, and I guess the bees were waiting. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2014)

File.Name: e116a_e116-003.jpg
Notes:
11990106584 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This shrub is growing on the edge of our garden, so maybe that's cheating. The pretty sky-blue flowers are growing from a swollen stem that's been galled by a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella, family Cosmopterigidae, in the order Lepidoptera). I've seen this before, where galled flowers appear early, sometimes very early - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2014)

Just four photos today. I had to go to town this morning and didn't get home until after noon. Fortunately that was just in time for these Ceanothus flowers which only catch the sun for about 1/2 hour a day at this time of year. I made a couple of collages of flies which are six photos each. So I really posted 14 photos today!
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: e116b_e116-007.jpg
Notes:
11990247976 This is a tiny Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf above our retaining wall. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo. I prefer not to use flash, but I had no choice in this place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. My first thought was a Fungus Gnat in the family Myceophilidae - which now seems to absorbed in the family Sciaridae of the "infraorder Bibionomorpha" - whatever that means. It's a cute little fly. It must be tough to be a Fungus Gnat when there are no fungi in the drought-dry woods! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2014)

This fly is really tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The fly is about 550 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.4x550/4800 = 2.7 mm, about 1/10th inch.

File.Name: e116c_e1xx-xxx.jpg
Notes: 11989421343 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies, and here are some of the more interesting flies (order Diptera) that I've found recently at my usual morning place in the sun by the creek. Counting clockwise from the upper-left, 1&2 are Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae), 3&4 areSyrphid Flies (family Syrphidae), 5 is a Scavenger Fly (family Sarcophagidae), and I'm not sure about 6. (Anthomyiidae?) (San Marcos Pass, January 2014)
CommonNames: Bee Flies; Syrphid Flies; Scavenger Fly

File.Name: e116d_e1xx-xxx.jpg
Notes: 11988965805 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds! This is a collage of Fly (Diptera) photos that I've taken recently at my usual morning place in the sun by the creek. I'm sure some of these are the same kind, but I won't try to guess. They are just flies! (San Marcos Pass, January 2014)

File.Name: e117a_e117-018.jpg
Notes:
12005048483 This is wonderful new foliage of a California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, sprouting like a mushroom on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. There are quite a few of these plants coming up now, but little else. The soil is dry and powdery. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)

I hiked along the "river trail" again today. It runs through a section of the Santa Ynez River that was burned by the White Fire. Actually it's been burned twice - by the fire and by the drought. There are few seedlings in this drought year, but it's a pleasure to see these perennials.

"Christmas in July" is a fine 1940 comedy film by Preston Sturges. We're having "Summer in January". I was at Paradise today, and according to their weather station it got to 86∞ with just 4% RH and 2% fuel moisture where I was. This isn't right.

File.Name: e117b_e117-012.jpg
Notes: 12004674625 I'm not sure, but this looks like native Salt Grass (Distichlis spicata) in the Poacea plant family, growing on charred soil after the White Fire last May. At least I hope it's not some horrible invasive weed! I'm not sure what it's doing here on this dry flat on the river wash, but here it is. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)

File.Name: e117c_e117-027.jpg
Notes: 12004315575 These are plants of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plant in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae on a dry flat by the Santa Ynez River, burned by the White Fire last May. The small ones look dead, but if you look close you can spot a couple of seedlings in the foreground. Apparently I never photographed this particular cluster just after the wildfire, but this photo from July 26 is similar. The younger plants have all died, but the large one is hanging on. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: e117d_e117-023.jpg
Notes: 12004613734 Here is new life on the charred trunk of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's nice to see after the White Fire burned this place last May. Sadly, many other trees aren't sprouting at all. Wildfire and drought is a tough combination. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)

File.Name: e117e_e117-009.jpg
Notes: 12004119655 This is a limb-sprouting tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's nice to see after the White Fire burned this place last May. Sadly, many other trees aren't sprouting at all. Wildfire and drought is a tough combination. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)

File.Name: e117f_e117-028.jpg
Notes: 12004022395 These are large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. They are growing on the desert-like river wash of the Santa Ynez River that was burned by the White Fire last May. They are making some recovery with limb-sprouts, despite the drought. See my [Next] two photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2014)

File.Name: e118a_e118-005.jpg
Notes:
12022243473 This is looking close at the surprisingly intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers related to sassafras and avocado. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens, with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo of the flowers in this place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)

*Sigh* It was another "Summer in January" day. It's getting hard to find new photos at home in this drought year.

File.Name: e118b_e118-022.jpg
Notes:
12021928473 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of a native tree of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. I've seen a few trees in the woods with buds, but no flowers. This one is growing on the edge of our garden where it gets some summer water, and I guess that makes all the difference in a drought year. This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens from the upstairs porch. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)

It's also growing in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I waited some time for a filtered ray of sunshine to show up. "We were only waiting for this moment to arrive." See my [Next] photo for a closer look of the flowers, with flash.
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; "We were only waiting for this moment to arrive.
associatedTaxa: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; "We were only waiting for this moment to arrive.

File.Name: e118c_e118-002.jpg
Notes:
12022238806 These are the first new flowers I've see this year of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's growing on the dry side of our garden where it gets a bit of summer water, and I guess that makes the difference. Plants is the wild aren't looking so good in this drought year, though I have seen a few buds. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)

This plant growing in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, so I got this photo with a tripod and long exposure. I know, the white balance isn't quite right, though these flowers really are magenta rather than red. It's interesting how the eyes (and brain) automatically adjust white balance so well, but in a photo the results are often hideous. It's a stumper why our eyes can't adjust to a photo as well as the real scene.
Locality: and brain) automatically adjust white balance so well

File.Name: e118d_e118-012.jpg
Notes: 12021477723 I'm pretty sure this is a galled stem of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It's been galled by a Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaca) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves also look different above the gall, with teeth rather than smooth like below. Offhand, nearly 1/3 of the stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge

File.Name: e118e_e118-016.jpg
Notes: 12021288094 So that's why they call this shrub Greenbark Ceanothus! This is a stump-sprouting native Ceanothus spinosus in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's also known as Red Heart for the deep-red wood in the burl that is prized by local woodworkers, but I guess I don't have a photo. (I should!) See this recent photo for the pretty blue flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)

File.Name: e118f_e118-014.jpg
Notes: 12021114184 Here is another photo of the elegant seed pods of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriatus - in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are long gone and the seed pods are empty, but they are quite beautiful. See this photo from October. Usually the old stalks are beaten down by the rain by now, but not in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: e119a_e119-007.jpg
Notes:
12042453825 This is a handsome Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a leaf of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. The butterfly seemed very attached to the plant. I got this photo, then went for a walk, and found it on the same plant when I came back. See my [Previous] photo. I didn't see it, but I wonder if she was ovipositing? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

The butterfly looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it.

Today was cloudier, cooler, and breezier than it's been. I figure any change in the weather is a good sign in this drought year! I'll be computer-bound for the next couple of days putting together the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. The archives are in good shape, so I'll be able to keep posting.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

Notes:
12042602794 This is a handsome Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. The butterfly seemed very attached to the flowers. I took my [Next] photo first, then went for a walk, and found it on the same plant when I came back. I didn't see it, but I wonder if she was ovipositing? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

The butterfly looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it.

File.Name: e119c_e119-010.jpg
Notes: 12041955043 These are new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves, like hard taco shells - but these leaves have been deformed by an infection that sure looks like "Peach Leaf Curl". I guess it's a fungus, maybe a different Taphrina species? The buds will wait on the plant for a couple more months to flower, until next March or April. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

File.Name: e119d_e119-002.jpg
Notes: 12042363836 This is the same bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house that I've photographed before. See this photo from Christmas Day, almost a month ago. She's still guarding her egg-sack, and she will until spring. She does occasionally change sides, for exercise? I used my on-camera flash for this photo where the sun never shines, using "Slow Sync" flash. It adds a bit of sparkle to the photo without overpowering it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

File.Name: e119e_e119-029.jpg
Notes: 12041494923 Life is resilient! Even in this drought year, this native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family has managed at least one rather deformed flower. Compare this photo of what the flowers should look like, and this one showing lots of them. The usually shiny leaves look pale, and this is the only flower I found on the shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

File.Name: e119f_e119-033.jpg
Notes: 12041089675 This is what most of our trees of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family look like, still in bud. Compare my [Previous] two photos of trees flowering on the edge of our garden. Actually many trees don't even have buds in this drought year. On the other hand, I did find a tree flowering without help near our mailboxes today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

File.Name: e120a_e119-025.jpg
Notes:
12062077015 I guess this is a knobby gall of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). It's growing on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaf beyond the gall is still alive, and that's said to be a characteristic of these galls, but compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

I was dutifully working on the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter today (I'm the editor), when it slowly dawned on me that this not a deadline job. I double-checked with the president of the society. Hurray, I can put it off for a few more days and look for new photos tomorrow! It is nice to have a start on the work, and it was breezy today anyway.

Notes: 12061854415 I guess this is a knobby gall of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). It's growing on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like a peanut! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

File.Name: e120c_e119-030.jpg
Notes:
12062542056 Here is a tiny ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) crawling on a gall of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis). This gall is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see a fresh exit hole just to the right of the ant's head. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2014)

I got sharper photos of the gall, but the ant was a bonus!
CommonNames: ant; Round Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: e120d_e114-019.jpg
Notes: 12061717953 This is another photo of a small stem gall on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. See this photo (and story) from last November. Now there is no doubt that it's a gall, maybe of the "Dried Peach Gall Wasp" (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2014)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Dried Peach Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e120e_7728-074.jpg
Notes:
12061682996 This is a galled stem of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family on Santa Cruz Island way back in 2007. These galls may be caused by a moth in the genus Mompha, family Momphidae, Lepidoptera. I don't recall ever seeing them in the mountains, but I have a vague recollection of finding them on the mainland by the coast. (Pozo Canyon, Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

I was going through old photos today looking for pictures of the islands for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter today (I'm the editor), and happened to notice this old photo. It's worth sharing on an all-gall day. Darn, I haven't been out to the islands in a few years.
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Mompha; Momphidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Mompha; Momphidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: e120f_e118-019.jpg
Notes:
12060706095 This is a "rolled leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. There's a critter in there, but is it a gall or a mechanically rolled leaf? The leaf looks discolored like a gall, but I'm still not sure if this counts as a gall. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2014)

"Leaf rollers" and "gall insects" really have a similar strategy - to have the plant provide food and shelter. The more I think about it, the more similar they seem. Both kinds of insect get shelter and food from the host plant. And how can one folded leaf provide all the sustenance that an insect larvae needs without some help? Now that I think about it, how does the insect fold the leaf UP rather than DOWN in its natural direction? Is it a gall?

File.Name: e121a_e121-021.jpg
Notes:
12078307144 These are bristly new berries-to-be of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are doing surprisingly well in this drought year! (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)

It was cloudy today, but the soft light was becoming for photos. *Sigh* No rain is expected for the next 10 days. I went up into the higher mountains today looking for new flowers, and I found them.

File.Name: e121b_e121-025.jpg
Notes: 12077766165 This is an abundance of new and old flowers of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are doing surprisingly well in this drought year! I love the way they hang in rows from the spiny stems. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)

File.Name: e121c_e121-029.jpg
Notes: 12078032844 These are the first new flowers that I've found this year of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. The old Spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry! (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)

File.Name: e121d_e121-018.jpg
Notes: 12077489515 These are the first flowers I've found this year of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Most plants in our mountains aren't looking so good in this drought year, but these plants not too far from Arroyo Burro Saddle higher in the mountains look almost normal. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)

File.Name: e121e_e121-007.jpg
Notes: 12077326065 These are new female pistillate flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family, the first flowers I've seen this year. I shot this photo "high-key" (over-exposed) against the cloudy-bright sky. I like the way it turned out. When I first learned my plants, this winter form was known as B. viminea and the summer form was B. glutinosa, but now they are considered the same. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)
ScientificName: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; B. viminea; B. glutinosa
associatedTaxa: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; B. viminea; B. glutinosa

File.Name: e121f_e121-014.jpg
Notes: 12077559104 This small native shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family is covered with flowers! The plants that flowered first - as early as September 22! - are done, but other plants keep starting. This is one of our longest flowering natives, from September to April on different plants in different years - seven months! (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2014)

File.Name: e122a_e122-022.jpg
Notes:
12095293993 This is another handsome Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly resting on the first joint of my index finger! I chased it for a while, and then it landed on my finger. It stayed there for quite some time, while I walked to a nearby rock for stability and got my tripod flat with my other hand. I got the photo, sharp enough! It's one of few times I've ever wanted to use auto-focus. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

I found the first of these butterflies a few days ago, see this photo. I guess that butterfly wasn't a fluke. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it.

I went back up the Aliso Canyon trail today. Nothing is flowering, and very little is sprouting. This combination of wildfire and drought is a tough one for the woods.

File.Name: e122b_e122-027.jpg
Notes:
12095122144 This is native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family, coming back after the White Fire came through last May. I wouldn't recognize it, except that I knew it was here. See this photo of another plant before the fire. Compare this photo from a few weeks ago, and compare my [Previous] photo of a different native grass. They have the same sort of burned "stump" - though this one is a foot or more across, and the other one is only a few inches across. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

This grass does grow on San Marcos Pass in a few secluded places, but it's more common in the interior backcountry. It likes a well-watered spot by a creek, but not in the creek. It was an important plant for native American basketry, and it's widely used as a landscaping plant.

File.Name: e122c_e122-005.jpg
Notes: 12094583185 This is a different native grass in the Poacea plant family from the one I show in my [Next] photo, though both are "stump-sprouting" after being burned by the White Fire last May. Both grasses have similar black "stumps". This whole plant is only about 3 inches across rather than a foot or more, and the new leaves are thin. If I had to guess, I would figure it's Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua). Time will tell. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

File.Name: e122d_e122-017.jpg
Notes: 12094796644 This is a wonderful native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plant in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. It was burned by the White Fire last May, but this one looks like a survivor. I love that "grass skirt" of dead leaves. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: e122e_e122-014.jpg
Notes: 12094945936 These are burned plants of native Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla) in the Labiatae plant family, with a burned hillside in the background after the White Fire last May. It's hard to photograph a drought after a wildfire. It's hard to show what isn't there. But look - there are no stump-sprouts, no seedlings, no life, it's still basically a moonscape. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

File.Name: e122f_e122-003.jpg
Notes: 12094256343 This is the only water hole in Aliso Canyon still holding water, as far as I know. Everything else is dry in this drought year. The spring is situated where a geological fault crosses the canyon. It's a great spot for native Stream Orchids (Epipactis gigantea), see this photo taken a couple of weeks before the White Fire took everything out. I hope the orchids come back, but there's no sign yet. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

File.Name: e123a_e123-006.jpg
Notes:
12110455435 This is a tiny native ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) crawling over an old gall next to an acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I originally thought the galls were aborted acorns like the one to the right with a visible pistil, but they are probably the galls of Clasping Twig Gall Wasps (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. When they are fresh they produce real "oak honey" on their surface, which may explain why the ant is here. I was just trying to photograph the galls when the ant walked into my photo. Lucky photo! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2014)

It's getting hard to find new photos every day at home in this drought year, but I'll keep trying as long as I can!
CommonNames: ant; Scrub Oak; Clasping Twig Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e123b_e123-013.jpg
Notes:
12110641603 These are knobby galls of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). They are growing on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. This must be a gall wasp hotel! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2014)

This is the time of year when I mostly find these "integral stem galls" on the scrub oaks. It makes sense, because the oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once later in the spring, then get new leaves at about the same time. A leaf gall wouldn't have a chance to mature, and I guess they know the schedule.
CommonNames: Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e123c_e123-001.jpg
Notes: 12110276095 These are gnarly cones, dripping with resin, on a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. It's a three-needle pine. The huge cones can weigh as much as 10 pounds, according to Wikipedia. I don't recommend camping under one of these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2014)

File.Name: e123d_e123-011.jpg
Notes: 12110120775 These are still-new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family with the sun behind, which shows off the pretty shredding bark on the stems. The flowers just keep coming on one plant or another, even in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2014)

File.Name: e123e_e123-019.jpg
Notes: 12110644966 Here are cheerful yellow flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I can't call them "first flowers" because they never seem to stop flowering, at least *somewhere* in the mountains, even in this drought year. This shrub is covered with new flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 January 2014)

File.Name: e123f_e122-008.jpg
Notes: 12110135863 This is a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family "stump-sprouting" after being burned by the White Fire last May. Most of the burned trees are not sprouting like this, though a few are "limb-sprouting". See this photo. Most of the trees just look dead, though many still have singed leaves. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 January 2014)

File.Name: e124a_e124-002.jpg
Notes:
12126800996 This is looking "under the skirts" of a new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. It has a purple heart. The red "petals" are really sepals, and the true petals are the while skirts. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2014)

Today was a gloomy day - cloudy and breezy. I did my best for new photos. I'll be busy tomorrow with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter which I've been putting off as long as I can. I probably won't get outside, but its time for a catch-up day.

File.Name: e124b_e124-013.jpg
Notes: 12126458114 This is an exploring new stem of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae plant family), with grasping tendrils. They are scarce in this drought year. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat the spiny fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2014)

File.Name: e124c_e124-016.jpg
Notes: 12126188713 These are only the second new flowers I've seen this year on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. They have pretty little urn-flowers, like those fancy easter eggs that you can look into. This shrub grows from a burl and has berries with several separate nutlets. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2014)

File.Name: e124d_e124-006.jpg
Notes: 12125773175 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of a non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) shrub on the dry edge of our garden. These aromatic shrubs are one of the few that the deer haven't eaten to the ground this year. I know it's not a great photo, but it clearly shows how pollination happens. The bee lands on the lower limb of the flower, which brings the stamens and pistil down like a fulcrum to touch its back. I guess the bee is after nectar, but it also has full pollen baskets. Just sit back a few feet when you look at it! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2014)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Rosmarinus officinalis; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Rosmarinus officinalis; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e124e_e121-003.jpg
Notes: 12126204756 This is a little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a new flower of a non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) shrub on the dry edge of our garden. These aromatic shrubs are one of the few that the deer haven't eaten to the ground this year. I know it's not a great photo, but it clearly shows the fly getting a snootful of pollen. Just stand back a few feet! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2014)

File.Name: e124f_e123-009.jpg
Notes: 12125791264 These are odd "ear-flap galls" (my name) on a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They form on the upper side of the leaves. I don't know what critter causes these, and it's not in my book. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2014)

File.Name: e125a_e107-029.jpg
Notes:
12143203655 I believe this is the tiny stalked egg of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) insect in the insect order Neuroptera. They build them on stilts like this so the first larvae to hatch don't eat the others. They are voracious predators. I found this one is on the tip of a spine on a leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. We've been seeing lots of small lacewings on the window screens at night lately. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2014)

I did my duty today, and the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society is all done. I never got outside. Of course I'll let it rest for the night and look again tomorrow. It was too windy for photos today anyway. It's a chance to show a few photos (just three) that almost got lost in the archive.

File.Name: e125b_e106-008.jpg
Notes: 12143371943 This is the trail of some leaf-miner insect on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It has an add "happy face" pattern at the bottom. I have no idea what critter came out of this. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2014)

File.Name: e125c_e106-010.jpg
Notes: 12143696256 This is a neat fungus pattern on a green leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can almost see it growing outwards like a fairy ring from a central spot. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2014)

File.Name: e126a_e126-011.jpg
Notes:
12163222564 These are new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - with raindrops! The curled up flower bud below the flowers is especially handsome. I've been seeing more of these since my first flower on January 15. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

We had a few sprinkles today - not even enough to settle the dust, but it does the soul some good!

File.Name: e126b_e126-009.jpg
Notes: 12162969593 These are the soft flower heads of white-stemmed Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceaeplant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. The red color is from the leafy bracts. The real flowers are the yellow-green tubes, with an exerted pistil. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

File.Name: e126c_e126-027.jpg
Notes: 12162538565 This is looking close at the surprisingly intricate flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers are like little origami boxes. The dark treasure inside is a nectar disk in addition to the usual floral parts. We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains. This one is distinctive for its white flowers and alternate leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

File.Name: e126d_e126-024.jpg
Notes: 12162427525 This is a mass of flowers on a shrub of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains. I guess many shrubs won't flower in this drought year, but in a good year they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. It's good to find at least a few shrubs doing their stuff. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

File.Name: e126e_e126-019.jpg
Notes: 12162663884 This is a pretty plant of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae plant family) growing along a neighbor's fence. Most of the flowers are staminate or pollen-bearing, but you can see a few of the new spiny fruit on the bottom coming from the less common pistillate flowers. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

File.Name: e126f_e126-004.jpg
Notes: 12161917115 This is new foliage of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see old flower stalks on the plant. The pretty summer flowers begin in late July, see this photo, and go until November. It was a gloomy day, but the gloom helps bring out the colors in a muted sort of way. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2014)

File.Name: e127a_e127-021.jpg
Notes:
12182093983 These are tiny Planthopper insects in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Auchenorrhyncha of the Hemiptera. They are meeting on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I guess they might be family Issidae because of their resemblance to this critter, but they are certainly different. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)

These critters are quite small. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The planthoppers are about 700 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the planthopper is 23.4x700/4800 = 3.4 mm, about 1/8th inch.

The rest of my photos today are plant galls. When there's nothing else in the woods to photograph, there are always galls!
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Issidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Issidae

File.Name: e127b_e127-024.jpg
Notes:
12181905643 This is a tiny gall on the vein of a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I don't expect to find galls on this shrub, but here it is. It might be the gall of a Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)

This gall is really tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The gall is about 260 pixels high in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the gall is 23.4x260/4800 = 1.3 mm, about 1/2 of 1/10th inch.
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: e127c_e127-009.jpg
Notes: 12181503125 This is a new gall of the green form of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is a compromise exposure between too bright and too dark. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: e127d_e127-014.jpg
Notes: 12181401025 This is a fresh-looking gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks fresh, but it already seems to have an exit hole at the base - or is that an entrance hole where some predator got in? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: e127e_e127-017.jpg
Notes: 12181329705 This is another mature "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It has a wonderful texture. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e127f_e127-018.jpg
Notes: 12181242385 These are old galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - with exit holes where the critter got out. The galls are growing on a dead-looking twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)
ScientificName: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e128a_e128-007.jpg
Notes:
12198504406 This is an extraordinary new flower head of an Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family. It looks interesting, but it's extraordinary because it's flowering at all in this drought year. This is the first perennial I've seen in flower this year, all my other "first flowers" have been shrubs or subshrubs. It's not only first in flower, this - and the plant in my [Previous] photo - are the only plants I've seen this year. It looks a bit bug-eaten. I'm not surprised, with any flowers so scarce this year. It shows how resilient life is! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2014)

These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. They must have a fungal connection with their host plant, and maybe that's why they can flower when nothing else can.

I found this plant yesterday, but I didn't like my photos taken in the shade. I went back today with a sturdier low-level tripod, and even got a bit of filtered sunlight - and darned if the photo isn't quite sharp. *Sigh* I had other errands to run in the valley today, including delivering the the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to the printers. Another job done.

File.Name: e128b_e128-009.jpg
Notes: 12198387366 This is a new plant of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family. It's not flowering yet, but it does have flower-colored leaf bracts. I put an old berry of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family in the photo for scale. The berry is about 3/8 inch across, which means the whole plant is less than an inch across. I only noticed it because it was growing next to the flowering plant in my [Next] photo. Life is tough! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2014)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: e128c_e128-002.jpg
Notes: 12198228806 This photo is sad or encouraging depending how you look at it. It's the first - and so far only - flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in our woods in this drought year. It's sad because it looks so poorly, but it's encouraging because it shows how tough life is. The blackberry brambles are not looking good, but they managed this flower! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2014)

File.Name: e128d_e127-003.jpg
Notes:
12197876734 This is another photo of an infestation of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Eriosomatinae, Aphididae, Homoptera). They are producing masses of waxy wool on leaves of this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see lots of the tiny critters if you look close. The population has grown since my last photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)

There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF). This appears to be a document worth downloading.
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae; Aphididae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae; Aphididae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e128e_e128-014.jpg
Notes: 12197616013 These are pretty lichens growing on a dead branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe the orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Maybe that lichen is the Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) - but there are so many others! They make a community on this dead twig. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 January 2014)
CommonNames: lichens; Scrub Oak; Gold Eye Lichen
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus

File.Name: e128f_e127-013.jpg
Notes: 12197638414 These are pretty lichens growing on dead branches at the base of a Scrub Oak in the mountains - maybe Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus pacifica. See the discussion with this photo. How many different lichens do you see? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2014)

File.Name: e129a_e129-002.jpg
Notes:
12214208844 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly trying to warm up this morning on a sunny leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. I don't feel confident identifying skippers, but this may well be the relatively large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens from the upstairs porch. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2014)

Today was another nice day, though the winds kicked up early and put an end to my photos. We actually have a 20% chance of rain tomorrow. We'll see what happens.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: e129b_e129-020.jpg
Notes: 12213900753 I believe this is a new plant of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing on scorched soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. Most of the new life I've seen here in this drought year are shrubs and perennials, but you can tell that this one is an annual seedling by the seed leaves at the base of the stem. Maybe we will have a spring despite the drought! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2014)

File.Name: e129c_e129-029.jpg
Notes:
12213464195 These are new shoots of Wild Cucumber (genus Marah) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing from bone-dry soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. The stems arise mushroom-like from bare ground with tendrils ready to grab on to anything. I'm not sure what species this is - Marah macrocarpus or Marah fabaceus. Time will tell. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2014)

I believe that's a stem of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family in the background. The twisted stem and peeling bark are distinctive.
ScientificName: Marah; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Marah; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: e129d_e129-032.jpg
Notes: 12213663264 Here is the first new growth I've seen this year of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family, stump-sprouting in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. It looks positively dripping with skin-irritating oil. Pretty though! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2014)

File.Name: e129e_e129-022.jpg
Notes: 12213481634 This is the charred femur or thigh bone of a deer on burned soil after the White Fire last May. I don't think it was caught by the wildfire, but I suspect a mountain lion used to hang out here. See this photo from nearby. The place is a small island between two river channels. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2014)

File.Name: e129f_e129-010.jpg
Notes: 12213579556 This is a wall of winter-dry Cattails (family Typhaceae) at what's left of the river. They are starting to release their fluffy seeds. I've never learned cattails, but maybe these are the Narrowleaf Cattail (Typha angustifolia)? This is a rare place on the Santa Ynez River that still has water. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2014)

File.Name: e130a_e130-014.jpg
Notes:
12229433014 Surprise! These are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I was surprised to find them at all in this drought year. Life is resilient! It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but I don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. Last year I found the first flowers on February 1, so I guess they are right on schedule - despite the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)

It was gloomy and drizzly today. I stayed home and walked along East Camino Cielo where I found this flower. It wasn't the drought-buster rain we need. We ended up with half a tick more than 1/100th inch.

File.Name: e130b_e130-002.jpg
Notes:
12228669255 These are tiny native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera), three of them if you look close, tending aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new flower buds (with drizzle-drops) of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Each flower bud has an ant taking care of the aphids on that stem. The ants place the aphids on the flower stems and protect them from predators. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)

This is the same shrub in our garden that I showed in flower in this photo. It's growing on the edge of our garden, but I've also seen a few wild shrubs in flower along the highway to Santa Barbara. They are starting.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e130c_e130-006.jpg
Notes: 12228968864 This little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is working flowers of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) along the top of our retaining wall. Don't believe the rumor that hummers only go for red flowers. I'll never forget the day years ago when a hummer stuck its bill up Julie's nose! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)

File.Name: e130d_e130-010.jpg
Notes: 12228583063 This is the hammock-like spider web of a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae, with drizzle-drops today. It's growing in a barely-alive plant of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. This drought year has been tough for both the spiders and the deerweed. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: e130e_e130-018.jpg
Notes: 12228487053 This is an uncommon shrub of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. It's a rarity in our mountains behind Santa Barbara. I only find it in a few shady places like this where the sun never shines, and I've been worried about it in this drought year. It actually looks pretty good, but there are no flower buds yet, and it may skip this year. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that has managed to hold on so far against climate change. (The climate has been changing since the end of the Pleistocene!) (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)

File.Name: e130f_e130-007.jpg
Notes: 12228636394 This is a group of small saplings of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family, across from our mailbox. They are beautiful, and so far hanging on in this drought year. I got this photo on a gloomy day with some drizzle. The low light suits the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2014)

File.Name: e131a_e131-012.jpg
Notes:
12244033914 This is a new sprout of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - the first I've seen in this drought year. It's now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family - those darned botanists! This plant is no doubt var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. See this photo. It looks a lot like native Soap Plant at this stage, but that plant has wavy leaves and these are quite flat. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2014)

It was cold and windy today, in the 30s last night. I've almost forgotten what it's like with the summer weather we've been having lately! It was a crummy day for photos, but I got a few.

I checked the Santa Barbara Hydrology web page this morning to check on our rainfall yesterday. They say 0.0" but I measured 1/100" in our gauge. I was shocked to discover that our rainfall this rain year stands at just 10% of normal. This is a historic drought.
