CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: e131b_e131-007.jpg
Notes:
12244051426 This is a sad-looking small shrub of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It's showing some stress in this drought year. That's an interesting native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family at the bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2014)

Believe me, I'd rather be photographing healthy plants and butterflies! But it seems almost a duty to document our drought. We stand at just 10% of normal rainfall, and it's having an impact.
ScientificName: Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Galium porrigens; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Galium porrigens; Rubiaceae

File.Name: e131c_e131-006.jpg
Notes: 12243749384 These are sad-looking plants of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They are not looking good in this drought year, and I don't expect many flowers. Compare this photo of a plant on the edge of our garden where it gets occasional water. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2014)

File.Name: e131d_e131-003.jpg
Notes:
12243038705 I guess these bumps on the leaves are "Erineum Galls" caused by tiny Live Oak Erineum Mites (Eriophyes mackiei) in the family Eriophyidae of the order Acarina. I found them today on leaves of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. There are rusty-brown hairy pockets underneath which correspond to the bumps on top. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2014)

These galls are especially interesting because the bumps on the right seem to break through the leaf. I'm not sure what this means.
CommonNames: Erineum Galls; Live Oak Erineum Mites; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Eriophyes mackiei; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Eriophyes mackiei; Eriophyidae; Acarina; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e131e_e129-005.jpg
Notes: 12243248314 This is looking close at an infestation of Woolly Oak Aphids (Stegophylla, Eriosomatinae, Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's like looking at an alien ecology, but I got this photo on a tree from the upstairs porch. (I carefully hung my tripod off the porch to get this photo!) I'm not sure what I'm looking at. The adult aphid on top seems to be blowing bubbles out of its tail, I'll add a note. Maybe it's making more waxy filaments? (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2014)
CommonNames: Woolly Oak Aphids; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e131f_e129-003.jpg
Notes:
12242929704 These are "rolled leaves" on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure if they are galls or mechanically rolled leaves. It's interesting that they roll against the natural curvature of the leaf, which makes me think that they are real galls. "Leaf rollers" and "gall insects" really have a similar strategy - to have the plant provide food and shelter. The more I think about it, the more similar they seem. This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens from the upstairs porch. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2014)

I'm not sure if these are made by the same critter as this photo as they look smaller and skinnier. The rolled leaves in this photo are clearly different as they roll the other way, under rather than over the leaf.

File.Name: e201a_e201-029.jpg
Notes:
12261637563 These are pretty new flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, from a plant that is not in our garden. See my [Previous] photo. I've seen a few other shrubs in flower along Highway 154, but always in places I don't care to walk. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)

A bit more rain is likely tomorrow! Not much, but it sounds like we're in the wet place and might get 1/4 to 1/2 inch. I emptied the rain gauge. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: e201b_e201-005.jpg
Notes: 12261261805 This is looking close at new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, from a plant that is not in our garden. The surprisingly intricate flowers look like perfect little origami boxes. It was breezy today and close photos were impossible. This is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)

File.Name: e201c_e201-036.jpg
Notes: 12261287623 These are new glandular flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family, growing on the dry side of our garden. They are finally getting some sunlight. The sun is noticeably higher in the sky than it was a month ago, but it still only shines here for a brief time in the afternoon. Plants in the wild aren't looking so good, see this photo from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)

File.Name: e201d_e201-020.jpg
Notes:
12261092814 This is the view from the ridge above Paradise Canyon today, looking towards Santa Cruz Island past an old forest service planting of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. The trees were planted a century ago, and they've done OK until now.

I guess the trees are being hit by Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). It's interesting that the damage is all on the side of the grove that the winds usually blow towards. I like how the ocean looks like ice! (It's a bit over-exposed to bring out the trees.) I reckon the high point on the island is Picacho del Diablo, the highest mountain at 2470 feet. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)
ScientificName: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: e201e_e201-014.jpg
Notes: 12260962016 I guess these are "Erineum Galls" caused by tiny Mites in the family Eriophyidae of the order Acarina on the underside of a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. Compare my photo yesterday of mite galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Those bumps go up, but these go down. Like my other photo, some of these galls have grown through the leaves. Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)
CommonNames: Erineum Galls; Mites; Toyon; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Eriophyidae; Acarina; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Eriophyidae; Acarina; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e201f_e201-007.jpg
Notes:
12260684104 This is non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. The stems are tough and wiry, but at least the seeds don't stick in your socks. It's impossible to pull by hand from the dry ground. I reckon this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. This photo shows an infestation along a trail in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2014)

I reckon these are old seed heads, but I'm worried that these plants are greening up even in this drought year. Drought can be an important player in the spread of weeds, when the natives don't have a chance.
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: e202a_e202-003.jpg
Notes:
12279545573 Here are brand new male staminate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family in the canyon at home. They weren't here a couple of days ago when I was here last. The sun even broke through the clouds for a moment to let me get this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)

It was mostly cold and gloomy today. I wasn't expecting to get any new photos, but I manged to fill my quota. And we got (almost) 1/4 inch of real much-needed rain. It's not the drought-buster rain we need, but it's something. Another band of rain seems to be moving down the coast, so I hope we'll get more. It's been cold, in the mid-30s all afternoon.
Locality: almost) 1/4 inch of real much-needed rain.  It's not the drought-buster rain we need

File.Name: e202b_e202-007.jpg
Notes: 12279835166 Here is beautiful foliage of perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon. They are waiting for rain to fill the cups of their palmate leaves, see this photo! They don't look too bad in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)

File.Name: e202c_e202-009.jpg
Notes: 12279561324 This is a lovely native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family that's growing almost horizontally down by the creek. The polished brown wood is remarkable. Gloomy days bring out the beauty of these fine trees. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)

File.Name: e202d_e202-013.jpg
Notes:
12278979655 Here is a group of galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see lots of exit holes. These galls are really wasp hotels! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)

I don't usually find these galls in clusters. They are different from the more common "oak apples" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in this photo because they favor Coast Live Oak (a "black oak") instead of Valley or Scrub Oak (both "white oaks").
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus

File.Name: e202e_e202-006.jpg
Notes: 12279263006 This is a tiny gall on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I don't expect to find galls on this shrub, but here it is. It might be the gall of a Tube Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia salviae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, but cp. the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) in this photo. This gall has straighter sides than the other one and it looks different. It's said to be more common on the stems. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Tube Gall Midge; Sage Leaf Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia salviae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhopalomyia audibertiae
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia salviae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhopalomyia audibertiae

File.Name: e202f_e202-005.jpg
Notes: 12279036374 This is a sad-looking shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. They are not looking good in this drought year, and I don't expect many flowers. Maybe the 1/4 inch of rain today (so far) will help, but it needs more than that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2014)

File.Name: e203a_e203-042.jpg
Notes:
12298502833 This is looking close at the head of the first large hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, formerly included in A. columbianus) that I've seen in almost exactly a year. See my [Previous] photo. The longer tentacles in front are eyestalks (the one on the right is withdrawn) with sensitive dark "pupils" and the lower pair are feelers and tasters. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. Our little storm with less than 1/4 inch of rain yesterday was enough to bring them out. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

How in the heck do these slugs survive from one year to the next, especially in this drought year? Do they dehydrate into almost nothing, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time. I only found this one today.

I was really looking for early mushrooms or slime molds today, but no such luck. This is the year without mushrooms. We ended up with just 0.23 inches of rain. We stand at just 11% of normal rainfall.

File.Name: e203b_e203-051.jpg
Notes:
12298352854 This is the first large hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, formerly included in A. columbianus) that I've seen in almost exactly a year. See this photo from last year. These slugs are 6-8 inches long. Our little storm with less than 1/4 inch of rain yesterday was enough to bring them out. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

How in the heck do these slugs survive from one year to the next, especially in this drought year? Do they dehydrate into almost nothing, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time. I only found this one today.

File.Name: e203c_e203-031.jpg
Notes:
12298215576 I should apologize for the photo on the left that isn't sharp, but it's the sequence that's interesting. I got both photos today, about 2 hours apart, on the same day. These are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants that shrivel up when it's dry and come back when it rains - and they've already shriveled up after our 1/4 inch of rain yesterday! I don't believe these are the same California Liverworts (Asterella californica) that I show in my [Previous] photo, though there is a resemblance. These are much smaller and more sensitive to drying out. They are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

The little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the right was just a bonus today!
CommonNames: Liverwort; California Liverworts; Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard
ScientificName: sequence; same day; Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; Sceloporus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: sequence; same day; Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; Sceloporus occidentalis

File.Name: e203d_e203-010.jpg
Notes: 12297986884 Here are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I'm pretty sure they are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). They're coming alive after our (scant) 1/4 inch of rain yesterday. See this photo of them with umbrella-like sporophytes last year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

File.Name: e203e_e203-034.jpg
Notes:
12297706324 Here are new female pistillate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. Compare my [Previous] photo of the male flowers. Notice that there are native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on the catkin in the upper-right. These trees are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. I'm *sure* these willow flowers (both male and female) produce nectar to attract insects. The flowers appear to be wind-pollinated, but I reckon the insects help. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

These flowers were a stretch for my tripod, growing pretty high in the tree. This is a tight crop.
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; dioecious

File.Name: e203f_e203-024.jpg
Notes: 12297388253 Here is another photo of new male staminate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. Compare my [Next] photo of the female flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

File.Name: e204a_e204-008.jpg
Notes:
12312930605 This is the same bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our roof that I've shown a few times before - here, here, and here. She's been sitting on her nest since November, but she did something different today. The spider hung down from her web and spun around. Then she climbed back up and did it again and again, like she was exercising. After this she went back to the nest, but when I came back in the afternoon, she was gone. I suspected the eggs were starting to hatch, but I couldn't see any activity. Blurry as it, I like this photo. It's the spider that's spinning around, not me! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)

It's not a bad photo day when I get a spider, insects, deer, mushrooms, wildflowers, and a gall all in the same day!

File.Name: e204b_e204-009.jpg
Notes: 12313234264 These are tiny native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new flower buds Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Every flower bud in the shrub seems to have aphids and ants tending them. That ant on top of the flower bud seems to be threatening me! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e204c_e204-014.jpg
Notes: 12312757963 This is a beautiful young Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) near the road by our mailbox. I guess this is one of last spring's fawns, not quite a yearling. I got this photo hand-held, using the car door for support. I don't know how these wild deer survive in this drought year, but eating our garden must help! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)

File.Name: e204d_e204-023.jpg
Notes: 12312227625 I found mushrooms in the woods today, after our small 1/4 inch storm two days ago! They're just LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - but I'm not complaining. I guess they are Collybia dryophila, though smaller than usual, only about 1/2 inch across. Could that be a response to the dry weather we've been having?. Note the thick root-like rhizomes growing from the base. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)

File.Name: e204e_e204-021.jpg
Notes:
12312273013 This is another very small flowering plant of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family. It's extraordinary because it's flowering at all in this drought year. It shows how resilient life is! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)

These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. They must have a fungal connection with their host plant, and maybe that's why they can flower when nothing else can.

I found half a dozen of these plants with red bracts but no flowers yet. There's an old berry of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family just to the right of the base of the plant. The berries are about 3/8 inch across, which means the whole plant is little more than an inch across. For some reason, I find it hard to get a sharp photo of these plants, even with my low (but sturdy) tripod.
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family just to the right of the base of the plant. The berries are about 3/8 inch across
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: e204f_e204-017.jpg
Notes: 12311975135 This is a old thin-walled and hollowed-out "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, no doubt fallen from a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like an egg shell! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2014)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e205a_e205-020.jpg
Notes:
12333876223 Here are (already) the first flower catkins of a native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family! It seem like they just dropped their leaves, and here they are getting new ones. See this photo from December 27. I don't expect them to flower for at least another month. I guess this is another result of our drought year - trees get confused. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

Hah - I can see a couple of fat insect galls on the catkins! I'll add a note. I'll try to get back for a closer photo of the gall, and this will give me chance to figure out what it is!

It's been cold, we had frost on the upstairs porch this morning. There's a 90% chance of more rain tomorrow, but it doesn't sound like we'll get much. At least it will keep the ground damp and maybe bring up a few more mushrooms.

File.Name: e205b_e205-009.jpg
Notes: 12333498175 This is the burned-out trunk of a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family, burned by the White Fire last May. It sure looks like it tried to stump-sprout and failed, but maybe the brown leaves at the bottom are just winter-dormant. (I don't think so.) (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

File.Name: e205c_e205-012.jpg
Notes: 12333339555 Here are new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns in the Polypodiaceae plant family arising from a slide of Monterrey Shale rock. Yep, I was surprised to find them in this unlikely place, but here they are. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

File.Name: e205d_e203-032.jpg
Notes:
12333317424 There are (at least) three things to notice in this photo of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family.

First, notice the neat little boat-like seed pods from last year on the left. Second, notice that the flower stalk is fat and there's a new leaf coming out that has bumpy rather than smooth edges. I believe it's been galled by a Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaca) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. Compare this photo. And third, notice the composite seed stuck to a leaf on the right. I believe it's the seed of a native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family, see this photo! Has it been stuck to this leaf since last July? That's one thing about a drought year - there's no heavy rain to wash things away. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

Look close - there are always things to find!
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge; Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaca; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Agoseris grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaca; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Agoseris grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: e205e_e203-021.jpg
Notes:
12332465045 These are (I guess) galled berries of a native shrub of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The normal berries turned blue-black and were gobbled up by birds months ago, but these galled berries are still on the shrubs long after their season. They all have an odd "crook in the neck" like these. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one.

I actually wonder if they aren't galled flowers not berries. They look something like the galled flowers of this plant's cousin Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub - see this photo.
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus ilicifolia
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus ilicifolia

File.Name: e205f_e203-015.jpg
Notes: 12332487153 Here is another photo of a tiny straight-sided gall on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe it's the gall of a Tube Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia salviae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. See this photo. It's said to be more common on the stems, but I've only found them on leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia salviae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia salviae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: e206a_e206-001.jpg
Notes:
12353559143 It's always fun to try to photograph the smallest mushroom! This tiny 'shroom is growing on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale, so the "mushroom" isn't much more than 1 mm across. I have no idea what kind it is. I wish the photo was sharper, but this is the best I could do on a gloomy day. This was the only photo I got today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2014)

Note the raindrops on my pen! It was drizzly all day, with episodes of real rain. We've had an honest 1/3 inch so far. This is actually our biggest storm in two months in this drought year! I'm hoping for real mushrooms tomorrow.

File.Name: e206b_e203-008.jpg
Notes: 12353291955 This is another lovely flower and new berry of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. You can see the little red-tipped glands that are the hallmark of this species. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

File.Name: e206c_e205-004.jpg
Notes:
12353486234 These are new sprouts of monocot plants on burned soil from the White Fire last May. I'm pretty sure they are native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. There are quite a few of them and not much of anything else. I guess these plants that grow from bulbs get a boost in this drought year? (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

Note the small annual weed at the base of the flower on the left. I'm sure it's a non-native Filaree (Erodium) weed. They grow everywhere.

File.Name: e206e_e203-004.jpg
Notes: 12353287914 This is a sharper photo of the same tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants that I showed a few days ago in this photo. I got this photo in the morning, and two hours later they were shriveled up and black. I don't believe these are the same California Liverworts (Asterella californica) that I show in this photo, though there is a resemblance. These plants are much smaller and more sensitive to drying out. They are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2014)

File.Name: e206e_e205-015.jpg
Notes: 12353056854 These are grand native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family that was burned by the White Fire last May. It seems odd to me that they still have a full canopy of singed leaves, and they don't have a sign of new growth. Maybe it takes a living tree to drop leaves? Sure it's sad, but the trees have a lovely autumnal color. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

File.Name: e206f_e205-019.jpg
Notes: 12352942034 This is looking up into the canopy of a grand native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family that is leafing out and starting to flower. See my [Previous] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2014)

File.Name: e207a_e207-010.jpg
Notes:
12375514304 This is a large hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, formerly included in A. columbianus) munching a seedling plant along the road in the canyon. These large native slugs are 6-8 inches long. You can see it's "pneumostome" - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this on the slug-right side, so you can't see it from this photo taken from the left. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)

I guess the seedling plant is a weedy (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae). I found this slug about 10 feet from the slug in my previous photo, and again it was the only one I saw. I hope it's not the only one that survived our drought year.

We ended up with just over 4/10 inch of rain from our storm yesterday - not a lot, but it feels wonderful in this drought year! We have a slight chance of a bit more rain tomorrow. It's gloomy enough tonight.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: e207b_e207-002.jpg
Notes: 12374950165 Here is another photo of tiny native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new flower buds of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with raindrops. Every cluster of flowers has it's own group of ants. The ants place the aphids on the flower stems and protect them from predators. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e207c_e207-012.jpg
Notes:
12374673575 Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know! These are new leaves of a perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon still holding raindrops from last night. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)

I've been doing everything the same as ever, but my recent photos have a sharpness problem. It only seems to happen when my tripod legs are spread wide, so I reckon it's time for a new tripod. My old Gitzo explorer tripod is held together with epoxy and pony clamps! I'm considering a Manfrotto 055 carbon fiber model. Please let me know if you have a reason why I shouldn't buy this!

File.Name: e207d_e207-019.jpg
Notes:
12374488974 These are crustose lichens on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They show the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. The fungi are no doubt little "cup fungi" in the Ascomycetes. I can also make out a few tiny black "carbon fungi" on the left edge of this photo. It's a beautiful scene. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)

I have no doubt that these "mushrooms" have been out for a while. But I was looking for mushrooms today after our rain yesterday, and I happened to notice them.
CommonNames: crustose lichens; Coast Live Oak; Ascomycetes

File.Name: e207e_e207-025.jpg
Notes:
12373889245 This is a strange Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing in the woods. They are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. The old rinds last in the woods for years, but I suspect this is a new one with remnants of the inner spore sack still attached. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)

It's surrounded by seedlings that are looking happy after this break in our drought year. At about the 10 o'clock position you can make out a seedling Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Portulacaceae).

File.Name: e207f_e207-026.jpg
Notes: 12373715655 These are mosses and lichens on a sandstone boulder along our driveway. The woods are greening up after our recent rains. This rain was no drought-buster, but we got a bit over 4/10 inch yesterday, and it helps. How quickly these plants respond! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2014)

File.Name: e208a_e208-004.jpg
Notes:
12398036784 This is a large winged "alate" Ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on our upstairs deck railing this morning. They have clean, beautiful lines. I don't care to guess what kind of ant it is - these adult winged forms all look very similar. They are not strong fliers. This ant tried to take off (photo tomorrow) and fell to the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

Julie said that "it looks like a classic rain day today, except that there's no rain." I could see water droplets in the air, and we did get a trace in the rain gauge. We have a better chance of rain tonight and tomorrow night.

File.Name: e208b_e208-012.jpg
Notes:
12397895104 Here is yet another photo of tiny native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new flower buds of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with raindrops. Every cluster of flowers has it's own group of ants. The ants place the aphids on the flower stems and protect them from predators. In exchange, they collect honeydew from the aphids. I guess it's a kind of husbandry, beneficial to both. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

I've been posting quite a few similar photos recently, but once the flowers open they vanish from view. I figure I better get them while I can.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e208c_e208-010.jpg
Notes: 12397221505 These are fronds of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. They're not having a great time in this drought year, but the recent small rains have helped them. They are hanging on. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

File.Name: e208d_e208-023.jpg
Notes:
12397345944 This is a frond of a native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing in the woods. A few of the leaflets are turned over so you can see the powdery "gold backs" that they have. You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

My recent photos - including this one - have a sharpness problem. In my triage for the problem, I'll start with a new tripod, I'm considering a Manfrotto 055 carbon fiber model. Then comes lens; camera; and (*gulp*) me!

File.Name: e208e_e208-025.jpg
Notes:
12396588073 I believe this is a "Dog-Lichen" in the genus Peltigera. You can several of the typical tooth-like structures along the edge of the lichen on top. These are apothecia, the fruiting bodies of the fungal part of the lichen. They are interesting because many Peltigera species are cyanolichens (having a cyanobacteria symbiont), but some Peltigera sp. have only an algae symbiont, and others have both. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

This one is growing on the ground, with mosses. These lichens are uncommon in our mountains. They really belong in Oregon. I only know them from this one place, and you have to get down on your hands and knees to see them. The photo isn't as sharp as I like, but you can see it well enough.

If you look on the right side of the main lichen body you can see a tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida)! I'll add a note. I don't know what kind of tick or what it's doing here, but here it is! This is the first tick I've seen this year.
Locality: superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida)! I'll add a note. I don't know what kind of tick or what it's doing here

File.Name: e208f_e208-001.jpg
Notes: 12396763744 These are lichen-encrusted branches of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, shot from the upstairs porch. Our deck is like a tree house. The lichens have been digging the rain! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

File.Name: e209a_e209-013.jpg
Notes:
12422703055 These are tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves in the woods today. Note the long dark brown stems coming from the right. Rain revives these mushrooms, so they may have been around for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2014)

These are actually the first new mushrooms I've found in the canyon in this drought year! I hope there will be more. We had a small storm last night with 0.06" of drizzle, and there is 50% chance of a bit more rain tonight. We've only had 2.5 inches of rain since September, just 13% of normal, so even small storms are welcome.
ScientificName: Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; first
associatedTaxa: Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; first

File.Name: e209b_e209-004.jpg
Notes: 12422373905 Here are raindrops on flowers of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae), reflecting the woods. We had just the right kind of drizzle last night for raindrops, before it got windy and blew them all away. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2014)

File.Name: e209c_e209-017.jpg
Notes: 12422689374 This small native Moss (Bryophyta) has skewered tiny raindrops with its immature sporophytes, with wonderful reflections. How do you skewer a raindrop? (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2014)

File.Name: e209d_e209-023.jpg
Notes: 12422426974 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. Those little tongue-like structures with tiny bumps on the leaves are the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which will splash out in the rain when mature. Some plants produce them, others don't. I guess it's always good to have a backup! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2014)

File.Name: e209e_e209-006.jpg
Notes: 12421919793 This is a group of tiny Pyxie Cup (Cladonia) lichens growing on a sandstone boulder in the canyon. The fruticose stalks come from a foliose base. A few of the stalks have raindrop caps, and you can see a few new stalks forming in the top-left. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2014)

File.Name: e209f_e208-003.jpg
Notes: 12421615085 This is another photo of the large winged "alate" Ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on our upstairs deck railing yesterday morning. See my [Previous] photo. It's preparing to take wing, but it didn't get very far - about 12 feet straight down, near as I could tell! I don't care to guess what kind of ant it is - these adult winged forms all look very similar. Yeah, it's not quite sharp, but it captures the moment. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2014)

File.Name: e210a_e210-012.jpg
Notes:
12447556655 This is a handsome little Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera working new flowers of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. You can see its long proboscis fully extended. See my [Previous] photo. I found several different flies on these flowers this morning. The flowers don't smell like carrion! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)

Today started gloomy and windy, and ended up sunny and windy. Our little "storm" last night produced just a trace of drizzle and more wind than rain. I managed to get a few new photos.

File.Name: e210b_e210-013.jpg
Notes: 12447281325 This is a handsome little Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera working new flowers of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. See my [Next] photo. I found quite a few different flies on these flowers this morning. The flowers don't smell like carrion! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)

File.Name: e210c_e210-007.jpg
Notes: 12447006125 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) working a new flower of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. At least it's working the flowers and not the trash! It may well be a another Muscidae - or is it something more interesting? (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)

File.Name: e210d_e210-002.jpg
Notes: 12446911193 This is a furry honeybee-size Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae) in the order Diptera, perched on a leaf of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. Maybe it's the species Brachypalpus alopex in the subfamily Eristalinae? It was interested in the flowers, but it's quite camera shy, and flew off just after I snapped this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Crassula ovata; Crassulaceae; Brachypalpus alopex; Eristalinae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Crassula ovata; Crassulaceae; Brachypalpus alopex; Eristalinae

File.Name: e210e_e210-019.jpg
Notes: 12446557575 Here are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family, growing among the weedy grasses along East Camino Cielo road. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but I don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)

File.Name: e210f_e210-017.jpg
Notes: 12446547473 This is a colony of native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a mossy bank in the woods. At least this is what passes for mossy in this drought year! See this photo. You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2014)

File.Name: e211a_e211-008.jpg
Notes:
12470039075 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking right at me from from behind a succulent leaf of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. They have fantastic eyes. It's remarkable to make eye contact with any critter so small! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)

This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4800x3200 original photo, taken back a ways. It wouldn't let me get any closer. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year.

It was very windy last night, but the winds died down to normal levels today. The winds have really dried out the woods. *Sigh* It feels like high pressure is back in control.

File.Name: e211b_e211-002.jpg
Notes: 12470345884 This is a large Ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) getting sweet nectar from a new flower of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. I'm pretty sure this is a Carpenter Ant in the genus Camponotus. They are better on the flowers than eating our house! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crassula ovata; Crassulaceae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crassula ovata; Crassulaceae; Camponotus

File.Name: e211c_e211-009.jpg
Notes: 12469514005 This is a Turkey Vulture (Cathartes aura) bird perched on a branch by the start of my trail today. You can see its bare red head. The white balance is a bit too warm, but even so I'm impressed how brown this large bird is as they look quite black when they soar overhead. I reckon someone tossed a dead pet down the hillside at this place along Stagecoach Road. I could smell it. These are birds that scavenge the dead. It's an important job in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)

File.Name: e211d_e211-014.jpg
Notes: 12469460163 This is another beautiful flowering plant of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. I used to think they had a fungal connection to the shrub, but Mark Egger set me straight. The plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. See the discussion with this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; haustoria

File.Name: e211e_e211-013.jpg
Notes: 12469265193 I believe this is a native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, growing from a crack in solid sandstone bedrock. The stems are not jointed like a grass. It's a remarkably tough sedge. I think of them as wetland plants, but this one proves otherwise. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)

File.Name: e211f_e211-022.jpg
Notes: 12469076293 I believe these are small Rock Tripe Lichens in the genus Umbilicaria growing on a sandstone rock face. They are not common in our mountains. I don't know anything about them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2014)

File.Name: e212a_e212-025.jpg
Notes:
12491662443 This is a solid new flower bud of beautiful California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, growing on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. They are fire followers, but I guess they were already here under the trees and shrubs just waiting for some sun. Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora, Orobanchaceae) has a similar strategy. See this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

I'll probably miss the open flowers. Julie and I are heading up to Oregon on Friday to visit the grandkids and other family matters. I normally would refuse to go at this time of year, but with the drought I welcome the change! I'll be busy with errands and packing tomorrow, but I can (at least) post a few more from the recent archives.

associatedTaxa: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae; Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: e212b_e212-003.jpg
Notes: 12491287795 This is a small succulent insect gall growing on a new catkin of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can make out a couple of the galls in this recent photo of the flowers. I don't know whose gall it is. It's interesting that the catkins emerge through the gall tissue. I've also found tiny galls on Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but they look very different. See this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

File.Name: e212c_e212-021.jpg
Notes: 12490917283 I noticed this orange fungus growing over bare soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. I put my pen in the photo for scale. I saw something similar after the Gap Fire back in 2009, see this photo from the archives at Fotolog. I reckon "Fire Fungi" would be a good name for this fungus. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

File.Name: e212d_e212-007.jpg
Notes: 12490663215 This is the fantastic new foliage - and flower buds! - of Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with those long pinnate leaves. It looks tropical. This plant used to be known as a locoweed, but there's nothing about it that will get you high or crazy. It is beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

File.Name: e212e_e212-018.jpg
Notes: 12490462895 This is another new plant of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing on scorched soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. Most of the new life I've seen here in this drought year are shrubs and perennials, but you can tell that this one is an annual seedling by the seed leaves at the base of the stem. There are other natives on the bare soil, but you have to hunt for them all. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

File.Name: e212f_e212-016.jpg
Notes: 12490384333 This is looking at a bit of the river bed that was burned by the White Fire last May. This place is an "island" with oaks between river channels. It looks pretty bare. The combination of wildfire and drought has really taken a toll this year. There are a few green plants on the charred soil, but I feel cheated this year - this should be a carpet of wildflowers by now! I don't know if the trees will come back, as some of the early limb-sprouts have died. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2014)

File.Name: e213a_e213-013.jpg
Notes:
12511699325 Spider love! Just count the legs and divide by two...

I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transfering genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigyne. It's a tender moment.. and complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2014)

[Spoiler: Explicit Sexual Details Follow!] Mating is difficult for these preditors who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. The smaller male spider is on the left, and the larger female is higher behind. Both are hanging upside down. The male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates his spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. You can see the dark inflated spermaphores on the end of the leg-like palps in this photo. The female's epigyne opening to the right is ready to accept the sperm package. Do I have this right RJ?

*Sigh* It got up to 84∞ today at Paradise, where I was yesterday. It feels like summer in what should be our rainiest month, but the drought continues. I saw a Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly today, looking for flowers. Good luck, little butterfly!

I wasn't expecting to get any new photos today, but I got a few. Julie and I are heading up to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to visit the grandkids and other family matters. I normally would refuse to go at this time of year, but with the drought I welcome the change. We're driving up the east side of the Sierras, and I'm worried about snow. I'll be back in a few weeks.

File.Name: e213b_e213-022.jpg
Notes:
12511588033 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of non-native Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden. I haven't seen these little bees for some time. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2014)

If you look close, you can make out a tiny orange Springtail (order Collembola) insect hiding in the flower! I'll add a note.

File.Name: e213c_e213-003.jpg
Notes: 12511169455 This is a tiny pollen-dusted Fly (order Diptera) working a staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. I guess from its round head and flattened proboscis that it is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - or are they all predators? This is not the best photo, in the shade and breeze today, but it's good enough to see the critter! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2014)

File.Name: e213d_e213-005.jpg
Notes:
12511308054 I was trying to get a photo of the small fly on a catkin in my [Next] photo today when I noticed these tiny caterpillars on a nearby leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. They all seem to be making poop, sitting at the same angle. It's an odd line! I have no idea what they will become. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The caterpillars are about 280 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of these critters is 23.4x260/4800 = 1.4 mm, about 1/2 of 1/10th inch. Ain't life grand!

File.Name: e213e_e213-016.jpg
Notes: 12510835923 This is a group of little galls on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be the galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? The size is right, but the outsides are more interesting than those usually are. That hole is too big to be an exit hole. I reckon something got in to consume the gall larva. Is it still in there? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2014)

File.Name: e213f_e213-018.jpg
Notes: 12510607853 Here are lovely little lichens growing on a dead branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I don't know why this evergreen shrub attracts lichens, but it does. I guess the orange caps are apothecia, the fruiting bodies of the fungal part of the lichen. They are hanging on in this drought year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2014)

File.Name: e303a_e303-012.jpg
Notes:
12917363065 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a well-camouflaged White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth resting on dead leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Compare this photo. I don't know what "moth flowers" it's looking for, but whatever they are, they're not blooming in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2014)

We got back from our Oregon trip last night - and totally missed the great rains last week. We ended up with 8.36 inches of rain at home, sometimes quite heavy. That brings our total to 10.89 inches for the rain year, but still only 41% of normal. The drought is not over.

Oregon is also having drought even though it rained a bit almost every day we were there. As of the end of February, Portland had 13.64 inches of rain out of an average of 22.52 inches, and Eugene had 14.49 inches with an average of 30.35 inches - according to local newspapers. We're not too far off. The difference is that our rain came all at once instead of day-after-day of drizzle. We had more rain than Portland or Eugene this month - all in the last five days!

I'll have more Oregon trip photos tomorrow, and hopefully a new photo of the day.

File.Name: e303b_e228-020.jpg
Notes:
12917001395 This is a brand new flower of the Western Trillium (Trillium ovatum) in the Liliaceae plant family, with raindrops. They are also known as "Wake Robin" because they flower so early. The petals look like wet tissue paper. Everything comes in threes with these flowers - three leaves, three sepals, three petals, three styles, and 3+3=6 stamens. This is the first of these flowers I found in the Oregon woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

It's time to get back in the woods Curtis! I also found mushrooms and lichens. More Oregon trip photos tomorrow.

File.Name: e303c_e228-028.jpg
Notes: 12917306494 I believe this is the Snow Queen (Synthyris reniformis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantaginaceae. (Those darn botanists!) The blue or lavender flowers - sometimes pure white - mostly have two stamens. They are one of the first wildflowers in the Oregon woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

File.Name: e303d_e227-008.jpg
Notes: 12916676125 This is a pretty new flower of (I guess) Smooth Yellow Violet (Viola glabella) in the Violaceae plant family, with raindrops. They are one of the first wildflowers in the Oregon woods. I know the local Santa Barbara wildflowers pretty well, but I'm always guessing on these Oregon flowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)

File.Name: e303e_e226-019.jpg
Notes: 12916622223 These are new flowers of Western Coltsfoot (Petasites frigidus var. palmatus) in the Asteraceae plant family in the Oregon woods. It's an early flowering perennial that comes up like a bulb in wet places. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e303f_e226-025.jpg
Notes: 12916787004 This is a flowering shrub of native Oso Berry (Oemleria cerasiformis) in the Rosaceae plant family growing with mosses and ferns by a small creek in the Oregon woods. I have a closer shot of the white flowers here. The shrub is said to grow in Refugio Pass in the Santa Ynez Mountains back at home, though I haven't noticed it yet. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e304a_e304-012.jpg
Notes:
12939431255 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a beautiful dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus or Coprinopsis) mushroom coming up along our road. It's quite large - maybe 2 inches across. The caps are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2014)

The rest of my photos today are from Oregon, but this one is from home. Mushrooms in our Santa Barbara mountains have been almost as scarce as hen's teeth in this drought year. I've only found a few this year, and we should have 100s of different species. I hope their mycelium is still ok. We just had a good rain, so there's still hope.

File.Name: e304b_e226-012.jpg
Notes:
12939378524 I believe these are the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis) in the Oregon woods last week. They are growing on decomposing wood. They are said to be edible but *please* don't take my word for it! They are rather thin-fleshed, so it would take a lot of mushrooms in better shape than these to make a meal. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

This is another mushroom that I found last fall - see this photo. I found all of my Oregon mushrooms today last fall.

File.Name: e304c_e226-005.jpg
Notes:
12939200704 Here is a group of Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum) in the Oregon woods. Instead of gills or pores, they have spines or "teeth" to distribute their spores. They are delicious when cooled in a dry sautÈ. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

I found this same mushroom last fall - see this photo, though these spring mushrooms look paler. I found all of my Oregon mushrooms today last fall.

File.Name: e304d_e227-005.jpg
Notes: 12938849114 These are tiny white mushrooms growing on a dead mossy branch of native Vine Maple (Acer circinatum, Aceraceae) in the Oregon woods last week. Maybe they are Marasmiellus candidus or kin? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)

File.Name: e304e_e228-032.jpg
Notes:
12938407973 I believe this is the "Hooded False Morel" (Gyromitra infula or kin) growing from dead wood in the Oregon woods. It's quite large, almost fist-size, and has a lovely chocolate color. I first thought genus Helvella, but I think this is right. It's said to be quite poisonous, with MMH (monomethylhydrazine) - rocket fuel! Admire it, but don't eat it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

I found this same mushroom last fall - see this photo. I found all of my Oregon mushrooms today last fall.

File.Name: e304f_e227-014.jpg
Notes:
12938501664 This is a colony of Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa or kin) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. I pulled the one specimen to show the fluted stem. They are said to be edible when cooked, but I've never felt inclined to try them, and they have dangerous relatives - see my [Next] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)

It's curious that I also found all of the Oregon mushrooms I show today when we were last up here at Thanksgiving. I'm not sure what this means. Fall is definitely the time for mushrooms in the Oregon woods, but there are a few hangers-on.

File.Name: e305a_e305-016.jpg
Notes:
12960638804 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched (for just a moment) on a dead grass stem in our canyon. Its host plant is growing nearby, native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2014)

I first saw this wonderful butterfly two days ago. I went back today and got the shot.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever

File.Name: e305b_e225-011.jpg
Notes: 12960028963 This is a lovely photo of a little Mycena mushroom growing from a mossy log under a canopy of Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the Oregon woods. See my [Previous] photo for more info on moss sex - it's complicated! This is probably the same moss I showed before, as I can see a few female archegonia stalks to the left of the mushroom. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 February 2014)

File.Name: e305c_e227-010.jpg
Notes:
12959542393 This is a native Moss (Bryophyta) in the Oregon woods showing its sexual parts. Moss sex is complicated and I can't say that I understand it. It's been too many years since I took biology! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)

I believe the leafy stems with brown tops are the male antheridia and the bare stalks with green tops are the female archegonia. The males produce sperm cells which swim through a film of water to the female parts, where they form a zygote which grows to form a sporophyte. This grows essentially as a parasite on the mother plant that makes the typical "moss stalk" we can recognize. (See my [Next] photo, which may well be the same sort of moss.) The spores disperse and make algae-like protonema which produce new moss-like plants, and the cycle repeats.

Do I have this right?
ScientificName: Bryophyta; antheridia; archegonia; zygote; sporophyte; protonema
associatedTaxa: Bryophyta; antheridia; archegonia; zygote; sporophyte; protonema

File.Name: e305d_e227-003.jpg
Notes: 12959158925 I believe this is a giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods. The colors are different, and they have a strong ridge along the back that our California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) seem to lack - compare this photo. They are beautiful, in their own sluggish way. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slugs

File.Name: e305e_e226-016.jpg
Notes: 12959017253 This is the work of "Madrone Leaf Blight" on a sapling of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. See my [Previous] photo for more info and links. The infestation starts as dark blotches on the new leaves which spread until the entire leaf is dead. Fortunately the trees recover later in the year as new leaves emerge. Apparently this is a new blight, first noticed in 2009. The prime suspect is the fungus Phacidiopycnis washingtonensis, but it's my impression that the specific cause in still unknown. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e305f_e228-004.jpg
Notes:
12959045914 This is the work of a "leaf blight" on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. The good news is that the trees recover in the summer, see this photo for a before and after shot. Apparently this is a new blight, first noticed in 2009. The prime suspect is the fungus Phacidiopycnis washingtonensis, but it's my impression that the specific cause in still unknown. This is NOT caused by drought. This most beautiful of trees should not look like it's dying! (Wright Creek Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

There's more info here (PDF) from Washington State University, or try a google search on madrone blight oregon.

File.Name: e306a_e305-012.jpg
Notes:
12981286243 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a rather large Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera hanging on new leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. Hah - I think it's actually sitting on the leaf at the bottom! Its proboscis is sclerotized and quite rigid. It sat still for some time and let me get close. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2014)

*Sigh* It's been very windy today, and its been breezy all week. This is a drying wind, just what we don't need after our first real rains in a drought year.

File.Name: e306b_e228-036.jpg
Notes: 12981363534 I guess this is some kind of Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera growing on a fallen branch in the Oregon woods. It's quite beautiful. The orange pads are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. (Wright Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

File.Name: e306c_e226-014.jpg
Notes: 12980618903 I found this interesting lichen in the Oregon woods last week - maybe a Tube Lichen in the genus Hypogymnia? The green or brown pads are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. There are more different lichens (and a moss) in this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e306d_e228-010.jpg
Notes: 12980679184 These are a "British Soldiers" type of lichen in the Oregon woods - maybe the Toy Soldiers Lichen (Cladonia bellidiflora) with "squamules on the podetia". I think that means that the stalks are shaggy. I believe the red caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. (Wright Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

File.Name: e306e_e226-013.jpg
Notes: 12980164605 I believe this is the Oregon Lung Lobaria aka Lettuce Lichen (Lobaria oregana) in the Oregon woods. It should be growing up in the trees, but I found this piece on the ground. There are many other lichens visible in this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e306f_e226-011.jpg
Notes: 12979682995 This is an interesting Lichen in the Oregon woods. It seems to have roots, but this is a lichen and not a vascular plant. I believe this is a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, maybe the Frog Pelt Lichen (Peltigera polydactylon). The "roots" are really holdfasts for this lichen which grows on soil. We have a very similar lichen at home, maybe the same one - see this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)
CommonNames: Lichen; Pelt Lichen; Frog Pelt Lichen

File.Name: e307a_e307-018.jpg
Notes:
13000846835 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

These are the first new flowers of well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains at home this year. I guess there are different species of this plant everywhere in the west. It's been a tough year for wildflowers with the drought. This plant is growing in a place where the sun never shines, and the recent rains must have helped. We might have a spring after all! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2014)

I have a few more Oregon trip photos I want to post, then I'll be done with that. The winds died down today. There are still few mushrooms in the woods, so maybe their time is past. I'll always remember this as the year without mushrooms. It is good to see a few new flowers!

File.Name: e307b_e227-004.jpg
Notes: 13000617485 These are impressive large mushrooms in the pink-spored Entolomataceae family of mushrooms in the Oregon woods, maybe genus Nolanea with those barely-visible umbonate caps, like little pointy hats. The gills are definitely "pink" - or what passes for pink among mushroom hunters. They may well be poisonous. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 February 2014)

File.Name: e307c_e228-002.jpg
Notes: 13000640334 I believe these are Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood in the Oregon woods. The wood is buried and invisible, but I'm sure it's down there under the layer of fallen leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. This is one mushroom I didn't find last autumn. (Baker Wayside, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Bigleaf Maple
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

File.Name: e307d_e226-008.jpg
Notes: 13000014613 This is a neat clump of lichen growing on a conifer trunk in the Oregon woods, maybe Sphaerophorus tuckermanii? (Thanks Curtis!) I might be wrong. I don't live in Oregon yet, so I don't have to get it right - but I'd like to! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e307e_e226-007.jpg
Notes: 12999701985 I guess this is a different Tube Lichen (maybe genus Hypogymnia?) in the Oregon woods. Compare yesterday's photo. They look similar except for the brown tips of the stems. These brown pads are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Lichens are classified according to their fungal parts, so I reckon this is an important difference. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 February 2014)

File.Name: e307f_e228-011.jpg
Notes: 12999787434 I believe this is Witch's Hair Lichen (Alectoria sarmentosa) shrouding deciduous trees in the Oregon woods last week. It's a "Treebeard" lichen - my namesake! It's similar to the Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) that shrouds the oaks at home, see this photo. The details of these lichens are different, but it's interesting that both favor deciduous trees. I guess it's because they let some sunlight in during the rainy part of the year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 February 2014)

File.Name: e308a_e308-032.jpg
Notes:
13023431284 These look for all the world like little puffball fungi, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). Something different comes from their spores! I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrumor kin. They are about the size of fresh peas. These are growing on a fallen log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March and 8 March 2014)

I got these two photos just two days apart - on March 6 (left) and March 8 (right). I guess that's another difference with puffballs - they change so fast! See this photo for more info about slime molds.

I figure I've posted enough Oregon photos for one trip. I'll save a few for a windy day. We had perfect weather today - sunny, warm in the 70s, not too breezy. It's time to get back to our local woods full time. That's the trouble with trips - you show trip photos and then have to make up the home photos! I'll get there.
