File.Name: e308b_e308-023.jpg
Notes:
13022792645 This is looking close at the surprisingly intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are related to sassafras and avocado. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)

They should have flowered back in January, see this photo of a native tree on the edge of our garden where it gets a bit of extra water. But this clever tree waited until we got a bit of real rain. Not all plants can do that. It's late but welcome.

File.Name: e308c_e308-027.jpg
Notes: 13023066224 These are new flower heads of white-stemmed Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. The red color is from the leafy bracts. The real flowers are the yellow-green tubes, with an exerted pistil. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. It seems to be getting new life after our rains a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)

File.Name: e308d_e308-016.jpg
Notes: 13022465335 Here are pretty new leaves and flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Look at the stalk on the middle leaf on the upper-right and compare my [Previous] photo of native Basket Bush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata). It looks positively dripping with skin-irritating oil. Pretty though! Many plants are deciding not to flower in this drought year. I had to look close to find this one with flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)
CommonNames: Poison Oak; Basket Bush; Squaw Bush

File.Name: e308e_e308-007.jpg
Notes:
13022300235 These are the first new flowers of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. (At least the first I've seen - I've been away in Oregon.) (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)

This plant was important to the native Chumash people for several kinds of their fabulous baskets. This shrub looks a bit like Poison Oak with those 3-part leaves, but the leaves are dull not shiny, the flowers are different, and the middle leaf grows down to the base without a separate stalk. Compare my [Next] photo. This plant is not irritating to most people.
CommonNames: Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush; Poison Oak

File.Name: e308f_e308-008.jpg
Notes: 13022174713 I've been waiting since October for these flower buds of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family to open and spread pollen. See this photo from October 5. It seems they've finally given up in this drought year. The buds have turned gray without releasing pollen. Compare this photo from a happier year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)

File.Name: e309a_e309-008.jpg
Notes:
13048082474 "Yipes, I'm formicating!"

Said properly, the word "formication" means "the sensation that insects or snakes are crawling over the skin". It's a difficult word to say properly in public!

I believe these are California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae of the Formicidae in the Hymenoptera. They are worker ants that were tending their reproductive "alate" or winged forms earlier, but the winged forms are all gone now. See this photo from earlier in the morning. There seems to be an inexhaustible colony in our walls, as the ants cover at least 20 square feet of deck and walls when they are swarming like this. They bite and sting! *Sigh* The things we do for photos! These ants are vicious, and the only thing to do is to strip down, get a few quick photos, and run to the shower with dozens or hundreds of them biting my legs. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2014)

It was cloudy and breezy today. Not the best day for photos, but I got a few. Now for the new Cosmos. I miss the voice of Carl Sagen already...
ScientificName: Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Cosmos
associatedTaxa: Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Cosmos

File.Name: e309b_e309-005.jpg
Notes:
13047344285 The ants are totally in control of the far corner of our upstairs porch!

I believe the ants are California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae of the Formicidae in the Hymenoptera. These are worker ants tending their reproductive "alate" or winged forms. There seems to be an inexhaustible colony in our walls, as the ants cover at least 20 square feet of deck and walls when they are swarming like this. They bite and sting! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2014)

*Sigh* The things we do for photos! These ants are vicious, and the only thing to do is to strip down, get a few quick photos, and run to the shower with dozens or hundreds of them biting my legs. We've also had feeding streams of these ants across the living room floor to get old cat food and garbage. Many are now entombed in our vacuum and drain. This invasion happens every year, and it only lasts for a week or two at mating time. They are well-behaved for the rest of the year. We can live with that.

File.Name: e309c_e308-035.jpg
Notes:
13047192334 This is a native ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a non-flowering branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. There are a couple of large "lumps" below the aphids that I believe are Scale Insects (Coccoidea). The ants seem to be tending them too. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2014)

I believe the ant is a California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. This is the same kind of ant that is invading our house right now, but this one is better behaved. See my [Next] two photos.

I like this photo because it's a glimpse of the complex ecology on our local shrubs. I'd like it even better if I'd managed to capture the tiny Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) that also appeared. Maybe next time!
CommonNames: ant; aphids; Greenbark Ceanothus; Scale Insects; California Velvety Tree Ant; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Coccoidea; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Tachinidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Coccoidea; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: e309d_e309-010.jpg
Notes: 13046492875 Here are new flowers and leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family along San Jose Creek a mile from home. The red bits of the flowers will become the wings of the seeds later on. It's our only native maple, though we also have the related native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) growing along the river downstream from Buellton. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2014)
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum

File.Name: e309e_e309-014.jpg
Notes:
13046521694 This is a new sprout of native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. The large basal ovate leaves wither away before the flowers appear in summer. At least IF the flowers appear. We also have plants on the hillside beyond our garden, and they haven't made flowers for the past two dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2014)

It's curious that we also have what I think is the same kind of orchid in the Oregon woods, there called the Denseflower Rein Orchid. See this photo from last summer.

File.Name: e309f_e309-020.jpg
Notes:
13045988283 This is a new sprout of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family, next to an old flower stem. It's now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family - those darned botanists! This plant is no doubt var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2014)

I have this photo of the same plant just starting to flower from last year - which was also dry, but not as dry as this drought year. The difference in the Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) is remarkable. I hope it manages to flower again.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Hummingbird Sage
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e310a_e310-067.jpg
Notes:
13072830544 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's a handsome butterfly on handsome flowers, but the whitish pinnate foliage is equally remarkable - see this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)

It was another spring-like day, though breezy and it did get cloudy.

I did watch most of the new Cosmos TV show last night, after I finished my photo work. I found the constant commercials to be a real drawback, also the CGI stuff. (I mostly watch TCM and PBS with no commercials, and the original series was commercial-free.) The animations by Seth MacFarlane were very good (especially that moment of souring before Giordano Bruno was put to death for his beliefs), and the closing tribute to Carl Sagen was quite touching and had a good message. I'm not much interested in watching the rest of the series until I can do so without the damn commercials.
Locality: especially that moment of souring before Giordano Bruno was put to death for his beliefs)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Santa Barbara Milkvetch; Cosmos
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e310b_e310-054.jpg
Notes: 13072306423 This isn't the best photo, but look at the bottom to see a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly. I've seen a few on the wing, but this is the first one I've managed to capture this year. It's working new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I saw several of them down by the river today. See my [Next] photo for a better shot of the flowers with a different butterfly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)
ScientificName: Erynnis propertius; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Erynnis propertius; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e310c_e310-041.jpg
Notes: 13072315444 These are the first new flowers of pretty Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. At least they are the first new flowers I seen this year. It's growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)

File.Name: e310d_e310-027.jpg
Notes: 13071818283 These are new flowers on a small native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing on bare soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May - with its shadow. They are the first flowers I've seen this year, but I've been away and I saw several of them today. These are another annual that have been scarce in this drought year, so I was happy to see this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)

File.Name: e310e_e310-014.jpg
Notes:
13071683884 This is a humble plant of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family growing on bare soil that was burned by the White Fire last May - with it's shadow. Note that they have two different kinds of leaves - the familiar round "perfoliate" leaves that cradle the flowers, and also the narrow non-flowering leaves. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)

These native plants are kind of weedy, but this one is growing where it should be. Native annuals have been scarce in this drought year, so I was happy to see this one.

File.Name: e310f_e310-032.jpg
Notes:
13071075885 These are new staminate flowers of our other Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. They are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat the spiny fruit. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)

The flowers are smaller and flatter and just a bit more yellowish than the very similar Marah macrocarpus, compare this photo. Marah macrocarpus grows as a fire-follower on the south-facing slopes of our mountains, while Marah fabaceus grows more in the back mountains, sometimes but not always as a fire-follower. At home on San Marcos Pass, we get them both.
ScientificName: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus

File.Name: e311a_e311-008.jpg
Notes:
13094875535 This is another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2014)

I walked our canyon, and then drove up East Camino Cielo road today looking for photos. I found a few! It started sunny, but then the clouds moved in. It feels like spring, but wildflowers are still scarce in this drought year. They're out there, but you have to hunt for them.
CommonNames: Brown Elfin; Coast Live Oak; Hairstreak
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: e311b_e311-015.jpg
Notes: 13094731295 Here are the first new grass-like flowers of native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. You can see the yellow male staminate flowers on top, and if you look close you can see the pale female pistillate flowers lower on the stem. I have many photos of this plant, most recently here. I wasn't expecting it to flower in the drought year, but here it is. It's a remarkably tough plant! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2014)

File.Name: e311c_e311-013.jpg
Notes: 13094520955 These are the first new flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon. Like all lupines, they have hand-shaped "palmate" leaves - like holding your hand open with fingers extended. We have many different species. This one is perennial, with semi-whorled flowers. This plant looks a little "thin" in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2014)

File.Name: e311d_e311-030.jpg
Notes:
13094639674 This is looking close at a new flower head of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Look close - the flowers are full of beetles! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 March 2014)

This photo is like one of those "find the animals" gags in the comic books when I was a kid. (I count 6.) It's a good naturalist trick to hold your hand out and knock the flowers with your walking stick. You will often get a handful of beetles and other insects!

File.Name: e311e_e311-033.jpg
Notes:
13094099733 These are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family just starting to open from their brown-paper husks. (But see my [Next] photo!) The shrubs have opposite toothed leaves and "corky stipules" - those dark cones at the base of the leaves. The leaves are whitish-tomentose underneath. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 March 2014)

We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in a wetter year - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. All the flowers look the same, but these might be the most interesting because of the stipules and hairy bracts.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: e311f_e310-058.jpg
Notes: 13093842955 Here's one more photo of the beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, from yesterday. See my [Previous] photo. One photo is not enough! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2014)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e312a_e312-002.jpg
Notes:
13115890873 Here are wonderful new flowers on Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The plants have got a second life after the rains two weeks ago, and there are lots of new flowers - at least on this one shrub. There was a hummingbird working the flowers, but he kept to the other side of the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2014)

It was breezy this morning and the NWS forecast was for the winds to grow. I drove up into the higher mountains in the hope that the winds would stick to the passes. It was breezy, but the major winds didn't materialize. I did get my photos for the day.

File.Name: e312b_e312-010.jpg
Notes: 13115925374 These are bristly new berries of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are edible when ripe, if you can get past the bristles. They are doing surprisingly well in this drought year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2014)

File.Name: e312c_e312-016.jpg
Notes: 13115205845 I believe these are new flowers of the White-flowering Current (Ribes indecorum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's growing on a north-facing slope where the sun rarely shines, though I waited and got just a bit of sunlight filtered trough the trees. This shrub looks similar to the more common Chaparral Currant (R. malvaceum) - but it seems more compact, it grows higher in the mountains on mostly north-facing slopes, and it has almost white flowers which open later in the season. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2014)

File.Name: e312d_e312-014.jpg
Notes:
13115385304 Here are new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. They look like something that lives in the ocean! You can see a few beetles on the flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2014)

The old Spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry!

File.Name: e312e_e312-007.jpg
Notes: 13114948185 These are lovely new flowers and leaves on native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. It's curious that it also has old seeds still attached. Maybe there hasn't been enough rain in this drought year to knock them fluttering away? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2014)

File.Name: e312f_e312-006.jpg
Notes:
13114531215 I believe this is the gall of a Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's quite pretty with those white hairs and red colors, but they lose the colors as they mature. I saw quite a few of these today, but only on some shrubs. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2014)

It makes sense that this is a stem gall. The scrub oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once later in the spring, then get new leaves at about the same time. A leaf gall wouldn't have a chance to mature, and I guess the wasps know the schedule.
CommonNames: Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e313a_e313-013.jpg
Notes:
13138068453 This is a small Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera trying to hide from me behind a "spring" leaf of Wild Cucumber (genus Marah) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. The plant doesn't have flowers yet, but I suspect it's Marah fabaceus. This is a tight crop. I tried to get closer and it hid away. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)

It's interesting that for the first time since I restarted my Flickr account about two years ago, my Flora set has finally outpaced my All Insects set. At least it will when I add today's photos to the piles. It's been that kind of year. In this drought year, flowers are scarce and insects are even scarcer.

I had to go the the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped by Lake Cachuma on the way. It was a beautiful day, with temps in the 70s and not too breezy. The forecast for the weekend is to be even warmer, possible record setting. Just what we don't need.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Marah; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Marah; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus

File.Name: e313b_e313-018.jpg
Notes:
13137698365 This is a blue (oh-so-blue) flower of native Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae plant family. (Those darned botanists!) Annuals like this one have been scarce in this drought year, but we might have a real spring after all, thanks to our recent rains! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)

Did you spot that little beetle digging into the flower?

File.Name: e313c_e313-006.jpg
Notes: 13137461634 On the left are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) with very hairy stems. On the right is a variant sometimes called Hoffman's Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) that has almost glabrous stems. Both are in the in the Solanaceae plant family. On San Marcos Pass, we get some plants that are intermediate in hairiness. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2014 (left) and Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)
ScientificName: Solanum xanti; xanti; Solanum xanti; hoffmannii; Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Solanum xanti; xanti; Solanum xanti; hoffmannii; Solanaceae

File.Name: e313d_e313-015.jpg
Notes:
13136991313 On the left is a new sprout of native Fremont's Star Lily (Zigadenus fremontii), and on the right is a sprout of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum). Both used to be placed in the Liliaceae plant family, but now the plant on the left is moved to the Melanthiaceae plant family, and the plant on the right is considered a Agavaceae. Those darned botanists!

They look quite distinct side by side like this, but it's easy to confuse them in the woods when they grow together like here. The one difference I count on is that the Amole has wavy leaves and the Star Lily's are quite straight. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Soap Plant; Amole
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Melanthiaceae; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Melanthiaceae; Agavaceae

File.Name: e313e_e313-024.jpg
Notes:
13136732354 This is definitely a native Sanicle in the Apiaceaeplant family - the first I've seen in flower this year. I suspect it's a Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata) with those small flower heads, but cp. Turkey Pea (Sanicula tuberosa). The parsley-like leaves smell just like cilantro! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)

If I'm right, the common name sounds scary, but the leaves do taste like cilantro, and I've been munching them for years. The parsley-like foliage is distinctive. We have many different kinds of sanicles in our mountains.

File.Name: e313f_e313-029.jpg
Notes:
13136379493 These are the first - and so far the only - white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've seen in this drought year. It should be common in the Santa Ynez Valley with zillions of flowers by now, but I was happy to find this one cluster. They have (mostly) opposite leaves and (mostly) yellow floral disks. Most of the plants look terrible this year, but maybe the rains will bring them out late. It's interesting that these are a dominate shrub on the north-facing slopes of the mountains, but they are replaced by Ceanothus megacarpus on the south-facing slopes with alternate leaves. They look almost identical. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2014)

You can make soap from the flowers - a handful of flowers will make suds when you rub them in water! It's a fun trick when you're taking kids in the woods.

File.Name: e314a_e314-009.jpg
Notes:
13158367345 This is an interesting ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) licking a new leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure the ant is in the genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. They have a pair of thoracic spines and an interesting articulated abdomen.

The tree buds are interesting too. It seems odd to think of bitter oaks making sweet honey, but oaks do it all the time with their galls, see this photo and this one. I've found these ants on the leaf buds before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

These ants are tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The ant is about 600 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x600/4800 = 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch.

It was breezy today, and it ended up rather cool. Despite that, the forecast is still for quite warm tomorrow.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: e314b_e314-007.jpg
Notes: 13158193955 One neat feature of these Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushrooms is that they very quickly turn blue when bruised. You can write messages on the pores for someone else to find when you replant the mushroom! I found this one in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees, see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

File.Name: e314c_e314-005.jpg
Notes:
13157704473 I believe this is the Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It is a typical "bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills. The pores and flesh quickly stain blue when bruised, see my [Next] photo. It's said to be edible, but not choice - "mushy and insipid". I haven't tried it. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

This is actually the first mycorhizal mushroom that I found in this drought year, meaning that it grows as a partner on oak roots. I count it as a "perennial". All the other mushrooms that I've found this year have been "annual" decomposers - Agaricus, Coprinus, Marasmius, Collybia, and Psathyrella, plus a few oddballs. I'll always remember this as the year without mushrooms.
ScientificName: Boletus chrysenteron; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; mycorhizal
associatedTaxa: Boletus chrysenteron; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; mycorhizal

File.Name: e314d_e314-021.jpg
Notes:
13157504323 I found this brown-spored Agaricus or Agrocybe mushroom in the drying-out woods today, the first one I've found in this drought year. I won't try to guess what kind it is since it's hardly in pristine condition, but it's good to know they're still out there! Note the little Fly (order Diptera) on the rim of the cap in the upper-right! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

There have been almost NO mushrooms this year. I've been worried about them. It was good to find this one.

File.Name: e314e_e314-026.jpg
Notes: 13157437504 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year on native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are growing down a steep hillside along East Camino Cielo road, and I couldn't get closer. The unusual pure white flowers are lovely. Usually I find the first flowers along West Camino Cielo, but those plants are just starting to leafout. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

File.Name: e314f_e314-017.jpg
Notes: 13156943415 I have to guess that these are new male staminate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family in the canyon at home. But they sure look different from this tree 20 feet away. The catkins are longer and stand out from the stems, more like the Red Willow (Salix laevigata). Could it be? (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2014)

File.Name: e315a_e315-015.jpg
Notes:
13180181215 I believe this a Long-horned Bee in the tribe Eucerini of the Anthophila aka Apoidae in order Hymenoptera, for obvious reasons. It's working a new flower cluster of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. What a wonderful bee! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)

I hiked the Aliso Canyon trail today to see how the wildflowers are doing - in this drought year, but also after a good rain. There are still large bare patches, but the flowers are starting to appear. It will be better in a week or two. It was a perfect day for a walk - quite warm, near 80∞ at home and up to 86∞ at the river, where I was. This was the first day this year I've worn shorts! It's forecast to be even warmer tomorrow.
CommonNames: Long-horned Bee; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Eucerini; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Eucerini; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: e315b_e315-053.jpg
Notes: 13179797565 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family - those darned botanists! This plant sure looks like var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals - though it's growing by the river instead of the mountains. Note the little Fly (order Diptera) - maybe a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) - in the lower-right. This is a fire-follower flower that seems to appear magically after wildfires. It's one of my very favorite native wildflowers. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Fly; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus; Diptera; Tachinidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus; Diptera; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: e315c_e315-047.jpg
Notes:
13179704383 This is a new flowerhead of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia - aka Amsinkia intermedia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The flowers are pretty enough, but they are kind of weedy. But look at that spiral in the inflorescence! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)

The most immaculately hip aristocrat Lord Buckley had a line "Dig Infinity!" Well, can you dig it?
ScientificName: Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Amsinkia intermedia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Amsinkia intermedia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e315d_e315-025.jpg
Notes:
13179109695 This is an oddity. It sure looks like an "oak gall" made by a Cynipid Wasp in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but this one is growing on a fire-dead stem of California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family. Note the thorns. It has many "exit holes" which shows that it is polythalamous, containing many larvae. I've never seen this before. Now I have to look for fresh galls! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)

I've seen something similar before. See this photo on an "oak gall" growing on a native Redberry (Rhamnus crocea) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's interesting that these misplaced galls succeed!
CommonNames: Cynipid Wasp; California Wild Rose; Redberry
ScientificName: Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; polythalamous; Rhamnus crocea; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; polythalamous; Rhamnus crocea; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e315e_e315-022.jpg
Notes: 13179110374 I'm pretty sure this is Turkey Pea or Tuberous Sanicle (Sanicula tuberosa) in the Apiaceae plant family. It has small clusters of yellow flowers, finely dissected leaves, and a carrot-like tuberous root - see my [Previous] photo. It has a strong smell that is a bit like cilantro, but musty and not really pleasant. The Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata) smells better, see this photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)
CommonNames: Turkey Pea; Tuberous Sanicle; Poison Sanicle

File.Name: e315f_e315-020.jpg
Notes:
13178524523 Mea culpa. I've been confused about native Sanicles in the Apiaceaeplant family, so I dug one up today. I replanted it after I got this photo. I hope it makes it, but there are quite a few more. See my [Next] photo for a plant in flower.

I believe this is Turkey Pea or Tuberous Sanicle (Sanicula tuberosa) in the Apiaceae plant family. It has finely dissected leaves and a carrot-like tuberous root. It has a strong smell that is a bit like cilantro, but musty and not really pleasant. The Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata) smells better, see this photo. (I guess now I have to dig up one of those too.) The two plants look similar, and I'm not sure how to tell them apart without digging them up. The key difference is whether or not the seeds have hooked bristles, which doesn't help when the plants are still in flower. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)
CommonNames: Sanicles; Turkey Pea; Tuberous Sanicle; Poison Sanicle
ScientificName: Apiaceae; Sanicula tuberosa; Apiaceae; Sanicula bipinnata
associatedTaxa: Apiaceae; Sanicula tuberosa; Apiaceae; Sanicula bipinnata

File.Name: e316a_e316-011.jpg
Notes:
13206555235 A Horny Toad in the hand is worth...

This is a wonderful little Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum) in my hand. I held my left hand out with the lizard, and got the photo with my 105 mm macro lens. It looks grumpy, but who can blame it? What an amazing spiny appearance, like a little dinosaur - but they feel quite soft in your hand. I know, I shouldn't handle these lizards, but I was very careful holding it, and it's irresistible! I found this remarkable critter on the ridge above Paradise Creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

It was cooler and breezy this morning, but the wind died down and it heated up to 80∞ on the upstairs porch in the afternoon, up to 89∞ at Paradise. It feels like summer and it's not even officially spring yet!

File.Name: e316b_e316-014.jpg
Notes: 13206216305 This is a wonderful little Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum) hiding on open ground behind a stick and a mustard plant. I found this remarkable critter on the ridge above Paradise Creek today, where I often find them at this time of year. I wonder if it deliberately sat with its eye behind the stick, like "If I can't see you, then you can't see me!" See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

File.Name: e316c_e316-008.jpg
Notes: 13206041433 These are the first new flowers I've found this year on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's good to see them - the plants have been looking really bare in this drought year. I was really looking for green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterflies which favor this plant. I saw a few today, but I couldn't get a photo. I'll keep trying! The plants are important for other butterflies too, especially our Blues. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Callophrys dumetorum
associatedTaxa: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Callophrys dumetorum

File.Name: e316d_e316-043.jpg
Notes: 13205352475 These are the first open flowers I've found this year of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. On the left are male staminate flowers, and on the right is a female pistillate flower. The shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that look quite different. The male plant on the left is the only one I've found to open flowers in this drought year. (Compare this photo.) It was growing high up a hill so this was as close as I could get. The plants don't have fancy flowers, but they are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

File.Name: e316e_e316-020.jpg
Notes: 13205020655 These are the first new flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains. The flowers always look a little ratty, even when they are new. Note the little wild bee flying in from the right. If you look closer, you can make out a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with another bee for prey, just below the middle flower cluster. (I'll add a note.) Darn, I didn't look close enough when I got the photo! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

File.Name: e316f_e316-026.jpg
Notes: 13204849065 These are pretty new leaves and many flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The flowers are just starting to open, but they're not quite there yet. It looks positively dripping with skin-irritating oil. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

File.Name: e317a_e316-013.jpg
Notes:
13231976064 Here is one last photo of the well-camouflaged Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii or P. coronatum) that I found yesterday. This is how you usually find them, if you can! This one is burrowing into a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) plant in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2014)

It's been too windy for my kind of photos today, though I got a couple in the morning before the wind got worse. We've had 20-40 mph winds today. It may be a proper "Sundowner" wind event, since it was in the 80s today in Goleta, but only in the 60s here at home in the mountains. I can deal with fog and rain, but not wind!
CommonNames: Coast Horned Lizard; Horny Toad; Deerweed
ScientificName: Phrynosoma blainvillii; P. coronatum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Phrynosoma blainvillii; P. coronatum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: e317b_e317-010.jpg
Notes:
13230707505 This is a "leaf roller gall" (my name) on a new spring leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I have no doubt that it's a real gall as it goes against the natural downward curve of the leaf and it's made of "galled tissue". I don't know what critter's gall it is. You can see the "stellate hairs" on this new oak leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2014)

I find the same galls on the leaves in the fall - see this photo. But the gall in this photo is on a brand new leaf that has just opened from it's bud.
CommonNames: leaf roller gall; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: e317c_e317-006.jpg
Notes:
13230425015 This is an odd gall on a new spring leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose gall it is, but maybe I have another photo of it here. (That's IF it's really the same thing!) By the process of elimination, maybe it's the gall of a Ball Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perfoveata, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera)? Coast Live Oaks are not big gall producers compared to Scrub and Valley Oaks, so any galls on them are interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2014)

Many gall wasps have complicated life cycles - male and female get together and make eggs which produce a unisexual generation that lay eggs to produce a sexual generation of males and females and... the cycle goes on. That means that these wasps make two different kinds of galls. The summer galls are usually more interesting and distinctive, but I can't think which one this gall will become.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Ball Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis perfoveata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis perfoveata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e317d_e315-010.jpg
Notes: 13230152183 This is lovely new foliage of a deciduous native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family, with the sun behind. The leaves are odd-pinnate into 3-7 segments. This plant is growing in Aliso Canyon after the White Fire burned through last May. I've seen a few stump-sprouts, but this plant along the creek escaped being burned. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)

File.Name: e317e_e315-019.jpg
Notes: 13229730685 Here are a pair of lovely little flowers of Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family. They look like garden violets, but these are natives. I don't think of them as fire-followers, but here they are on soil burned by the White Fire last May. They are the first flowers I've seen this year. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 March 2014)

File.Name: e317f_e315-019.jpg
Notes: 13229664413 This is the whole shrub of the staminate native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family that I showed yesterday. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that look quite different. This male plant is the only one I've found to open flowers in this drought year. It's a handsome shrub in flower, here surrounded by yuccas and a dead-looking Deerweed. It's growing on a steep hillside along East Camino Cielo road. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 March 2014)

File.Name: e318a_e318-030.jpg
Notes:
13255845313 Here is a beautiful new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I had to help open the flower by spreading its petals apart to get this photo. I found the first flowers for the year last week along East Camino Cielo road, but I couldn't get close. This shrub is along West Camino Cielo. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They are pretty common on San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)

I watched the second episode of Cosmos last night - despite the commercials - and it was very good! The show was mostly about evolution, and host Neil deGrasse Tyson didn't pull any punches - of course evolution is fact. If you missed the episode, you can watch it at without commercials at www.cosmosontv.com. It's worth watching.

File.Name: e318b_e318-031.jpg
Notes: 13255283425 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Southern Tauschia(Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. It has beautiful foliage, and later in the summer it will develop tasty caraway-like seeds. You have to look for it, but it is pretty common in the woods. Once you notice it, you'll find more. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)

File.Name: e318c_e318-016.jpg
Notes: 13255534074 Here are more wonderful new flowers on a Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The plants got a second wind after the rains a couple of weeks ago, and there are lots of new flowers. There was a hummingbird working the flowers, as they should be. The flowers are very glandular. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)

File.Name: e318d_e318-024.jpg
Notes:
13254858825 These are the first new flowers I've found this year on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (I won't count that oddball flower that I found back in January.) These are the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins. The female pistillate flowers look like tiny acorns and appear later in the spring. These trees support much life in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)

I did find galls on the catkins today - see this photo. But it's not the best photo because of the wind this morning. I'll try again.

File.Name: e318e_e318-008.jpg
Notes: 13254746893 These are the first new flowers I've found this year on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These are the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins. It's a humble shrub, but it supports much live with galls and acorns. I'm sure it's one of the most important shrubs in our mountains for the life it provides! This plant is "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once about now and get new leaves at the same time. You can see both old and new leaves in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)

File.Name: e318f_e318-012.jpg
Notes: 13254371935 This is a brand new "oak apple gall" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's not much larger than a fresh pea, but it will become much larger as it grows. I really don't understand the timing of these galls, as I seem to find new ones throughout the spring and summer months. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e319a_e319-005.jpg
Notes:
13278186453 Look at the end of the upper catkin. (There's more on the right, but not in focus.) These are remarkable tiny galls on new catkins of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure, but I think they might be the spring "sexual generation" of the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. If I'm right, the summer "asexual generation" galls look quite different. I don't seem to have a photo, but see this photo at BugGuide. I'll have to look for them this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2014)

These galls are all the more remarkable when you consider how small an oak catkin is. If you know anything about these galls, please let me know.

I had an errand in town in the middle of the day, but I did get back to the flowering oaks in the morning and got a few photos when the light was right. Today's photos are nothing but galls. The vernal equinox happens tomorrow morning - Thursday, 20 March 2014, at 9:57 AM PDT. Happy equinox! It's officially the first day of spring, and it's also that time of year when the path of the sun changes the most every day. We're finally getting some sunlight in our north-facing garden!

File.Name: e319c_e319-026.jpg
Notes: 13277506503 These are more "leaf roller galls" (my name) on a new spring leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I have no doubt that it's a real gall as it goes against the natural downward curve of the leaf and it's made of "galled tissue". I don't know what critter's gall it is. The pattern of red galled tissue on the leaf is quite beautiful, like the work of a cellular automaton. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2014)
CommonNames: leaf roller galls; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: e319d_e319-008.jpg
Notes: 13277267243 I believe this is the gall of Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). It's growing on a branch of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Apparently the gall larvae form capsules that somehow rise to the surface and fall to the ground to mature, leaving these open scars behind. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2014)
CommonNames: Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: e319e_e318-013.jpg
Notes: 13277051674 I believe this is the gall of a Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's sprouting growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) look very similar, but my book says the prefer Blue Oak, so I guess this is the one. The galls are small, about 3/8 inch across. The old galls stay on the branches for some time. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)
CommonNames: Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Round Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Besbicus conspicuus

File.Name: e319f_e318-004.jpg
Notes:
13276518493 Galls (almost) on galls...

This is a photo of a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family that is just leafing out, with the sun behind. Starting from the bottom, you can see an old acorn and a bud. Then a couple of fat galls of (I believe) the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. On the branch tips beyond those are new galls of (I believe) the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the same wasp family. (Galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) look very similar.) There are lots of galls in the oaks right now! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2014)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Round Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Besbicus conspicuus
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Besbicus conspicuus

File.Name: e320a_e320-057.jpg
Notes:
13298597663 This is a fantastic White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth working flowers of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae). I stopped by Paradise Store for a brew after my walk today, and I noticed this critter working the flowers in front. I bumped up my ISO to 400, and it wasn't enough! I have another recent photo of this pretty moth at rest in our woods. (Paradise Store, Santa Ynez Valley, 20 March 2014)

It was cooler and cloudier today, but still warm enough for shorts. (Once I switch to shorts, I'm loath to go back to long pants!) I got lots of photos today in case tomorrow is drizzly as forecast. I hope so.

File.Name: e320b_e320-011.jpg
Notes:
13298317553 What a difference a couple of hours makes!

On the left is a new plant of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. The flowers normally open in the afternoon (around 4:00 pm?) and close in the morning, but these flowers were open later thanks to a cloudy morning. That's the same plant on the right a couple of hours later, after the sun came out and went away again. Sure the white balance is different, but look at how the pretty flowers have changed! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)
